MENTORIA Career Journey

STEP 1. Enroll with MENTORIA and undergo the Psychometric Test and select the best career / profession based on their interests, personality traits and ability and to build a development plan to prepare them for the desired career with the MENTOR.

STEP 2. Train to get the dream job by Learning & Practicing Job Interview Skills.

STEP 3. Understand Self-Learn methodology using Oversimplify.in & AI Tools

STEP 4. Practice Public Speaking & Soft Skills using Exercises in Oversimplify.in

STEP 5. Plan your Career and build your LIFE PLAN with the help of the Mentor.

STEP 6. Apply for jobs through our Ai Platform AARYA – India’s first fully automated job application platform which builds CV, finds hidden jobs and automatically applies & tracks them till success is achieved.

Emotional Preparation for the Interview â€“ Speak to Yourself

  • The worst-case scenario is not getting hired.
  • I have nothing to lose and everything to gain.
  • This is practice, and I will learn from it.
  • This is one of many opportunities, and I will use it to grow.
  • I will be honest and frank.
  • I will be patient.
  • I will stay calm and not get stressed.
  • I will remain positive and hopeful until the end.
  • I will not speak negatively about my present or previous employer.

Researching the Company

Researching the company is one of the most important steps before an interview. It helps you answer confidently and shows genuine interest in the organization.

  • Read the company website carefully.
  • Understand their products, services, and values.
  • Learn about recent achievements and news.
  • Understand the job description clearly.

Interview Process â€“ 4 Stages

  1. Application Review: Employers review resumes and cover letters to shortlist candidates.
  2. Initial Screening: A phone or video interview to assess basic qualifications.
  3. In-Person Interview: Meeting with managers or a panel to discuss experience and skills.
  4. Follow-Up: Feedback, further rounds, or a job offer.

Qualities Interviewers Look For

  • Focused, enthusiastic, confident, and to the point
  • Passionate and ambitious
  • A team player
  • Energetic and mature
  • Emotionally stable and adaptable to company culture

Important Points:

  • First impressions matter.
  • The most qualified person may not always get hired â€” others also have a chance.
  • Read the job description carefully.
  • Maintain eye contact and speak confidently.
  • Be honest and highlight strengths with examples (leadership, motivation, flexibility, decision-making, problem-solving, loyalty, integrity, creativity).

IMPORTANT

  • Describe your personality honestly and explain why the job excites you.
  • Do not criticize your previous company.
  • Do not disguise a weakness as a strength (e.g., â€œI am a perfectionistâ€).
  • Mention a real weakness and explain how you are improving it.

HOW TO DRESS

  • Males: Formal suit (coat and tie).
  • Females: Formal suit or saree with sober makeup.
  • Use light deodorant or perfume.
  • Carry: Folder, pen, highlighter, certificates, CV, visiting card.
  • Keep your mobile phone switched off.
  • Reach 15 minutes early.

HOW TO HOLD THE INTERVIEWERâ€™S ATTENTION

Attention Levels:

  • 0â€“10 seconds: 100% attention
  • 10â€“60 seconds: Drops to 50%
  • 60â€“90 seconds: Drops to 10% (if no interaction)

Keep answers clear and structured.Ask relevant questions to maintain two-way communication.Avoid long, boring responses.

Other Important Questions to Prepare

  • Tell me about yourself.
  • Why do you want to do an MBA?
  • Where do you see yourself in 10 years?
  • What are your ambitions?
  • Who is your role model and why?
  • What field do you want to choose and why?
  • What are the qualities of a good leader?
  • What if you are not selected?
  • Give an example of a successful project.
  • How do you handle underperforming employees?
  • Where do you see the industry going?
  • What motivates you more â€” pay, growth, recognition, or environment?
  • Do you want to ask us something?

Examples of Questions You May Ask

  • What position are you considering me for?
  • What are the main challenges in this role?
  • What qualities make someone successful in your company?

SAMPLE ANSWERS (Improved & Structured)

1. Tell Me About Yourself

â€œI am currently a Senior Executive in Accounts with experience in taxation and auditing. I have conducted internal audits for ISO 9000 and resolved tax issues for the past two years. I have worked in the construction and media industries. My background includes roles as Junior Accountant, Senior Accountant, and Senior Auditor. I hold a CA qualification and an M.Com degree. My colleagues describe me as results-focused and detail-oriented. I have received the â€˜Employee of the Yearâ€™ and â€˜Best Suggestion Award.â€™â€

2. Why Do You Want to Leave Your Previous Organization?

â€œMy company merged with another firm, and the new management decided to bring in their own team. Before the merger, I consistently received positive performance reviews. I am happy to provide references to verify my performance.â€

3. Why Do You Want to Do an MBA?

â€œI come from an army background, and I want to gain knowledge in marketing and finance to start my own business in the future. I admire entrepreneurs like Kiran Majumdar Shaw, who built a successful biotech company. I want to learn how to manage and grow a business effectively.â€

4. Where Do You See Yourself in 10 Years?

â€œI see myself gaining experience in marketing and finance, learning how businesses operate, and eventually becoming a successful entrepreneur leading a strong team.â€

5. What Are the Qualities of a Good Leader?

â€œA good leader is a good listener, patient, and not self-centered. They motivate others, understand human behavior, and balance compassion with discipline. Strong professional knowledge and integrity are essential.â€

6. What If You Do Not Get Selected?

â€œIf I am not selected, I will treat it as a learning experience. I will continue improving my skills and apply again with better preparation.â€

Group Discussion

Again, Watch These Short Videos Before You Start

Group Discussion Strategy

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BguYUJ7cWrs

Group Discussion Tactics (Funny Video in Hindi)

https://youtu.be/QqGOUFVfoWI?si=Anh8k2omL4vnWiFX

Personality Traits Gauged in a Group Discussion

  • Ability to interact in a team
  • Communication skills
  • Reasoning ability
  • Leadership skills
  • Initiative & enthusiasm
  • Assertiveness
  • Flexibility
  • Nurturing & coaching ability
  • Creativity
  • Ability to think on oneâ€™s feet

HOW TO DO WELL IN A GROUP DISCUSSION

  • Try to grab the opportunity to speak first and introduce the topic.Example Opening (5 Sentences):â€œGood morning friends. The topic given to us is ______. This has been the center of discussion in many forums and in the media. This topic is very important in our lives, and I am glad we are discussing it today. In my view, I believe ______. Now let us hear the views of others.â€
  • Listen carefully and look for the right opportunity to speak (do not interrupt too often).
  • Agree with others and add examples or experiences.
  • Disagree politely and support your view with logic.
  • Present both sides of the issue.
  • Intervene if the discussion becomes aggressive.
  • Cooperate and show leadership.
  • Do not corner or make fun of participants.
  • Encourage quiet members to speak.
  • Use examples and real experiences.
  • If you did not start, try to conclude the discussion.

Starting a GD â€“ Introducing the Topic

â€œFriends, the topic given to us is ______. This is one of the most discussed topics.â€

OR

â€œThis is a controversial topic and has been widely debated in the media and public forums. As every issue has two sides, this topic will also have different viewpoints. Our aim should be to reach a balanced view. The way I understand this topic is ______. Some may say ______, while others may say ______. Let us proceed.â€

Intervening When the Discussion Goes Out of Control

â€œFriends, I think we need to control this discussion. Otherwise, everyone will speak, and no one will listen. Let us speak one by one. Let us also hear from those who have not yet spoken. I am fine speaking at the end.â€

Concluding the Discussion

â€œFor the past 10â€“15 minutes, we have had a very interesting discussion. We have heard diverse views. The overall consensus appears to be ______. However, we must consider the points raised by others as well. Therefore, we can conclude that:

  1. ______â€

Self-Learning UsingChatGPT â€“ Your Knowledge Assistant

ChatGPT is one of the easiest tools to use. The key is knowing how to ask the right question (Prompt Engineering).

It can:

  • Write emails
  • Summarize long essays
  • Create presentations
  • Teach subjects
  • Generate sales leads
  • Write software code
  • And much more

It is a must-learn tool for students and employees.

AI is now integrated into search engines like Google, Bing, and Microsoft applications such as Excel, Word, and Outlook.

Easy Way to Start:Download Bing on your mobile phone and start asking questions by voice.

Optimizing Questions (Prompt Engineering)

  • Assign a role: â€œYou are an expert web developer.â€
  • Give a clear task: â€œDevelop an e-commerce website to export handicrafts.â€
  • Set format: â€œUse simple language and bullet points.â€
  • Limit length: â€œRespond within 500 words.â€

Types of Prompts

  • Chain-of-thought prompts
  • Role-play prompts
  • Conversational prompts
  • Prompt priming (provide context)
  • Avoid bias and use neutral language

Examples of Prompts

  1. Create a banner for an online summer sale with a beach background and the text â€œSummer Sale â€“ Up to 50% Off.â€
  2. As a CRM executive, help a customer who cannot log into a website.
  3. Write a Python function that returns the sum of all even numbers in a list.

Free AI Tools to Improve Productivity

  • Bing
  • Grok
  • ChatGPT
  • DeepSeek
  • Gemini
  • Copilot
  • DALL·E

Other Tools:

  • 12ft.io (http://12ft.io/) â€“ Summarize large articles
  • Yoodli.ai â€“ Improve communication skills
  • SignalHire.com â€“ Lead generation
  • GPTSlides.com â€“ Create presentations
  • Formula.dog â€“ Generate Excel formulas and code
  • Writesonic â€“ Content writing
  • Midjourney â€“ Generate art
  • Replit â€“ Coding
  • Synthesia â€“ Video creation
  • Soundraw â€“ Music creation
  • Fliki â€“ TikTok generation
  • Starrytars â€“ Avatars
  • SlidesAI â€“ PPT generation
  • Remini â€“ Edit pictures
  • Pictory â€“ Edit videos
  • Wordtune â€“ Summarize notes

Public Speaking & Soft Skills

Watch These Videos Before Starting the Course

  • How to Overcome Fear of Public Speakinghttps://youtu.be/80UVjkcxGmA?si=Cqo1w3-cVWnjuBa5
  • What Well-Spoken Words Can Dohttps://youtu.be/Iqq1roF4C8s?si=PLhufNvIvye6hIdC
  • Why Public Speaking Is Importanthttps://youtu.be/cmwTl7LXAR8?si=Teg7uXFTaY1YdZ_d
  • How to Speak Fluentlyhttps://youtu.be/VVhuLMELEcY?si=vzc676KQ142G9Prs
  • Behavioral Skills Traininghttps://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PUTbDB6etVs

Confidence-Building Exercises

  1. Call out to a person 200 meters away.
  2. Announce loudly: â€œFactory closed due to heavy rains.â€
  3. Read aloud while your partner writes facing away.
  4. Dictate from 15 feet away.
  5. Dictate to the whole class.
  6. Read an essay to the class.
  7. Deliver a lecture to 1 person.
  8. Deliver a lecture to 10 people.
  9. Give an extempore speech.
  10. Deliver a motivational talk.
  11. Introduce a guest speaker.
  12. Handle a difficult negotiation.
  13. Conduct a group discussion.
  14. Moderate a brainstorming session.
  15. Participate in a panel interview.
  16. Give a farewell speech.
  17. Address your department for the first time.

Master Public Speaking with Videos & Practice

Public speaking is about:

  • Connecting with your audience
  • Communicating clearly
  • Leaving a strong impression

Why It Matters:

  • Career growth
  • Confidence building
  • Influence & persuasion
  • Networking opportunities

Practical Tips:

  • Know your audience
  • Organize your content (Introductionâ€“Bodyâ€“Conclusion)
  • Practice regularly
  • Manage anxiety with deep breathing
  • Use simple visual aids

Final Thoughts

Public speaking and soft skills are lifelong skills.Practice consistently.Use videos for learning and exercises for application.Stay open to feedback and continuous improvement.

With dedication and the right guidance, you can become a confident speaker, strong leader, and effective professional.

ADVICE BEFORE STARTING PUBLIC SPEAKING TRAINING

Watch This Video on the Necessity of Public Speaking

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Cjs7dyzLVco

Public speaking is one of the most important skills to rise in your career.

MENTAL PREPARATION BEFORE YOU START

  • FIRST THING â€“ Stop finding ways to escape.
  • NO HURRY â€“ TAKE TWO WEEKS. Before starting, you must prepare yourself mentally. Take your time.
  • DECIDE ONCE AND FOR ALL. You cannot avoid learning this skill. Avoiding it can cost your entire career.
  • Go into a room alone and speak loudly to yourself. Force your mind to believe: YOU CAN DO IT.
  • You do not need perfect English to be a good speaker.
  • No one is born a great speaker. All great speakers worked hard.
  • If you can survive the first two minutes on stage, you will survive the entire speech.
  • Never get on stage unprepared. Practice, Practice, Practice.

FIRST STEPS BEFORE GOING ON STAGE

STEP 1

Start by telling a bedtime story to a child in your mother tongue. Then tell the same story in English.

STEP 2

Practice in front of friends or family:

  • First in your mother tongue.
  • Then in English.
  • Sit on chairs with your script in hand.
  • Speak casually to one person at a time.
  • Pretend to be confident.

You may panic and forget. If that happens:

  • Take a deep breath.
  • Smile.
  • Ask a question to buy time.
  • Ask someone to summarize what they understood.
  • Look at your script confidently and continue.

STEP 3

Rewrite your script in your own words.Use short sentences and familiar words. Add a story or experience.

STEP 4

Highlight key words in yellow.Write those key words on a small card and pin it to your script.

STEP 5

Memorize the first 20 lines.

STEP 6

Rehearse at least 7 times in front of a mirror.

STEP 7

Reach the venue 30 minutes early.Mentally adjust to the environment.Carry a spare copy of your opening.

BEST WAYS TO OPEN A SPEECH (First 60 Seconds Are Critical)

Your opening must be clear, catchy, and impactful.

You can start with:

  • A question
  • A news incident
  • A startling statement
  • A quote
  • A human-interest story
  • An elevator pitch

Always tell the audience:

  • What you are going to speak about
  • How long you will take

Use facts, analogies, statistics, and opinions (with reasons).

HOW TO START YOUR PRESENTATION

  • Walk confidently to the podium.
  • Place your script on it.
  • Keep your keyword card in your pocket.
  • Wait for the audience to settle.
  • Speak the first 3 sentences.
  • Take a deep breath.

Three Things Will Definitely Happen:

  1. You may forget your script.
  2. Your heart may pound.
  3. You may panic.

What To Do:

  • Take a deep breath.
  • Look at your keyword card confidently.
  • Drink water if needed.
  • Restart calmly.
  • If needed, read from your script.
  • Confidence usually returns within 30 seconds.

DOs & DONâ€™Ts

DOs

  • Maintain eye contact with one person at a time.
  • Use short, simple sentences.
  • Speak clearly and loudly (do not shout).
  • Face the audience while speaking.
  • Use your natural accent.

DONâ€™Ts

  • Do not insult or embarrass anyone.
  • Do not speak too fast.
  • Do not read continuously.
  • Do not apologize unnecessarily.
  • Do not show relief after finishing.
  • Do not use very big words to impress.
  • Avoid filler words, mumbling, and looking down.
  • Do not exceed your time limit.

SURVIVAL KIT WHEN YOUR MIND GOES BLANK

  • Take a deep breath and smile.
  • Look at your notes confidently.
  • Ask the audience a question.
  • Share a small story or experience.

EXERCISES TO IMPROVE PUBLIC SPEAKING

  • Call out loudly to someone 200 meters away.
  • Announce loudly in a factory setting.
  • Give dictation from 15 feet away.
  • Deliver a prepared lecture.
  • Give an extempore speech.
  • Conduct a group discussion.
  • Moderate a brainstorming session.
  • Participate in a panel interview.
  • Practice negotiation skills.
  • Give a farewell speech.
  • Address your department for the first time.

(Practice regularly to build confidence.)

KEY POINTS FOR PRESENTATIONS

  1. Choose not more than 5 key points.
  2. Repeat and emphasize your key ideas.
  3. Transition smoothly between points.
  4. Use facts, analogies, and statistics.
  5. Talk instead of reading.
  6. Move naturally and maintain eye contact.

VISUAL AIDS RULES

  • Use bullet points only (no full sentences).
  • Follow the 6Ã—6 rule (max 6 lines per slide, 6 words per line).
  • Use limited colors (max 3).
  • Ensure good contrast and large fonts.
  • Do not depend completely on PowerPoint.
  • Never write â€œThank Youâ€ on the slide â€” say it instead.

DRESSING UP

  • Dress according to the occasion.
  • When unsure, dress conservatively.
  • Keep hair neat.
  • Avoid excessive jewelry.
  • Maintain proper grooming.

GESTURES

  • Keep gestures natural.
  • Avoid hands in pockets.
  • Avoid exaggerated movements.
  • Do not clasp hands tightly.

HANDLING QUESTIONS

  1. Let the person ask the question fully.
  2. Rephrase the question.
  3. Repeat it for the audience.
  4. Invite opinions from 2â€“3 people.
  5. Consolidate answers and give your view.
  6. Ask if the questioner is satisfied.

Thumb Rule:Answer one person, but speak for the whole audience.

CLOSING THE SPEECH

  • Indicate you are concluding.
  • Summarize key points.
  • Invite questions.
  • End on a positive and friendly note.
  • Leave the stage calmly.

Must-Do Courses / Self-Study

  • Toastmasters Club (Public Speaking)
  • Train the Trainer
  • Microsoft Project
  • Law & Compliance
  • World History
  • Geography
  • Physics
  • Mathematics

Competitive Exams

  • CAT
  • GRE
  • IAS, IFS, IRS, IPS
  • UPSC
  • CDS (Army, Navy, Air Force)
  • Bank Officer Exams

OBJECTIVE TYPE QUESTIONS â€“ Negative Marking Strategy

Type 1:

Correct +1 | Wrong â€“1â†’ Answer only if fully sure.

Type 2:

Correct +1 | Wrong â€“0.5â†’ Answer if you can eliminate 2 options.

Type 3:

Correct +1 | Wrong â€“0.25â†’ Answer if you can eliminate 1 option.

Type 4:

No negative markingâ†’ Attempt all questions.

Assumption Strategy Example

  • Sure about 50 questions.
  • Can eliminate 1 wrong option in 34 questions.
  • Can eliminate 2 wrong options in 16 questions.

Use elimination wisely to maximize marks.

ESSAY TYPE PAPER

Time Management Strategy

Divide the total time by the total marks to calculate the number of minutes you can spend per mark. Then multiply that by the marks allotted to each question. Spend only that calculated time on that particular question.

NOTE

The basic aim is NOT to leave any question unanswered. Even if you are unsure, write something (minimum 10 lines).

Recommended Answer Format

CENTER HEADING

Write the main topic in the center of the page.

GROUP HEADINGS

These are headings like:

  • Introduction
  • Factors to be Considered
  • Effects of Factors
  • Conclusion

Rules:

  • Do not put a full stop after Group Headings.
  • Start writing on the next line.

If there are multiple factors, list them under Para Headings as follows:

3. Cause 1This is described in sentences starting on the same line.

4. Cause 2Writing starts on the next line.

If there are multiple factors under each cause, list them under Sub Para Headings:

c. Sub Para HeadingExplanation begins on the next line.

d. Effect of Cause 2

iii. Sub-Sub Para Headingiv. Result 2 is Severe Poverty

NOTE

All levels of headings (Group Headings, Para Headings, Sub Para Headings, Sub-Sub Para Headings) must be bold and underlined in the exam.

Leadership

The best way to understand the basics of leadership is to study multiple definitions.

Useful Videos

Leadership according to Dr. A.P.J. Abdul Kalamhttps://youtube.com/shorts/mvDVooJ-v44?si=jyN_A6jgPwC16cl-

https://www.instagram.com/reel/DOpIOxYDo1j/?igsh=ZGtvczBzdGVqbjU=

How to Handle Pressurehttps://www.instagram.com/reel/DN9Q8lKjrHo/?igsh=MW5uNm4yNTJjdG1idA==https://www.instagram.com/reel/DMacOt0x09H/?igsh=NzhvY2dsMHByYjhi

Best of Sourav Ganguly on Leadershiphttps://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fTdkJTXAAjw

Definitions of Leadership

Leadership is the process of influencing the thinking, behavior, and efforts of team members toward the achievement of organizational goals.

Leadership is a winning combination of personal traits and the ability to think and act as a leader, directing the activities of others for the good of all.

â€œLeadership is the art of getting someone else to do something you want done because he wants to do it.â€ â€“ Dwight D. Eisenhower

â€œA leader has the vision and conviction that a dream can be achieved. He inspires the power and energy to get it done.â€ â€“ Ralph Lauren

â€œThe best leaders surround themselves with assistants smarter than they are.â€ â€“ Amos Parrish

â€œReason and judgment are the qualities of a leader.â€ â€“ Tacitus

100 Answers to the Question: What Is Leadership?

Leadership is difficult to define because it means different things to different people. Here are 100 definitions:

  1. A leader is best when people barely know he exists. â€“ Lao Tzu
  2. A good leader takes more blame and less credit. â€“ Arnold Glasow
  3. Leadership shows in times of challenge. â€“ Martin Luther King Jr.
  4. You donâ€™t need a title to be a leader. â€“ Mark Sanborn
  5. Lead from behind and take responsibility in danger. â€“ Nelson Mandela
  6. Leadership and learning go together. â€“ John F. Kennedy
  7. A leader gets people to do great things. â€“ Ronald Reagan
  8. Leadership is leading with heart and empathy. â€“ Bill George
  9. Leadership elicits greatness already present. â€“ John Buchan
  10. A great leader attracts great people. â€“ Goethe
  11. Right leadership makes people follow till the end. â€“ Harold Seymour
  12. Leaders must have stamina and self-reliance. â€“ Thomas Cronin
  13. Leadership rallies people to a common purpose. â€“ Bernard Montgomery
  14. Leadership confronts anxiety of people. â€“ Galbraith
  15. Leadership translates vision into reality. â€“ Warren Bennis
  16. Leadership defines the future and inspires action. â€“ John Kotter
  17. Leadership produces more leaders. â€“ Ralph Nader
  18. Leadership is integrity in action. â€“ Scott Berkun
  19. Leadership influences through passion and purpose. â€“ Myles Munroe
  20. Leadership unlocks potential. â€“ Bill Bradley
  21. Leadership is saying no wisely. â€“ Tony Blair
  22. Leadership is defined by results. â€“ Peter Drucker
  23. Leadership is measured by quality of followers. â€“ Dennis Peer
  24. Innovation distinguishes leader from follower. â€“ Steve Jobs
  25. Leadership develops others to full potential. â€“ Bob Mason
  26. Leadership is influencing willingly. â€“ Eisenhower
  27. Leadership requires clear vision. â€“ Theodore Hesburgh
  28. Leadership mobilizes others for shared goals. â€“ Kouzes & Posner
  29. A great leader takes people where they ought to go. â€“ Rosalynn Carter
  30. If you inspire others to grow, you are a leader. â€“ John Quincy Adams
  31. Leadership is a moral relationship based on trust. â€“ Joanne Ciulla
  32. Leadership balances strength and kindness. â€“ Jim Rohn
  33. Leadership raises performance standards. â€“ Peter Drucker
  34. Leadership is service. â€“ J. Donald Walters
  35. Leadership builds confidence through control. â€“ Tom Landry
  36. A leader knows, goes, and shows the way. â€“ John Maxwell
  37. Leadership persuades a group to pursue goals. â€“ John Gardner
  38. Leadership persuades people to do the difficult. â€“ Truman
  39. Leadership translates vision into reality. â€“ Bennis
  40. A leader is a dealer in hope. â€“ Napoleon
  41. Leadership energizes others. â€“ David Caullo
  42. Leadership influences even when tough. â€“ Scott Adams
  43. Leadership guides without force. â€“ Lisa Hanson
  44. Leadership takes people where they havenâ€™t been. â€“ Henry Kissinger
  45. Leadership is service and co-creation. â€“ Greenleaf
  46. Leadership works with others to achieve objectives. â€“ Hersey
  47. Leadership nurtures and enhances. â€“ Tom Peters
  48. Leadership combines strategy and character. â€“ Schwarzkopf
  49. Leadership raises aspirations. â€“ David Gergen
  50. Leadership unlocks potential. â€“ Bradley
  51. Leadership prioritizes effectively. â€“ Stephen Covey
  52. Leadership solves problems. â€“ Colin Powell
  53. Leadership is key to success. â€“ Erskine Bowles
  54. Leadership is about being. â€“ Frances Hesselbein
  55. Leadership creates a climate of excellence. â€“ Mike Vance
  56. Leadership gets voluntary effort. â€“ Fred Smith
  57. Leadership prevents emergencies. â€“ Glasow
  58. Leadership maximizes performance. â€“ W.A. Cohen
  59. Leadership cares for people. â€“ Harald Anderson
  60. Leadership has many definitions. â€“ Ralph Stogdill
  61. Leadership develops people. â€“ Firestone
  62. Share inspiration. â€“ Stevenson
  63. Passion increases influence. â€“ Michele Payn
  64. Leadership is intangible. â€“ Scott Adams
  65. Leadership is doing right unseen. â€“ George Van Valkenburg
  66. Leadership demonstrates possibility. â€“ Mark Yarnell
  67. Leadership is attitude in action. â€“ Harold Geneen
  68. Leadership empowers ingenuity. â€“ George Patton
  69. Leadership by example is real leadership. â€“ Emerson
  70. Leaders, followers, and goals form leadership. â€“ Gary Wills
  71. A leader must know clearly. â€“ Clarence Randall
  72. Leadership takes responsibility. â€“ Mitt Romney
  73. Leadership is difficult but simple. â€“ McKinney
  74. Leadership is human experience. â€“ Lance Secretan
  75. Leadership influences toward common goal. â€“ Northouse
  76. Followers shape leadership brilliance. â€“ Ira Chaleff
  77. A leader knows direction. â€“ Anonymous
  78. Leaders are made, not born. â€“ Vince Lombardi
  79. A leader leaves others able to carry on. â€“ Walter Lippmann
  80. Leadership provides stability. â€“ Friedman
  81. Leadership measured by followersâ€™ success. â€“ Omar Bradley
  82. Leadership is developed. â€“ Elaine Agather
  83. Leadership connects deeply. â€“ Kouzes & Posner
  84. Leadership selects good people. â€“ Nimitz
  85. To lead, first understand others. â€“ Hazlitt
  86. Leadership uses power responsibly. â€“ Zalenik
  87. Leadership prioritizes vital over important. â€“ Sanderson
  88. Leadership develops people through work. â€“ Trinka & Wallace
  89. Leadership sees opportunity in difficulty. â€“ Reed Markham
  90. Leadership influences morale. â€“ Viscount Slim
  91. Great leaders increase momentum. â€“ John Maxwell
  92. Great leaders inspire. â€“ William Arthur Ward
  93. Good followers make good leaders. â€“ Aristotle
  94. Leadership creates change. â€“ Melanne Verveer
  95. Leadership shapes future opinion. â€“ Doris Goodwin
  96. Leadership makes people feel ownership. â€“ David Kelley
  97. Leadership leads by example. â€“ Ken Kesey
  98. Leadership leads through action, not noise. â€“ Chinese Proverb
  99. To command is to serve. â€“ Andre Malraux
  100. Leadership is leading with your whole heart. â€“ Lolly Daskal

NATIONAL BESTSELLER

THE LEADERSHIP GAPWhat Gets Between You and Your GreatnessBy Lolly Daskal

After decades of coaching powerful executives around the world, Lolly Daskal has observed that leaders rise to their positions relying on a specific set of values and traits. But over time, every executive reaches a point when performance declines and failure persists. Very few leaders understand why this happens or how to prevent it.

About the Author

Lolly Daskal is one of the most sought-after executive leadership coaches in the world. Her cross-cultural expertise spans 14 countries, six languages, and hundreds of companies. She is the Founder and CEO of Lead From Within. Her leadership program is designed to help leaders enhance performance and make a meaningful difference in their companies, their lives, and the world.

She has been recognized as:

  • Top-50 Leadership and Management Expert by Inc. magazine
  • The Most Inspiring Woman in the World by Huffington Post

Her writing has appeared in:

  • Harvard Business Review (HBR)
  • Inc.com
  • Fast Company
  • Huffington Post
  • Psychology Today

Her book The Leadership Gap became a national bestseller.

Additional Reading You Might Enjoy

  • 12 Successful Leadership Principles That Never Grow Old
  • A Leadership Manifesto: A Guide to Greatness
  • How to Succeed as a New Leader
  • 12 of the Most Common Lies Leaders Tell Themselves
  • 4 Proven Reasons Why Intuitive Leaders Make Great Leaders
  • The One Quality Every Leader Needs to Succeed
  • The Deception Trap of Leadership

Six Traits of Effective Leadersâ€“ Christian Nevell Bovee

  1. Make others feel important
  2. Promote a vision
  3. Follow the golden rule
  4. Admit mistakes
  5. Criticize others only in private
  6. Stay close to the action

Steps to Become a Leader

  1. Get your employees to want to do their job. Avoid controlling their every move.
  2. Share your vision, enthusiasm, and energy.
  3. Motivate employees with tangible rewards and genuine concern for their wellbeing and progress.
  4. Be accessible and transparent.
  5. Be strong and effective.
  6. Be a role model.
  7. Avoid exploiting your position.
  8. Identify and fully utilize the skills and talents of your staff.
  9. Give credit to others and take responsibility for mistakes.
  10. Take care of yourself.

Shift Your Style: From Traditional Leadership to Collaborative Leadership

  1. Power comes from designation â†’ Power grows through teamwork.
  2. Secretive and protective of information â†’ Transparent sharing of information and knowledge.
  3. Non-participative management â†’ Inviting suggestions and ideas.
  4. Top-down strategy â†’ Bottom-up, democratic brainstorming style.
  5. Focus only on execution and efficiency â†’ Encourage flexibility, innovation, and risk-taking.
  6. Firefighting problems and treating symptoms â†’ Root cause analysis and prevention.
  7. Annual performance review â†’ Continuous feedback and personal coaching.

Written Test â€“ Leadership Position (Objective & Essay Type)

OBJECTIVE TYPE QUESTIONS

Usually consist of 4 options to choose from.

Strategy for Answering Negative Marking Questions

  1. Correct +1 | Wrong â€“1â†’ Answer only if you are completely sure.
  2. Correct +1 | Wrong â€“0.5â†’ Answer if you can eliminate at least 2 options.
  3. Correct +1 | Wrong â€“0.25â†’ Answer if you can eliminate at least 1 option.
  4. No negative markingâ†’ Attempt all questions.

Assumption Strategy Example

Out of 100 questions:

  • You are sure about 50 questions.
  • You can eliminate 1 wrong option in 34 questions.
  • You can eliminate 2 wrong options in 16 questions.

Use elimination wisely to maximize your total score.

Got it. I will format everything properly with main headings bold, clean structure, and full content included. Nothing will be cut.

ESSAY TYPE PAPER

Time Management Strategy

Divide the total time by the total marks to find the number of minutes you can spend per mark. Multiply that by the marks allotted to each question. Spend only that calculated time on that particular question.

NOTE

The basic aim is NOT to leave any question unanswered. Write something for every question (minimum 10 lines).

Recommended Format for Essay Answers

CENTER HEADING

Write the main topic in the center.

GROUP HEADINGS

Examples:

  • Introduction
  • Factors to be Considered
  • Effects of Factors
  • Conclusion

Rules:

  • Do not put a full stop after Group Headings.
  • Writing starts on the next line.

If there are multiple factors:

1. Cause 1This is described in sentences starting on the same line.

2. Cause 2Writing starts on the next line.

If there are multiple factors under each cause:

a. Sub Para Heading

b. Effect of Cause 2

i. Sub Sub Para Headingii. Result 2 is Severe Poverty

NOTE

All levels of headings (Group Heading, Para Heading, Sub Para Heading, Sub-Sub Para Heading) must be bold and underlined in the exam.

EXAM SHORTCUTS

  • Read the syllabus thoroughly.
  • Highlight while reading.
  • Use a speech-to-text app (like Speech Notes) to dictate notes.
  • Solve 2â€“5 practice papers with proper time management.
  • Never leave any question blank (except in objective tests where negative marking is more than half).

STUDYING FOR RETENTION

  • Highlight or underline while reading.
  • Write important points and new words in the margin.
  • Read the preface, executive summary, and about the author before starting a book.
  • First go through the index.
  • After finishing each chapter, dictate main points into a voice dictation app.
  • Apply the knowledge practically within one week through an exercise or project.

SALARY DISCUSSION

It is best to avoid discussing your current salary during the first interview.If asked again, provide the correct information.If asked about salary expectations, you can safely ask for approximately a 30% increase.

CAREER SETTLING DOWN

  • Most important subject (personal and professional).
  • Not taught formally but learned through experience (e.g., Gandhi).
  • Practical, not theoretical.
  • Understand reporting structure and communication norms. These are often unwritten.
  • Read the HR manual and employee handbook thoroughly.
  • Ask HR for detailed briefing.
  • Find a buddy with at least 2 years of experience.
  • Understand internal politics and avoid power struggles.
  • Learn written, email, phone, and body language etiquette.

BOSS HANDLING

  • Donâ€™t get too close too soon.
  • Take notes and complete assignments before deadlines.
  • Discuss problems early.
  • Ask for training when assigned new tasks.
  • Be prepared for meetings.
  • Never speak behind anyoneâ€™s back.
  • Show enthusiasm and willingness to take responsibility.

BUILDING A REPUTATION

  • Project professionalism and dependability.
  • Avoid pleasing everyone or getting too close to seniors.
  • Adhere strictly to timelines.
  • Show honesty and willingness to learn.
  • Be a team player.
  • Avoid gossip and politics.
  • Show energy and readiness for bigger roles.

HANDLING OFFICE POLITICS

  • Donâ€™t form groups.
  • Never corner anyone.
  • Discuss personal problems only with your boss privately.
  • Be honest and avoid manipulation.
  • Understand power structure and groupism.
  • Never speak behind anyoneâ€™s back.

Soft Skill Training

Master the art of successful interactions.

Key skills:

  • Communication (Written & Verbal)
  • Teamwork
  • Adaptability
  • Problem-solving
  • Conflict resolution
  • Strong work ethic
  • Positive attitude
  • Time management
  • Self-confidence
  • Accepting criticism
  • Leadership ability

TYPES OF COMMUNICATION

Only 7% of communication happens through words.93% happens through non-verbal cues:

  • 55% facial expressions
  • 38% vocal tone

VERBAL COMMUNICATION

  1. Conversations
  2. Discussions
  3. Telephonic discussions
  4. Video conferences

NON-VERBAL COMMUNICATION

  • Body language
  • Kinesics, Proxemics, Paralanguage
  • Intention
  • Dress and appearance
  • Signs and symbols

INTERESTING FACTS ABOUT BODY LANGUAGE

  • It sends messages without words.
  • Mostly communicates feelings.
  • People can read it accurately.
  • You cannot not have body language.
  • If words and body conflict, people believe the body.
  • Changing body posture can change feelings.

COMMUNICATION SECRETS

  • Success = Talking so people listen & Listening so people talk
  • Make others feel secure and valued
  • Inspire through clarity and sincerity

ACTIVE LISTENING

  • Listen for key ideas and facts
  • Distinguish evidence from argument
  • Analyze key points
  • Look for unspoken messages
  • Keep open mind
  • Ask clarifying questions
  • Avoid interruptions
  • Take brief meaningful notes
  • Paraphrase what youhear

BODY LANGUAGE OF AN ACTIVE LISTENER

  • Maintains eye contact
  • Open posture
  • Pleasant expression
  • Relaxed but alert
  • Encouraging nods or sounds

PHONE ETIQUETTE WHILE SPEAKING

  • Plan what to say
  • Smile
  • Speak slowly
  • Be polite
  • Monitor time
  • Be clear and concise
  • Seek feedback

SOCIAL SKILLS â€“ PARTY ETIQUETTE

  • Arrive 15 minutes early if picking up guests
  • Be polite to driver
  • Address respectfully
  • Avoid touching unnecessarily
  • Introduce guests properly
  • Avoid gossiping or staring
  • Prepare short speech in advance
  • Follow dining etiquette properly
  • Thank hosts and staff

NEGOTIATIONS

  • Set clear boundaries beforehand
  • Listen first
  • Be ready to walk away
  • Final rate is rarely final
  • Take assurances in writing
  • Never show joy or regret
  • Never give false commitments
  • Negotiate from strength

NETWORKING â€“ GETTING AN INTERVIEW CALL

  • Start with personal contacts
  • Ask friends, mentors for help
  • Make personal connections
  • Track contacts
  • Follow up regularly
  • Be specific about what you want

TELEPHONE ETIQUETTE

  • Answer within 2â€“3 rings
  • Greet appropriately
  • Take accurate notes
  • Avoid negative phrases
  • Thank caller
  • Inform next action clearly

TEACHING SKILLS

  • Simplify concepts
  • Prepare well
  • Motivate students
  • Use real-life examples
  • Be patient
  • Avoid ridicule
  • Use simple language
  • Be impartial

TEAM BUILDING

  • Be fair
  • Select carefully
  • Maintain clear expectations
  • Delegate and coach
  • Give credit publicly
  • Counsel privately
  • Encourage accountability

SALES TRAINING

Watch the Following Videos

https://youtu.be/7EVeze5sP-khttps://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ar_lpoZi-cI&list=PLvoqzZu9FF7dflcpzt-SMF1xLMncTPi79&index=3https://youtu.be/7EVeze5sP-k

Also refer to the Dunn & Bradstreet Seminar presentation and additional sales videos:

â€¢https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XmYA5a_w3yEâ€¢https://youtu.be/Ar_lpoZi-cIâ€¢https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLvoqzZu9FF7dflcpzt-SMF1xLMncTPi79â€¢https://youtu.be/7EVeze5sP-k

TRAINING CARRIED OUT FOR MBA BATCHES

  1. Introduction and importance of communication
  2. Emergency announcements on shop floor (rain/blast situations)
  3. Handling latecomers (genuine and habitual)
  4. Proper stance and arm positioning while speaking
  5. Delivering a motivational lecture
  6. Talking about yourself
  7. Listening skills
  8. Eye contact in public speaking
  9. Gestures, mannerisms, and authenticity
  10. Leadership skills â€“ Leadership is the process of interpersonal influence over team members to achieve organizational goals in a given situation. Managers must work effectively with peers, supervisors, and subordinates. Understanding self and influencing others is essential.
  11. Appropriate voice volume
  12. Formal dressing
  13. Reading and writing in pairs (back-to-back)
  14. Reading aloud to class
  15. Short write-up and presentation
  16. Introduction to group discussion
  17. Preparing a CV
  18. What assessors look for
  19. Opportunities in group discussions
  20. Introducing a topic
  21. How to intervene (â€œbutt inâ€)
  22. Managing conflict
  23. Concluding and summarizing
  24. Interview preparation
  25. Emotional preparation for interviews
  26. Dressing and movement
  27. Researching a target company
  28. Following up on employment calls
  29. Controlling body language
  30. Participating in conferences
  31. Social party behavior / hosting skills
  32. Table etiquette
  33. Interviewing workers
  34. Negotiating with trade unions
  35. Written communication (types of letters)
  36. Light conversation and topics to avoid
  37. Talking with senior management
  38. Telephone etiquette
  39. Official spokesperson / P.R.O role
  40. Avoiding being misquoted
  41. Conflict resolution
  42. Making friends
  43. Maintaining a contact list
  44. Debate
  45. Talent exhibition
  46. Getting an appointment with senior officials
  47. Building and assessing organizational culture
  48. On-the-spot speaking / handling pauses
  49. Presence of mind
  50. Extempore agility

SKILLS YOU MUST PRACTICE

  • Induction speech
  • Speak about your family
  • Speak for 2 minutes in any language
  • Make announcements on shop floor
  • Call out to a worker far away
  • Drill square command
  • Read to the wall while partner writes
  • Write and deliver a speech
  • Convey messages through body language
  • Convey moods through action

  • Interviews

  • Conducting games

  • Condolence speech
  • Appreciation speech

  • Singing before an audience

  • Call center training

Life Planning Timeline Using a MENTOR (Sample)

FOCUS AREAS FOR FUTURE LEARNING

50-WEEK MANAGEMENT DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM

WEEK 1 â€“ THE GROW MODEL FOR COACHING

The single most important technique for executive coaching

The GROW model, developed by Sir John Whitmore, provides a framework for coaching. GROW has four stages: Goals, Reality, Options and Way Forward. Responsibility for setting goals rests with the coachee. The coach works in a non-directive way, supporting and challenging.

GOALS

Focuses on the coacheeâ€™s aims and priorities. Sets the agenda for the coaching conversation. Achieved through questioning and empathy.

Questions include:

  • What is your goal?
  • What are your priorities?
  • What are you trying to achieve?
  • How will you know when you have achieved it?
  • Is the goal specific and measurable?
  • What will success look like?

REALITY

Explore the learnerâ€™s current position and circumstances.

Questions include:

  • Can you control the result?
  • What are the milestones?
  • Who is involved?
  • What have you done so far?
  • What major issues are you encountering?

OPTIONS

Generate strategies and action plans.

Questions include:

  • What options do you have?
  • If you had unlimited resources?
  • What would be the perfect solution?

WAY FORWARD

Agree what needs to be done. Create a practical action plan.

Questions include:

  • What are you going to do and when?
  • Who needs to know?
  • What support do you need?
  • How will you overcome obstacles?

Include review and feedback for progress.

WEEK 2 â€“ DEVELOPING INFLUENCE AND ASSERTIVE LEADERSHIP

Providing support and challenge while strengthening results and relationships

Personality remains stable, but behaviour is flexible and can be developed.

Behaviour dimensions:

  • Warm â€“ supportive, empathetic
  • Cold â€“ detached
  • Dominant â€“ confident, direct
  • Submissive â€“ passive

Behaviour Types

  • Aggressive (Dominant + Cold)
  • Assertive (Dominant + Warm)
  • Avoiding (Cold + Submissive)
  • Appeasing (Warm + Submissive)

Key Insight: Assertive behaviour balances warmth and dominance.

WEEK 3 â€“ VISIONING

Creating your future

Visioning means imagining the desired future and translating it into actionable plans.

Why Vision Matters

  • Builds focus
  • Shapes culture
  • Increases resilience
  • Engages employees

The Process

  • Assess current thinking
  • Look at external trends
  • Involve others
  • Draft vision statement
  • Gain commitment

Vision must be:

  • Clear
  • Convincing
  • Credible
  • Inspiring
  • Actionable

A vision must be reviewed regularly.

WEEK 4 â€“ THE CHANGE CURVE

Understanding how people respond to change

Based on Elisabeth KÃ¼bler-Ross research.

Six Stages of Change

  1. Shock
  2. Denial
  3. Frustration and anger
  4. Depression
  5. Experiment and decision
  6. Acceptance and integration

People often move back and forth between stages. Leaders must support individuals through each phase.

WEEK 6 â€“ EMPLOYEE ENGAGEMENT AND THE THREE-FACTOR THEORY

The three things that matter most at work

Developed by Sirota Consulting.

1. Equity

  • Fair treatment
  • Safe environment
  • Fair pay
  • Respect

2. Achievement

  • Challenging work
  • Growth opportunities
  • Recognition
  • Pride in organization

3. Camaraderie

  • Good relationships
  • Supportive teamwork
  • Cross-department collaboration

Strong engagement improves performance for individuals and companies.

WEEK 7 â€“ THE NINE PRINCIPLES OF MOTIVATION

Creating the right environment

  1. Be motivated yourself
  2. Recruit motivated people
  3. Treat individuals uniquely
  4. Set realistic but challenging targets
  5. Focus on progress
  6. Remove motivational barriers
  7. Ensure fair rewards
  8. Recognize effort
  9. Be honest

Trust is the foundation of motivation.

WEEK 8 â€“ SITUATIONAL LEADERSHIP (LEADERSHIP STYLES)

Adapting your approach

Developed by Ken Blanchard and Spencer Johnson.

Leadership Styles

1. Directing (Telling)

  • High control
  • Suitable for new teams

2. Coaching (Engaging)

  • Teaching and guiding
  • Balances short-term and long-term

3. Supporting (Developing)

  • Listening and facilitating
  • Focus on development

4. Delegating (Hands-off)

  • Empowers experienced teams
  • Leader supports externally

Choose style based on:

  • Competence
  • Confidence
  • Motivation

WEEK 9 â€“ THE JOHN WHITMORE MODEL

Setting goals the right way

Whitmore emphasized goal-setting using SMART, PURE and CLEAR principles.

Goal-setting is essential during:

  • Appraisals
  • New roles
  • New projects

Each goal should meet Whitmoreâ€™s 14 criteria to ensure clarity, focus and achievement.

GOAL SETTING â€“ END GOALS vs PERFORMANCE GOALS

When goal-setting, distinguish clearly between end goals and performance goals:

  • End Goals are the ultimate objectives.Example: Promotion, completing a major project, achieving £300,000 in annual sales.
  • Performance Goals define the standards of performance required to achieve the end goal.Example: Improving negotiation skills, improving time management, increasing quality standards.

To assess the robustness of your goal-setting approach, ask:

  • What is your goal?
  • Is it specific? What exactly will success look like? Is it an end goal or a performance goal?
  • Is it measurable? How will progress be tracked?
  • Is it attainable? Do you have the required skills and resources?
  • How will you succeed? What risks exist?
  • Is it realistic? Does it align with other priorities?
  • What is the timeline? Are there milestones?
  • Is it stated positively and motivating?
  • Is it clearly understood?
  • Is it relevant to broader objectives?
  • Is it ethical?
  • Is it legal and compliant?
  • Is it environmentally responsible?
  • Is there agreement from stakeholders?
  • Is it documented and monitored regularly?

WEEK 10 â€“ ACTION-CENTRED LEADERSHIP

Managing the Task, Team and Individual

John Adairâ€™s model integrates three core responsibilities:

  1. Task Needs â€“ Systems, procedures, structures.
  2. Team Needs â€“ Cohesion and collaboration.
  3. Individual Needs â€“ Recognition, purpose, achievement, status.

All three must reinforce each other.

Eight Leadership Functions

  1. Define the Task â€“ Make it SMART.
  2. Plan â€“ Consider alternatives and contingencies.
  3. Brief Others â€“ Ensure clarity and open communication.
  4. Control Effectively â€“ Monitor, delegate, trust.
  5. Evaluate â€“ Assess performance and provide feedback.
  6. Motivate â€“ Recruit motivated people, reward progress.
  7. Be Organized â€“ Ensure systems and resources are in place.
  8. Set the Right Example â€“ Leadership by example.

WEEK 11 â€“ THE SIX STEPS OF DELEGATION

Developmental and Productive Leadership

Delegation improves productivity, innovation and retention.

Six Steps

  1. Prepare to DelegateClarify goals, priorities and expectations.
  2. Match the Person to the TaskUnderstand skills, potential and career aspirations.
  3. Discuss and Agree ObjectivesEnsure clarity and buy-in.
  4. Provide Resources and AuthorityEmpower decision-making.
  5. Monitor ProgressMaintain accountability with interim targets.
  6. Review ProgressLearn from results and improve future performance.

Delegation requires trust, empowerment and mindset.

WEEK 12 â€“ KOTTERâ€™S EIGHT-STAGE PROCESS FOR LEADING CHANGE

Driving Successful Organizational Change

  1. Establish UrgencyHighlight risks and opportunities.
  2. Create a Guiding CoalitionBuild a powerful, united team.
  3. Develop Vision and StrategyClear, simple and practical direction.
  4. Communicate the VisionRepeated, consistent communication.
  5. Empower Broad-Based ActionRemove barriers and encourage innovation.
  6. Generate Short-Term WinsPlan visible successes and reward contributors.
  7. Consolidate GainsMaintain momentum and deepen change.
  8. Anchor Change in CultureEmbed behaviours into organizational DNA.

WEEK 13 â€“ SIX PRINCIPLES FOR GAINING COMMITMENT

Maximizing Engagement During Transition

  1. Develop the Right PlanEnsure senior alignment.
  2. Plan the Transition TimelineConsider emotional and operational impact.
  3. Clarify InvolvementDefine roles clearly.
  4. Set Standards and Measure ProgressEncourage accountability without demotivation.
  5. Connect IndividuallyCelebrate past achievements and future potential.
  6. Tell and Sell the StrategyCommunicate with empathy and honesty.

Commitment drives performance.

WEEK 14 â€“ BELBINâ€™S TEAM ROLES

Building and Managing Effective Teams

R. Meredith Belbin identified nine team roles. While individuals may show multiple traits, one dominant style usually prevails.

Understanding team roles helps in:

  • Effective delegation
  • Conflict management
  • Motivation
  • Performance optimization

Belbinâ€™s Nine Team Roles

  1. Plant â€“ Creative innovator
  2. Resource Investigator â€“ Networker and opportunity finder
  3. Coordinator â€“ Clarifies goals and delegates
  4. Shaper â€“ Drives action and momentum
  5. Monitor Evaluator â€“ Analytical and objective
  6. Team Worker â€“ Supportive and diplomatic
  7. Implementer â€“ Practical and disciplined
  8. Completer Finisher â€“ Detail-oriented and quality-focused
  9. Specialist â€“ Expert in specific area

Strong teams balance complementary roles.

WEEK 15 â€“ DRIVERS OF TRUST AND THE TRUST CYCLE

What We Look for When Choosing to Trust Someone

The diagnostic questionnaire for Belbinâ€™s Team Role Analysis is available at:www.belbin.com (http://www.belbin.com/)

Trust matters because success can only be achieved by working through others. Inspiring trust encourages flexibility, collaboration and productive relationships.

Drivers of Trust

The main drivers of trust are:

  • Fairness
  • Dependability
  • Respect
  • Openness
  • Courage
  • Unselfishness
  • Competence
  • Supportiveness
  • Empathy
  • Compassion

Promoting these qualities strengthens relationships with colleagues, customers and stakeholders.

The Reality of Trust

In practice, we often encounter:

  • Likeability
  • Dependability
  • Critical behaviour
  • Ambition
  • Fairness
  • Professionalism
  • Competence
  • Respect
  • Controlling behaviour
  • Predictability

The Trust Deficit

When expectations are not met, trust is damaged. Rebuilding trust is time-consuming and stressful. Preventing a trust deficit is far easier than repairing it.

The Trust Cycle

  1. Explore â€“ Understand issues and priorities.
  2. Commit â€“ Agree what will be delivered, how and when.
  3. Deliver â€“ Take action and fulfil promises.
  4. Confirm â€“ Check that expectations were met.

Trust must be maintained continuously.

WEEK 16 â€“ THE TRUTHS OF STRATEGY

Who, What, How: Succeeding with Business Strategy

Strategy is about understanding:

  • Where you are now
  • Where you are heading
  • How you will get there

Three Essential Elements of Strategy

  1. Development
  2. Implementation
  3. Selling (Gaining commitment)

Three Strategic Dimensions

  1. Who â€“ Which customers to target (and avoid).
  2. What â€“ What products/services to offer.
  3. How â€“ How activities are performed efficiently.

Key Success Factors

  • Create a unique strategic position.
  • Make tough, distinctive choices.
  • Align culture, incentives and processes.
  • Gain emotional commitment.
  • Close the knowledge-doing gap.
  • Keep strategy flexible and adaptable.

Strategy requires clarity, discipline and commitment.

WEEK 17 â€“ SWOT ANALYSIS

A Valuable Decision-Making Tool

SWOT stands for:

  • Strengths
  • Weaknesses
  • Opportunities
  • Threats

Internal Factors

Strengths and weaknesses come from within the organization.

Examples:

  • Skills
  • Experience
  • Resources
  • Culture
  • Processes

Some factors can shift quickly from strength to weakness.

External Factors

Opportunities and threats arise externally.

Opportunities:

  • New markets
  • New technologies
  • Product innovations
  • Strategic partnerships
  • Regulatory changes

Threats:

  • Political or regulatory changes
  • Economic downturn
  • New competitors
  • Pricing wars
  • Environmental crises
  • Security risks
  • Supply chain disruptions

SWOT helps identify priorities and guide decision-making.

WEEK 18 â€“ SCENARIO THINKING

Walking the Battlefield Before Battle Begins

Scenario thinking explores possible futures to strengthen strategy and decision-making.

Purpose of Scenario Thinking

  • Challenge assumptions
  • Promote innovation
  • Improve risk management
  • Enhance responsiveness
  • Prepare for uncertainty

The Strategic Conversation

Ongoing process of:

  • Assessing present conditions
  • Creating and testing scenarios
  • Developing options
  • Selecting and implementing strategies

Initial Planning

  • Form a diverse planning team
  • Identify knowledge gaps
  • Gather input (â€œhistory of the futureâ€)
  • Analyze uncertainties

Developing Scenarios

Participants should:

  • Identify key forces
  • Define opposite outcomes
  • Assess probability and impact
  • Develop logical future stories

Using Scenarios

  • Identify early warning signals
  • Work backwards from possible futures
  • Develop proactive action plans

The goal is not prediction, but preparation.

WEEK 19 â€“ THE BALANCED SCORECARD

Linking Strategy to Measurable Performance

Developed by Robert Kaplan and David Norton.

Traditional measures focus on past performance. The Balanced Scorecard adds future-oriented performance indicators.

Four Key Areas

  1. Financial Performance
  2. Customer Perspective
  3. Internal Processes
  4. Learning and Innovation

Five Broad Implementation Stages

  1. Define and communicate strategy
  2. Decide what to measure
  3. Set targets
  4. Align initiatives
  5. Review and refine continuously

The Balanced Scorecard integrates quantitative and qualitative measures to provide a holistic view of performance.

Finalize and implement the plan â€“ this stage ensures that measures are workable, tailored and adopted. Essentially, this is managing by setting objectives.

4. Publicize and use the results â€“ being seen to act is important. Also, while ensuring that everyone understands overall objectives, decide who should receive specific information, why and how frequently.

5. Review and amend the system â€“ to solve any problems and to set new challenges

WEEK 20

THE 7S MODEL

Assessing business performance

The 7S model is a framework for assessing the performance of a company. It views all seven elements as equally important because they impact on each other â€“ with failure in one area undermining the others. By appreciating how they are related, and assessing performance from this perspective, companies and teams can better align activities to achieve goals.

Overview

First developed in the 1970s by McKinsey and refined by Tom Peters, Robert Waterman and Richard Pascale, the 7S model works from the principle that success relies on simultaneously pursuing a combination of seven hard and soft aspects of running a business. Known for changing peopleâ€™s thinking at the time, it still provides a useful framework for assessing and improving a company or how a team is working â€“ identifying gaps and enabling adjustments to be made to ensure that all seven aspects are aligned, working together, and supporting and reinforcing one another. By knowing how things are interrelated, the framework raises awareness of the full impact of any changes.

  1. StrategyThese are plans that determine, define and outline how to fulfill the companyâ€™s goals and purpose and to achieve competitive advantage.
  2. StructureThis is how the company is organized and how each part relates to the others.
  3. SystemsThis is about how both formal and informal business processes function.
  4. Shared values (superordinate goals)These are the companyâ€™s beliefs, values and guiding mission that draw people together and that directly influence their approach, thinking and actions.
  5. SkillsThese are the capabilities of both the people and the organization.
  6. StaffThis concerns the nature, type and general abilities of the people employed.
  7. StyleThis is the organizationâ€™s culture and style of leadership that, along with having an internal impact, determine how people outside the organization view the company.

The main point is that all seven elements are interrelated, with each affecting the others. In this, it can be viewed as an early proponent of holistic business. Significantly â€“ and this is of particular relevance to leaders today â€“ it reveals how underperformance can be attributed to neglect in any one of the seven aspects, regardless of strong focus and capabilities in one or more of the others. Richard Pascale subsequently argued that, while it is generally important to view all seven as equally significant to achieving success, having shared values (superordinate goals) is the element that binds all the others together.

WEEK 21

THE RULE OF 150

A bold way to create the right working conditions

This rule is about limiting the number of people at any one location to 150.

Overview

The rule is based on the idea that 150 is the largest group size that people can deal with â€“ beyond that number, it is increasingly difficult to form bonds with others. If groups are larger, hierarchies, regulations and formal measures are required. However, with fewer than 150, goals can be achieved informally and people work better and are happier, more motivated and more productive.

Why it works

Co-workers find socializing, teamworking, innovating, collaborating and sharing knowledge easier to achieve in groups of fewer than 150 people. By organizing operations into smaller groups, large companies can gain the benefit of smaller groups â€“ being closer, driven, entrepreneurial, supportive and productive.

The rule in practice

Gore Associates, a high-tech firm, uses this rule. It has 15 plants all within 20 kilometres (12 miles) of one another, and each with fewer than 150 employees. It has resisted the option of merging its separate sites â€“ despite potential cost savings â€“ because the small size of each unit ensures that everyone knows everyone else and works well together.

By organizing itself in this way, Gore, despite being a large company with thousands of employees, is still able to enjoy the entrepreneurial approach of a small start-up. Each unit enjoys the benefits of collective management, which are:

â€¢ improved communicationâ€¢ greater initiativeâ€¢ flexibility

It is notable that employee turnover is significantly less than the industry average and the company has enjoyed sustained profitability and growth for over 35 years.

This does not mean that Gore has no control or input. It has put a strong managerial system in place to oversee each unit, to ensure that activities are coordinated and efficient. The company also encourages a sense of community and teamwork within these groups â€“ after all, the rule only means that it is possible for workers to form positive bonds with each other, so efforts must still be made to ensure that this happens. In addition, Gore makes sure that it develops a sense of community across the company by encouraging people to communicate and collaborate with workers from other groups.

WEEK 22

THE SERVICE PROFIT CHAIN

Managing the vital link between people and profit

The service profit chain highlights how employee engagement drives improvements in company performance. When employees are able to see the impact of their actions, it changes their approach and improves results.

The idea

The service profit chain is based on the premise that market leadership requires an emphasis on managing value drivers â€” those factors that have the greatest impact on success and provide the most benefit to customers. This concept is then focused on the value drivers that are the most important determinants of success: employee retention, employee satisfaction and employee productivity â€” it is these that strongly influence customer loyalty, revenue growth and profitability.

How the service profit chain works

In practice: Sears

In the 1990s US-based retailer Sears reversed significant losses by focusing on employee issues in order to turn around the companyâ€™s fortunes. They examined:

â€¢ how employees felt about working at the companyâ€¢ how employee behaviour affected customersâ€¢ how customersâ€™ experience affected profits

Sears asked employees to estimate how much profit was made for each dollar sold. The average answer was 46 cents while the real answer was 1 cent â€” demonstrating that profitability was poorly understood. The company introduced changes in order to engage with employees and to get them to understand what influences profitability â€” in particular, to make clear the link between employee behaviour, customer satisfaction and company success. By understanding the implications of their actions, it changed their approach, resulting in sustained improvements in profitability.

In practice: B&Q

At UK retailer B&Q, each percentage increase in staff turnover was costing the company £1 million. By reducing staff turnover from 35 to 28 per cent through its Employee Engagement Programme, the company reduced costs and increased turnover per employee by 20 per cent.

WEEK 23

UNDERSTANDING AND AVOIDING INERTIA

When success traps us in the past

It might seem counterintuitive to warn people about the dangers of success but that is exactly what Donald Sull did when he developed the concept of â€˜active inertiaâ€™ â€” where people repeat the strategies and activities that have worked well in the past.

A reliance on previous thinking and approaches â€” the formula of success â€” can cause a company to fail to respond properly to new developments. By applying past approaches to new conditions, the end result can be a downward spiral â€” leaving an organization vulnerable to more dynamic companies with approaches better suited to the new environment.

How active inertia works

A firm correctly discerns gradual shifts and developments in the external environment, but fails to respond effectively.

Managers get trapped by success, often responding to the most disruptive changes by accelerating activities that succeeded in the past.

The source of active inertia is a companyâ€™s success formula, the unique set of strategic frames, resources, processes, relationships and values that collectively influence managersâ€™ actions.

With time and repetition, people stop considering alternatives to their formula. The individual components of the success formula grow less flexible.

How active inertia happens

Active inertia occurs because people come to rely on a past formula of success, where accepted approaches become entrenched and people stop considering alternatives. Consequently, people continue to respond to external changes by pursuing fixes and activities that worked in the past. However, these responses are likely to be ineffectual because they are based on past success and not current and future needs.

Why a past success formula does not guarantee a successful future

Essentially, like it or not, our brains are lazy â€” subconsciously preferring the easy route to solving problems and then, equally subconsciously, superimposing a solid layer of reasons to justify our decisions. So it is hardly surprising that our brains fool us into being happy to rely on approaches that have proven successful in the past: it is easy and we have a ready-made wall of rock-solid excuses to hand.

As individuals, our thinking, strategies, methods, use of resources, relationships and values all become firmly entrenched. The consequence for companies is that this formula becomes so deeply embedded that they are left vulnerable when faced with changing conditions.

It is understandable that past approaches should be so revered and relied upon â€” they are, after all, the reason for the companyâ€™s current success. However, we should keep in mind that this formula is exactly that: suited to the current, stable situation â€” not the future. Companies can suddenly find themselves commercially stranded.

The bottom line is that, when faced with new developments, your approach needs to change accordingly â€” essentially, the survival of the fittest depends on adaptation.

WEEK 24

THE SIX Rs OF BUSINESS

Business is a total activity

Luis Gallardoâ€™s Six Rs is a total approach to business â€” where all activities work together, moving the whole company forward in the same direction.

Having all company activities support one other enables us to develop the right mindset, strategy and approach for growing a successful business. This holistic approach ensures that no part of a company undermines overall goals or the activities of another part of the business. The Six Rs are:

â€¢ Reasonâ€¢ Revenueâ€¢ Rousersâ€¢ Reputationâ€¢ Relationshipsâ€¢ Resilience

Why the Six Rs matter

The Six Rs should work together, supporting one other and never undermining other business activities or goals. As companies can discover to their cost (witness the damage to sales when legal tax avoidance is revealed), any aspect of running a business can have serious consequences. Conversely, when the various corporate activities support one other, they will strengthen the brand and promote success. Essentially, everyone and all activities should pull together. To have parts, even unwittingly, pulling in different directions will derail strategy and cause a company to veer off course.

Reason

The starting point, and ongoing requirement, for setting and directing all activities is to know the reason why you are in business â€” your vision, values and purpose. This sets the tone and gains commitment and, consequently, has an enormous impact on customers and achieving goals. Your purpose should be communicated to everyone in the organization. Also, by fitting your products and services to your reason and values, customers and employees will understand what your company means.

Revenue

Managing and maximizing revenues is essential for enacting strategies and building resilience. An often overlooked but critical aspect is the portfolio of clients â€” it reveals strengths and gaps elsewhere in the company. The important thing is to manage revenues through the prism of the rest of the 6Rs â€” and to manage the others through the lens of revenue.

Rousers

Engaging your people and aligning their thinking and behaviours to the rest of the companyâ€™s activities depend on being able to inspire them. This has an enormous impact on all areas of a business â€” especially customers â€” and sets the right conditions for people to be innovative and to adapt successfully to change.

Reputation

Reputation is critical to success. It affects employees as well as current and potential customers and all stakeholders. The important point is that reputation can be affected by any aspect of the business â€” emphasizing the need to ensure that other activities do not undermine reputation.

Relationships

All business â€” internal and external â€” is about handling relationships. Everything is affected, with a direct bearing on profitability, so all relationships should be managed carefully, keeping in mind the importance of the Six Rs approach.

Resilience

Developing resilience enables companies to continue achieving goals, to survive difficult circumstances and to take advantage of opportunities. It enables swift and appropriate responses to any developments and the flexibility to adapt to change. Resilience involves being proactive, prepared and having the right mindset to deal with any events, threats or opportunities.

WEEK 25

THE BOSTON CONSULTING GROUP MODEL

How to manage your product portfolio

Identifying which products and investments should be continued (and at what level of investment) is a complicated task. Cutting through this confusion, the Boston Consulting Group model (developed by Bruce Henderson) provides a straightforward means of managing your portfolio of products.

How it works

The model uses a matrix, each box representing a type of product: Star, Cash cow, Question mark and Dog. Products are located in a quadrant according to market growth and market share. The category a product falls into enables you to see whether it is worth pursuing. By looking at the matrix, it is easy to see why each category has certain characteristics and prospects.

Star

Given the high market growth, this product is obviously a rising star and should be pursued. Coupled with high market share, the risks are minimal and the return will be high. A note of caution, though, is that a growing market will inevitably cost a lot to keep up with so it is advisable to consider your ability to fund this â€” especially if there are large set-up costs or if you expect a delay in the product generating revenue.

Cash cow

Clearly, given the large market share, there is still a lot of potential for generating revenue. However, given the low market growth, there may be some limiting factors (such as time or changing technology) that suggest you should milk these products as much as you can before the opportunity for high returns dwindles in a declining market. It would be wise to monitor market conditions closely to prevent losses should the market decline rapidly.

Question mark

If a product falls into this category, there are issues that need to be addressed before a decision can be made. Although there is high market growth, you have to ask yourself whether the low market share will generate enough revenue to justify the investment â€” especially given the likely high costs of keeping pace with a growing market. A key factor in making a decision is having deep-enough pockets either to wait for higher returns as the market grows or to turn it into a Star by securing a stronger market share.

Dog

With low market share and low market growth, this product is going nowhere fast. Clearly, it is not worth pursuing. Sometimes, you may wish to continue with this type of product if it provides other benefits â€” such as maintaining customer loyalty for your overall brand.

WEEK 26

THE PARETO PRINCIPLE

Finding the right locus and answer using the 80:20 rule

Pareto analysis arose from Vilfredo Paretoâ€™s observation that many activities break down into an 80:20 ratio, where 80 per cent of output is due to 20 per cent of the contributory factors. This observation is now used to focus business strategy, problem-solving and operations on the key inputs that are responsible for 80 per cent of the outcome.

How it works

The 80:20 ratio applies both to positive and negative situations, providing a useful means of dealing quickly with problems or opportunities. In other words, by identifying the small number of key factors that are contributing most to a situation, we can better focus efforts to achieve the desired result.

Pareto analysis is only as good as the data that is used, so we need to ensure that all contributory factors are identified and that appropriate and revealing parameters and measures are established and interpreted correctly. Although not everything falls neatly into an 80:20 rule, Pareto analysis is still useful for identifying the main causal factors.

This simple example shows how the process works:

  1. Research and discuss the issue, identifying all contributory factors.
  2. Decide an appropriate time period and method of measurement.
  3. Measure how frequently each factor occurs (or another measure, such as cost).
  4. Rank the factors in descending order, with the largest one first.
  5. Calculate the frequency of each factor as a percentage of the total occurrences (or cost).
  6. Calculate the cumulative percentage (current percentage plus all previous percentages).
  7. Depict this information on a graph â€“ with â€˜frequency as a percentage of totalâ€™ as a bar chart and â€˜cumulative percentageâ€™ as a line, adding a third line showing the 80 per cent cut-off point.

All factors that appear to the left of the intersection of the two lines are the ones contributing to 80 per cent of the result â€” these are the factors to focus on.

Example of how the Pareto Principle can be displayed.

WEEK 27

BLUE OCEAN STRATEGY

Creating unique market opportunities

A Blue Ocean Strategy is one where the key to success lies not in competing directly with rivals within a market, but in creating an entirely new market where there are currently no competitors and where the potential for high returns is vast.

Developed by W. Chan Kim and Renee Mauborgne, Blue Ocean Strategy involves a change in strategic thinking towards a mindset that challenges existing market boundaries, rewrites the rules of competition, and creates a new, as yet uncontested, market space. The theory outlines two attitudes to competition: Red Oceans and Blue Oceans.

The current marketplace for all products and services is made up of Red Oceans (bloody battlegrounds), where boundaries are clearly defined and companies operate within the boundaries of their accepted Red Ocean markets. Here, the entrenched battleground is one where companies compete to gain extra market share within the current market boundary.

A very different attitude pervades the Blue Oceans. These are areas of deep, uncharted, almost limitless potential where the aim is not to compete on traditional grounds but to develop products and services that create entirely new markets. In essence, it is creating customers that do not yet exist.

At its core, Blue Ocean Strategy believes that it is better to create tomorrowâ€™s customers through developing a new market rather than scrabbling around trying to capture existing customers in the current marketplace. There may be many justifications for this approach but, quite simply, the reason seems straightforward: to create a monopoly situation and reap the high rewards before competitors enter the new market.

Value creation

Value is achieved by integrating the utility of the product with its cost and price. It is not a case of choosing between competing through managing costs or product differentiation: it is about pursuing both. It is this that creates the value that appeals across customer groups, drawing them into a new market. Think of this as maximizing the gap between the utility of the product and its price (facilitated by lower costs) â€” the larger this gap, the higher the value and the more it attracts customers.

Blue Ocean Strategy relies on four main principles:

  1. Challenging existing market boundaries.Reconstruct the marketplace, identifying and creating new markets and customers. The Blue Ocean is a vast place where demand is unrealized â€” it doesnâ€™t yet exist. The aim is to bring this demand into existence.
  2. Keeping focused on the overall picture.Be clear about your goals: what matters and needs to be achieved.
  3. Minimizing risk.Assess current industry standards and decide what can be:a. eliminated â€” things that are not necessaryb. reduced â€” things that do not need to be done to a high standardc. raised â€” things that should be done betterd. created â€” things that have never been offered before.
  4. Planning careful implementation.You will need to overcome barriers and secure the resources and the support of your people (especially key influencers).

Continuing exactly as written â€” no reduction.

WEEK 28

BENCHMARKING

Measuring performance

Benchmarking establishes standards against which performance can be measured. It is used to assess performance and to set targets across a range of business activities.

Overview

The purpose of benchmarking is to improve efficiency and quality, to determine and promote best practice, to maintain competitiveness and to focus people on the need for change and improvement. Carol McNair and Kathleen Leibfried divide benchmarking into four categories as shown below:

Category â€” Aim

InternalUsing internal measures to match or surpass current performance, ensure consistent standards throughout the company, eliminate waste and improve operations

CompetitiveUsing competitorsâ€™ standards to set targets that match or improve upon their performance

IndustrySetting benchmarks that are industry standards

Best-in-classTo match or surpass the standards of the best companies in any industry or country

Setting benchmarks

The data should be free from bias or vested interests. Using an external company to gather evidence and measure standards will help to maintain impartiality.

Successful benchmarking needs everyone to be â€˜on the same pageâ€™ and to understand the process. People need to be clear about what is being measured and why, and it is important to give people the time and resources they need.

While targets need to be realistic and achievable, they also need to ensure that standards are maintained and consistent throughout a company and they should seek to continually improve upon performance. To do this, it is necessary to look at both internal and external evidence.

Benchmarking is a continual process that needs to adapt quickly to changes â€” it is no use measuring activities that are no longer relevant or failing to measure activities that are now more significant. To do this effectively, as well as assessing internal operations, you need a keen awareness of your customers, competitors and companies in other sectors. This ensures that benchmarking is focused on the issues that matter now rather than reflecting the past, and is not blinkered by a narrow, internal focus that risks delivering more of the same.

By enabling you to know what competitors are doing and what the most innovative, high-performing companies in other industries are achieving, benchmarking will help to maintain your companyâ€™s competitiveness.

WEEK 29

THE PRODUCT LIFE CYCLE

Managing your product portfolio

From development and launch, through its peak to eventual decline, a productâ€™s life cycle determines the strategy needed to optimize its return at each stage and to develop further products to ensure ongoing profitability and competitiveness.

Although not an exact science, the duration of each stage varies according to the product and the markets involved. Some life cycles are obviously shorter than others â€” such as technology products. With very short life cycles, it is essential to maximize returns as quickly as possible and to be continually developing the next products. A long-lasting branded product, despite undergoing many life cycles, enjoys continuity from its brand name.
