So, tweaking this and that further up the line may help to a degree but may not be tackling the root cause of the problem: a flawed or outdated business model. You are not likely to make significant changes to your situation without questioning the basic paradigm of your company and considering whether it is time to overhaul the entire business model.

One of the main hurdles in dealing with a failing or underperforming company is overcoming peopleâ€™s mental blocks that seriously limit the scope of strategic thinking. Such strategic inertia is a recipe for long-term decline because, when a company doesnâ€™t keep pace with external developments, its strategy drifts. It is essential to break out of the business-as-usual mindset and to open your thinking to possibilities. Competition doesnâ€™t stand still and neither should your business model.

The process of paradigm change

The following diagram outlines three stages of improving business performance. The first step involves tightening controls. The second step involves developing new strategies that are still aligned with the current paradigm. The third step involves changing the paradigm itself.

Crucially, this model is designed to improve business performance. It therefore starts with an existing model or paradigm, translated into a strategy which is then implemented. The opportunity and impetus to improve the business model becomes compelling only after the strategy has been implemented and the effects on performance are assessed. At that point the process of reinvention can gain pace starting with step 1 â€“ the need for tighter controls â€“ before moving to steps 2 and 3.

WEEK 36

ANSOFFâ€™S PRODUCT MATRIXGetting from A to B

Ansoffâ€™s Product Matrix provides a useful means of clarifying your thinking through generating a snapshot of where you are and where you would like to be and enabling you to identify strategic priorities.

By helping you to see the gap between the current situation and your goals, the Product Matrix serves to illuminate your situation, your goals, your thinking and the route you need to take. Knowing your goal isnâ€™t enough: you need to know what needs to be done to get there. Strategy consists of two elements: portfolio strategy and competitive strategy. Portfolio strategy sets the goals for each product and market, while competitive strategy determines how to achieve those goals.

The grid

The grid has four areas that point to different options, depending on your current situation and goals.

Current product

New product

Current market

Market penetration

Increase market share

Product development

Develop new products for existing markets

New market

Market development

Take existing products into new markets

Diversification

Develop entirely new products for new markets

The portfolio strategy explores each product and market combination as geographical growth vectors. These vectors have three aspects â€“ market needs, market location and product needs (such as required technology). The three-dimensional nature of Ansoffâ€™s grid highlights the many points of intersection of current and potential products, market locations and market needs. By seeing how these aspects intersect, it will clarify the strategic options that are open to your company.

Ansoffâ€™s Product Matrix provides a clear snapshot to help you set and achieve strategic goals. There are four aspects to using the matrix that are all connected â€“ the priorities you set in one will inevitably affect the others. The four aspects are:

  1. The geographical growth vector. Know where you are and where you want to be. Assess your current product and market combinations and decide what and where you would like those combinations to be in the future.
  2. Competitive advantage. Determine your core strengths and what gives you a competitive edge. Then identify the resources and capabilities needed to achieve goals â€“ know what your company does well and not so well and the skills, resources and technology it will need to acquire.
  3. Synergies. Identify synergies between activities, cut costs and bolster competitiveness.
  4. Flexibility. Ensure that your company is prepared for the unexpected and is able to respond quickly and effectively to change. Make sure that one part of the company can incorporate change without harming other parts.

WEEK 37

RESOURCES AND THE CRITICAL PATHThe drivers of business performance

â€˜Resourcesâ€™ is an overused term in business but any factor providing value or benefit, from whatever origin, is a resource that can be used to benefit the business. Increasing and strengthening resources over time can be seen as the critical path to business success.

Managing resources

Assessing which resources are important involves taking a view across the whole of the business and identifying those factors, direct or indirect, tangible or intangible, that can be expanded and used for competitive advantage. Understanding which resources are most important and how they should be managed requires a clear understanding of the nature of each resource, in terms of the following:

â€¢The interaction between resources. Resources can combine in a cycle to accelerate their growth. For example, rising sales volumes may lead to more cash and more internal capacity, both of which can be used to generate increasing sales, perhaps by entering new markets, in a self-sustaining cycle. Similarly, product quality (an intangible resource) may lead to increased sales, and this in turn can generate sufficient cash to continue improving product quality (and continue increasing sales). In the same way that resources can interact to reinforce one another, they can also interact by limiting one another.

â€¢The fragility of the resource. Cash, quality, customers, staff, reputation and most other resources can all disappear with remarkable speed and ease. It is, therefore, important to control the main factors likely to damage or undermine resources. For example: cash needs to be monitored and controlled; quality can be eroded by suppliers; service can be undermined by the attitudes of personnel; and brand reputation may be damaged by the actions of distributors.

â€¢The quality of resources. It is worth considering how the quality of re-sources can be developed. For example, a customer base is a valuable re-source, but its quality might be improved by increasing customer loyalty to your brand â€“ for instance, by using customer loyalty schemes.

How resources affect performance

Resources have a special characteristic: they fill and drain over time. Since a firmâ€™s performance at any time directly reflects the resources available, it is essential that we understand how those resources develop over time and how we can control that process. To build strong business performance, we need to know:

â€¢ how many resources are availableâ€¢ how fast these numbers are changingâ€¢ how strongly these factors are being influenced by things under our control and by other forcesâ€¢ how resources interrelate with one another.

In a system where resources are integrated and working together, what matters is not the uniqueness of individual resources but how they combine and work together to deliver value for customers. To manage resources and ensure that they drive performance in the desired direction, start by understanding how resources work together.

WEEK 38

DEVELOPING INTANGIBLE RESOURCESIntangibles: what they are, why they matter, and what they can do for you

Soft â€˜intangibleâ€™ factors can play a crucial role in developing a businessâ€™s competitive performance. For example, a charity with strong commitment from its donors will achieve its goals more easily, and a business with a culture that encourages coaching, risk-taking, new ideas and avoids blame is more likely to make improvements and achieve progress.

Unfortunately, intangibles can be tough to manage. You may easily borrow cash, buy production capacity or hire staff, but it is slow and difficult to build staff morale, a strong reputation, support from a charityâ€™s donors or to generate new ideas.

Overview

Resources can typically be classified into two of four categories: either direct or indirect and tangible or intangible.

â€¢Direct resources are those factors such as staff expertise, cash or intellectual property that can be developed and nurtured by the business. Customers are, perhaps, the biggest single direct resource. (Viewing customers as a resource focuses thinking on how to accumulate and retain them.)

â€¢Indirect resources are those factors that have a bearing on the quality, strength and value of resources. For example, effective training and development policies are an indirect resource, as they build the effectiveness of staff expertise.

â€¢Tangible resources are those that can be physically seen, such as cash, inventory, sales volumes and customers; typically, these have the highest profile within the organization, as they are the most apparent.

â€¢Intangible resources such as service quality, brand reputation or staff expertise are also vitally important to success.

Of these, intangible resources can be the hardest to manage (and the easiest to ignore). Several techniques will help ensure that intangible resources are working well with the rest of the business:

â€¢ Identify the most important intangibles. Since your performance relies on concrete resources, assess whether an intangible factor is likely to influence your ability to win or lose the resources. It is not advisable to waste time examining too many factors, as it is more likely that only one or two factors will have a significant impact.

â€¢ Be clear which of these factors genuinely â€˜accumulateâ€™ through time and which are simply current features of the business. â€˜Qualityâ€™ and â€˜serviceâ€™ reflect the balance between what has to be done and what is available to do it, in which case they do not accumulate. Reputation, motivation, commitment and relationships, on the other hand, are built up and drain away over time in response to events.

â€¢ Assess intangibles carefully, identif9 the best measure and also the events causing each intangible to rise or fall. Look for ways to strengthen intangibles.

â€¢ Build intangible measures into your performance tracking system. Reporting systems now commonly incorporate soft measures (as distinct from hard data, such as financial measures) from various parts of the organization, recognizing that soft measures such as engagement or reputation are crucial to a well-performing system.

â€¢ If you donâ€™t know, donâ€™t ignore the issue. Soft factors are influencing your organization, continually and powerfully. Remember, if you choose to ignore them, you are not, in fact, really leaving them out. Instead, you are assuming that they are satisfactory and unchanging. This is unlikely to remain correct, so make your best estimate and start tracking and understanding them.

WEEK 39

MARKET POSITIONING AND VALUE CURVESChoosing the best position in the market for your business or product

A value curve is a way of highlighting customersâ€™ needs and preferences. This can be used to understand a firmâ€™s competitive position, as well as potential trade-offs, opportunities and areas for further development.

Competing firms emphasize and trade off different things that customers value. For example:

â€¢ The UK retailer The Body Shop traded the slick packaging, clinical approach and glamorous image traditionally favored by the cosmetics industry in return for a lower price and a more sustainable identity (see diagram).â€¢ In the USA South-West Airlines pioneered low-cost aviation by trading the features of traditional air travel in return for the benefits of cheap, point-to-point travel.â€¢ Multiplex cinemas traded the conventional convenience and centrality of town centre locations in return for the benefits of space and a different experience for customers.â€¢ Home Depot expanded into out-of-town locations on freeways and employed ex-contractors as a way of providing a new level of service and value for customers who did not typically visit home building stores.

The concept of value curves highlights several points about market positioning:

â€¢ Competing firms emphasize and trade off different values (e.g. luxury may be traded for a lower price).â€¢ Customers value specific features (e.g. price, packaging) differently at different times.â€¢ Different values enable firms to target new, different â€“ and possibly un-fulfilled â€“ market segments, potentially increasing the size of the market.â€¢ Initially, strategic innovators (e.g. South-West Airlines) create new â€˜market spaceâ€™, gradually redefining the market.â€¢ It can be extremely difficult, if not impossible, for incumbents to successfully copy new arrivals. This is because internal cultural and resource issues keep firms anchored in their conventional way of working.â€¢ When reviewing a value curve, consider the trend: how are things changing?WEEK 40

COMPETITIVE ANALYSIS: PORTERâ€™S FIVE FORCESHow competitive is your company?

Porterâ€™s Five Forces model provides a deeper understanding of a firmâ€™s current competitiveness and highlights options to improve competitiveness.

Michael Porter outlines five forces for competitive analysis:

  1. New entrants
  2. Substitute products
  3. Buyers
  4. Suppliers
  5. Existing competitors.

1. New entrantsAsk yourself how easy it is for new companies to enter the market. There are many factors to consider, including barriers to entry (such as patents and high set-up costs), attractiveness of profit margins and the strength of your brand.

2. SubstitutesAssess how easy it is for your products to be substituted by other products. This includes all alternatives â€“ not just similar products. For example, airlines compete with train and coach companies, not just other airlines.

3. BuyersReview how strong your buyers are. Is it a buyersâ€™ market? Can buyers switch to competitors easily? Are some of your customers in such a strong position that this leaves you vulnerable? If your business-to-business buyers are operating at low profit margins, what impact will this have on your company?

4. SuppliersAssess the strength of your suppliers. Are you dependent on a particular supplier â€“ and how can this be mitigated? Does the supplier rely on your custom or could it easily take its operating capacity to other companies or sell directly to your customers? Could you use alternative products or methods to reduce your vulnerability?

5. Existing competitorsUnderstand your competitors and how you compare to them.â€¢ What threat do they pose?â€¢ What are their strengths and weaknesses?â€¢ Could there be a price war or other aggressive strategies â€“ and would you be able to survive such tactics?â€¢ Are they innovative?â€¢ Are customers able to move to other companies easily?â€¢ Now many competitors are there?â€¢ Which companies are the strongest?â€¢ Are there any newcomers ready to take the market by storm or render your products redundant?

Assessing competitiveness through all five forces will help you to determine how the company is performing, its strengths and weaknesses and the direction it is heading in. Because a weakness of Porterâ€™s approach is the focus on external issues, it is often used alongside complementary models that are better at revealing the internal issues that impact on a companyâ€™s competitiveness.

WEEK 41

INNOVATION HOTSPOTSHow to build a culture of innovation

Developed by Professor Lynda Gratton, Innovation Hotspots occur where conditions are right and there is encouragement â€“ they cannot be formally imposed. Encouragement is needed in four areas, which are:

  1. a co-operative mindset
  2. boundary spanning
  3. developing a sense of purpose
  4. productive capacity.

1. A co-operative mindsetA co-operative mindset results from a companyâ€™s practices, processes, behaviours and norms â€“ the behaviour of top management is significant. People have to want to share both explicit and tacit knowledge. Several elements are vital:â€¢ Consider relationships when selecting staff.â€¢ Emphasize relationships in inductions.â€¢ Provide mentoring.â€¢ Emphasize collective rewards over individual ones.â€¢ Establish structures that facilitate peer-to-peer working.â€¢ Develop social responsibility.

2. Boundary spanningThis involves thinking beyond your immediate boundaries â€“ seeing the larger picture. This involves:â€¢ being undeterred by physical distanceâ€¢ welcoming a diverse range of ideas, insights, experience and peopleâ€¢ being willing and able to explore issues togetherâ€¢ networking and building bridges for others to crossâ€¢ using different levels of co-operation (e.g. use strong ties where developing trust quickly is important; use weak ties to generate a lot of ideas)â€¢ listening and reflecting in conversations rather than just pushing a point of view.

3. Developing a sense of purposePose challenging (or â€˜ignitingâ€™) questions. These donâ€™t have a â€˜rightâ€™ answer; they invite exploration of options. They inspire and engage people and lead to a new vision that provides purpose and energy.

4. Productive capacityEnsuring that a hotspot realizes its full potential relies on building productive capacity by:â€¢ understanding and appreciating the talents of othersâ€¢ obtaining practical, public and explicit commitment from participantsâ€¢ harnessing the creative energy which results from problem-solving and decision-makingâ€¢ synchronizing time, especially where different time zones have to be accommodated or where there are different attitudes to timeâ€¢ ensuring that pressure is neither too high, where people burn out, or too low, where they lose interest.

Innovation relies on teamwork, agility and the ability to lead change. Crucially, it is about mindset: you need to think like an innovator and you need to encourage this in others. Innovation isnâ€™t only about products â€“ itâ€™s about understanding customers and building a brand, improving efficiency, reducing costs, improving the quality and quantity of peopleâ€™s work and removing constraints.

WEEK 42

DEEP DIVE PROTOTYPING

Developing creative, practical solutions

Developed and popularized by the consultancy firm IDEO, Deep Dive Prototyping is a focused, team-based approach to generating solutions to a particular problem or challenge. It is a useful way of stimulating creative thinking and to capture and fine-tune ideas.

The process

A deep dive combines brainstorming and prototyping (building and exploring a potential solution) to devise actions that will help move a business forward. There is no time limit, and the main stages are:

â€¢ Build a team that has a mix of strengths and approaches.â€¢ Define the design challenge â€“ to do this, understand your market, customers, technology and constraints and use this information to develop key themes.â€¢ Visit experts, and gather information on markets, customers â€“ and ideas generally.â€¢ Share ideas.â€¢ Brainstorm and vote â€“ this involves intensive brainstorming and discussion to imagine new concepts and ideas based around the main themes.â€¢ Develop a fast prototype.â€¢ Test and refine the prototype, streamlining ideas to improve the proto-type and to overcome obstacles â€“ at this stage, evaluate and prioritize ideas and decide how they can be implemented.â€¢ Focus on the prototype and produce a final solution.â€¢ Give credit to those involved â€“ this promotes motivation and encourages continued innovative thinking.WEEK 43

DEVELOPING CREATIVE THINKINGMaking creativity the norm

Edward de Bono sees creativity as a learnable skill, one that is best harnessed through formal techniques. He proposes that parallel thinking is a more useful and effective means of putting creative talent to work.

Formal creativity works because it works with the way everyoneâ€™s brains work: both consciously and subconsciously, we automatically filter, categorize, process and organize information. Building on this, de Bono argues that parallel thinking is more effective for generating the results that make a difference to companies. (Parallel thinking is when each individual puts forward their own thoughts in parallel with those of others. In this way, each individual is able to complement, enrich and build on one anotherâ€™s thinking, rather than competing or attacking the thoughts of others.)

The reason why this is more important than ever is because what companies previously relied on for competitive advantage â€“ competence, information and technology â€“ are now easy-to-obtain commodities. These are all buyable commodities, enabling your competitors to rapidly erode any advantage you may have had. Today, what matters is creating value from these commodities.

Understanding creativity

Creativity solves problems, challenges existing methods, and provides a better and constantly improving way forward. Given the reward, companies need to know how best to harness creativity in a way that is useful. A major flaw in traditional brainstorming is that it assumes that, if you give people the freedom to express themselves, they will magically become creative. This is not the case. For organizations, useful creativity needs to be a formal activity that requires thinking that provokes and challenges a current situation and then searches for answers.

Provoke, challenge and search for solutions

Given the brainâ€™s natural inclination to organize information and think laterally, we can tackle issues by simply taking a random starting point. Our brains will automatically process information, make connections and point us in new directions. Allowing such randomness in selecting a starting point is important. It suggests new possibilities and takes thinking along new paths. Significantly, it is likely that our brains have already processed information and are subconsciously suggesting such opening gambits because they could be highly relevant. This serves to break us out of the current doldrums and set us on a new course.

Next, our new thinking needs to move forward: to challenge the information it is processing. Just because something has always been done a certain way does not mean it is carved in stone: methods can always be improved upon. Constantly questioning and challenging is a mindset that is a huge source of competitive advantage precisely because it is the way that companies create value from their resources. An important point to remember is that even when something seems to be working and is successful it doesnâ€™t mean it is the best that it can be. Once thinking challenges the norm, we will automatically explore alternative and potentially better solutions.

Creating a culture of creativity in a world where competence, knowledge and technology are no longer enough is now the true source of success.

WEEK 44

THE DISCOVERY CYCLE (ORCA)Evaluating innovations

Discovery â€“ making things known or visible â€“ is a vital precursor for innovation. The Discovery Cycle is a way of choosing new ideas that are profitable and scalable.

The Discovery Cycle has four stages, summarized in the acronym ORCA:

  1. Observation. Understand how the world is changing â€“ for example, by looking for anomalies, paradoxes, peripheral developments and direct experience.
  2. Reflection. Techniques that work best at this stage include zooming in and out, using a muse, suspending judgement, slowing down, reflecting on whatâ€™s missing, restructuring data to simplify patterns, juxtaposing pieces of different information (bisociation) and taking time to rest.
  3. Conversation. People set the pace and scope for innovation, so the best techniques to use at this stage include contrasting views, setting the agenda, framing the issues and generating hypotheses.
  4. Analysis. The final stage of the Discovery Cycle involves gathering systematic evidence, classifying and categorizing data, naming, completing data analysis and hypothesizing.

Lessons from great innovators

What lessons do innovators have for us? Several come to mind:â€¢ Build on the ideas of others / collaborate. That should be easy for scientists who are, in the words of Isaac Newton, â€˜standing on the shoulders of giantsâ€¢ Take an unorthodox, distinctive approach.â€¢ Embrace diversity.â€¢ Create a diverse, open and creative culture.â€¢ Develop empathy for the consumer or customer (understand people).â€¢ Execute and practically take action.â€¢ Be confident and bold.â€¢ Find your motivation; enjoy your work.

This list also highlights three other vital points:

  1. Innovation relies on teamwork, agility and the ability to lead change, the other elements of this programme.
  2. Innovation is about mindset: you need to think like an innovator and you need to encourage that in others.
  3. Innovation isnâ€™t only about products: it is about improving efficiency, reducing costs, improving the quality and quantity of peopleâ€™s work, removing constraints â€“ and thatâ€™s just internally; it also means serving and understanding customers, building a brand â€“ and more.

WEEK 45

THE FORTUNE AT THE BOTTOM OF THE PYRAMID (BOP)Developing the innovatorâ€™s mindset

If a company goes to the bottom of the wealth pyramid and builds affordable products, creates awareness and provides access, then the market is phenomenal.

The late Professor C.K. Prahalad argued that there is a â€˜poverty penaltyâ€™ where the poorest people pay more for everything because they donâ€™t have a choice: they are stuck with local monopolies and bad products and services.

Research recently highlighted by the World Resources Institute shows that the worldâ€™s four billion poorest people represent a US$5 trillion market opportunity. There are several other issues at the bottom of the pyramid: eâ€¢ Pricing is vital. At the BOP, you need to start with an affordable price, understanding that price minus profit equals the acceptable level of cost. This different way of thinking leads to a new range of exciting options.â€¢ Innovation is essential. This can be accelerated and improved by focusing on BOP markets because minor, incremental changes wonâ€™t be enough: the market requires a fundamental rethink.â€¢ Businesses need to substitute investment for collaboration. Management time is needed to increase collaboration â€“ and it is cheaper than simply in-vesting cash.

Companies that ignore growth markets will be left behind â€“ and will have five years, at best, before businesses from growth markets start competing with them.

Developing the innovatorâ€™s mindset

Where can you improve your approach to innovation? Ask yourself the following questions and mark yourself out of 10 for each attribute: this will help highlight areas for improvement.

When innovating, how effectively do you:â€¢ engage as many people as possible â€¦?â€¢ â€¦ and build an open, diverse and positive team?â€¢ define the specific challenge or issue?â€¢ challenge assumptions: yours and other peopleâ€™s?â€¢ confront challenges and problems?â€¢ understand that good ideas can come from anywhere?â€¢ follow through â€“ by being practical and realistic, and planning implementation?â€¢ focus on the benefits as well as the potential pitfalls?â€¢ question? Questioning is a great way both to provide support (e.g. what help do you need?) and challenge (how can we do this faster/cheaper?)â€¢ give praise and credit: build momentum (revolutions fail, flywheels succeed)?â€¢ be open, build relationships?â€¢ remove constraints, tirelessly?â€¢ remember the essentials of leading change? (See Number 35.)â€¢ balance intuition and analysis?â€¢ build collaboration and teamwork? (Think of the 5Ms: meaning, mindset, measurement, mobilizing, mechanisms for renewal.)â€¢ avoid the pitfalls of decision-making? See the description of inhibitors below which ones are your greatest vulnerability?)â€¢ consciously develop your skills?â€¢ design matters? (This affects how people feel about something: whether itâ€™s credible, engaging, worthwhile.)

WEEK 46

THE SIX THINKING HATSIf you want to get ahead, get a hat

Created by Edward de Bono, the Six Thinking Hats technique explains the different styles of thinking we use while making decisions.

Overview

People usually have a preferred thinking style. While this style may be useful, it can limit thinking and cause us to miss better solutions. The Six Thinking Hats method allows us to deliberately switch thinking styles so that problems are viewed from multiple perspectives. This leads to better, more balanced decisions.

Using only one approach, such as logical thinking, may ignore emotional reactions, risks, or creative possibilities. Applying multiple hats gives a fuller picture and improves both decision-making and implementation.

The Six Thinking Hats

White HatFocuses on facts, data, and information.Looks at what information is available and what is missing.Analyzes trends, evidence, and objective data.

Red HatUses intuition, emotions, and gut feelings.Considers how people may emotionally react to a decision.Allows expression of feelings without justification.

Black HatLooks at risks, problems, and weaknesses.Asks why an idea might fail.Helps identify dangers and prepare contingency plans.

Yellow HatFocuses on optimism and benefits.Looks at value, advantages, and positive outcomes.Helps maintain motivation during challenges.

Green HatEncourages creativity and new ideas.Explores alternatives and innovative solutions.Free-flowing thinking with minimal criticism.

Blue HatFocuses on control, structure, and process.Commonly used by facilitators or leaders.Manages discussion flow and ensures productive thinking.

WEEK 47

INNOVATION CULTUREPeter Druckerâ€™s seven steps for developing a creative culture

Innovation is not limited to a few individuals or departments. It is a company-wide activity that requires the right mindset, culture, and systems. Profitable ideas can come from anyone, anywhere, at any time.

Where does innovation come from?

Innovation emerges when people are encouraged to question, think critically, and share ideas openly. It is not only about big breakthroughs; small, incremental improvements also create strong competitive advantages. Innovation does not depend solely on large research budgets. It is embedded in everyday thinking, operations, and decision-making.

Creating an innovative organization

Innovative organizations differ because they actively support creativity. They encourage questioning, challenge assumptions, and adapt systems to support innovation. They also collaborate with external experts to strengthen internal capabilities.

Peter Druckerâ€™s seven steps to promote innovation

  1. Analyze unexpected successes and understand why they worked.
  2. Examine situations where outcomes differed from expectations.
  3. Challenge accepted underperformance and question why it is tolerated.
  4. Identify and take advantage of changes in the market.
  5. Observe social changes to uncover new opportunities.
  6. Monitor economic changes and recognize business possibilities.
  7. Understand how new ideas, information, and technology affect customers.

Five catalysts for successful innovation

ConsciousnessPeople understand organizational goals and believe they can contribute.

MultiplicityTeams include diverse skills, backgrounds, experiences, and ideas.

ConnectivityStrong, trusting relationships are encouraged across teams and functions.

AccessibilityPeople have access to resources, time, leaders, and decision-makers.

ConsistencyInnovation is embedded in leadership style, systems, and daily practices.

To build a strong innovation culture, reflect on which of these catalysts need improvement and identify clear actions to strengthen them.WEEK 48

DISNEYâ€™S CREATIVITY STRATEGYWhen you need more than just the bare necessities

We all have a preferred thinking style â€“ some of us are dreamers, while others are realists or critics. This can prevent us seeing an issue from other angles. Walt Disneyâ€™s method uses all three of these thinking styles to help view a situation from different perspectives and find the best way forward.

Problem solving, decision-making and planning suffer when we have too narrow a focus, yet it can be difficult to change how we naturally approach issues. Using Disneyâ€™s three styles together will improve your decision-making.

â€¢The Dreamer, who is a dreamer, is focused on potential and possibilities.â€¢The Realist focuses on practical aspects and implementation.â€¢The Critic questions and challenges plans and assumptions, and notices potential problems or flaws.

Using the Disney method

  1. Select an issue you want to address but put it to one side while you get into the right frame of mind.
  2. Go to three different places to think about the issue from each perspective (you will associate each environment with that approach). These can be entirely different places or simply different parts of one room.
  3. For each way of thinking (starting with dreamer, moving to realistic and then to critic), first remember a time when you were either creative, realistic or critical. This will help you access that style and apply it to the current situation.
  4. In each frame of mind, address the issue at hand solely from that perspective. This will let you get the most out of each perspective, revealing more options and ideas.

â€¢ In the dreamer space, let your ideas flow freely.â€¢ In the realist space, think about how the ideas you have created can be implemented. How can they be achieved? What needs to happen?â€¢ In the critic space, question and challenge your ideas and plan. Identify strengths and weaknesses; look for flaws; look for gaps or potential problems. Determine what needs to be done better.

  1. Once you have completed these four stages, go back to the beginning and re-evaluate your original dream and plan through each thinking stage in turn. You can repeat this process until you feel the plan works well from each perspective.

Types of questions to ask at each stage

Dreamerâ€¢ Why am I doing this?â€¢ Can it be done better?â€¢ What would I like to happen?â€¢ Wouldnâ€™t it be great ifâ€¦..?â€¢ What reward or result would I like?

Realistâ€¢ How can I make that happen?â€¢ Who else do I need to make it work?â€¢ What needs to happen â€“ and when?â€¢ What resources do I need?â€¢ How much will it cost?

CriticDoes the idea really have potential?Is the objective achievable?Are there any barriers or resource issues?Does the plan work? Consider issues such as timing, cost or market potential.How can the plan be improved â€“ are there gaps or are some things unnecessary?

WEEK 49

THE MATE MODEL FOR STRATEGIC SELLINGAchieving your sales objectives

Segmenting and managing your contacts within a client organization in terms of their support for your sales objectives is a highly effective way of developing client relationships and selling.

Four steps

â€¢Step 1: define your unique sales objective.Be clear about what you are selling and when, and the value it brings. What makes it an attractive proposition? What is its value for the organization or client? This sounds simple but it can be muddled or overlooked, with disastrous consequences.

â€¢Step 2: identify all the players using the MATE model.MATE highlights the need to focus on Money, Allies, Technical experts and End users. Identify each contact (including those you donâ€™t know), recording their job title and name.

MoneyThey have the ultimate veto on sales

AlliesThey provide useful information, can guide you and influence others to support your objective

Technical experts/assessorsThey filter out information, can be gatekeepers, can influence â€˜Moneyâ€™

End usersThey use, manage or work with your products

â€¢Money. The budget holder has authority over the decision to spend. They tend to focus on the bottom line and have the power of veto. They will ask: â€˜What impact will this have and what return will we get?â€™â€¢Allies /Advocates. These can help guide you during the sales process. They provide valuable information, can lead you to the right people and may be influential. Allies are both inside and outside the organization.â€¢Technical experts. They are gatekeepers who evaluate technical aspects of the proposal. They do not have final approval but offer recommendations to the decision-maker. They can say â€˜noâ€™ on account of technical issues. They ask whether the product or service matches their specifications.â€¢End users. They judge the impact of your proposal on their job performance. They will implement or work with your solution, so their success is linked to your product and they will want to influence the decision to buy. They ask: Will it work for me or my department?

â€¢Step 3: consider each individualâ€™s level of support.Having placed each individual on the MATE model, assess their level of support for your sales objective as high, medium or low.

â€¢Step 4: consider each individualâ€™s level of influence.Assess each individualâ€™s influence within their organization â€” high, medium or low.

Check for warning signs

Ensure that there are no threats to the sale by asking yourself the following:â€¢ Have I at least one person for each area?â€¢ Am I free from concerns about their influence?â€¢ Have I made personal contact with them?â€¢ Do I know their response modes and what they are looking for?

Identify your tactics to further the sale and eliminate warning signs

Throughout, be honest and prepared to challenge and develop your thinking. With the information you have gathered, contact the key people, establish rapport and understand their needs.

WEEK 50

THE TEN Cs OF SELLING ONLINE

Building a successful business online

The Ten Cs focus on meeting customer needs and are the key drivers of success in online business. The importance of each factor may change depending on the companyâ€™s stage of growth, competition, market type, and brand strength.

1. Content

Content defines your brand and sets the tone of your website.It should be clear, engaging, informative, visually appealing, and tailored to the target audience.Customers should be able to access information quickly and control how much they view.

2. Communication

Communication is not just about sharing information.It is about listening, building trust, and creating one-to-one relationships.Encourage interaction, collect feedback, and study customer behaviour using data like click patterns.

3. Customer Care

Trust is essential in online selling.Customers must feel confident about payments, data security, and after-sales support.Provide multiple payment options, order tracking, and fast responses to queries.Good customer care leads to repeat purchases, referrals, and higher sales.

4. Community and Culture

People use the internet to connect and socialize.Create spaces where customers can interact, share views, and access expert content.Information should be clear, useful, and engaging to build loyalty and belonging.

5. Convenience

Customers expect ease and speed.Navigation should be simple, intuitive, and user-friendly.Every feature should be evaluated from the customerâ€™s point of view.

6. Connectivity

A website should be engaging enough to make customers return.High-quality content, incentives, and useful links help retain visitors.Linking to complementary sites adds value and improves customer experience.

7. Cost and Profitability

Your online strategy should clearly define objectives and benefits.Control costs and focus on profitability to ensure long-term sustainability.

8. Customization

Customization should be planned from the beginning.Allow customers to choose options through clear communication.Refine customization continuously to stay competitive.

9. Capability

Employees must see the internet as a tool to serve customers better.Set goals, measure performance, and improve systems regularly.Actively seek customer opinions to enhance services.

10. Competitiveness

Continuously track competitors and market trends.Understand what competitors are doing and anticipate future moves.Plan for worst-case scenarios to make your online strategy strong and durable.

Colonel (Dr) JC John

Founder & Chief Mentor â€“ Oversimplify - Mentoria

Emotional Preparation for the Interview â€“ Speak to Yourself

  • The worst-case scenario is not getting hired.
  • I have nothing to lose and everything to gain.
  • This is practice, and I will learn from it.
  • This is one of many opportunities, and I will use it to grow.
  • I will be honest and frank.
  • I will be patient.
  • I will stay calm and not get stressed.
  • I will remain positive and hopeful until the end.
  • I will not speak negatively about my present or previous employer.

Researching the Company

Researching the company is one of the most important steps before an interview. It helps you answer confidently and shows genuine interest in the organization.

  • Read the company website carefully.
  • Understand their products, services, and values.
  • Learn about recent achievements and news.
  • Understand the job description clearly.

Interview Process â€“ 4 Stages

  1. Application Review: Employers review resumes and cover letters to shortlist candidates.
  2. Initial Screening: A phone or video interview to assess basic qualifications.
  3. In-Person Interview: Meeting with managers or a panel to discuss experience and skills.
  4. Follow-Up: Feedback, further rounds, or a job offer.

Qualities Interviewers Look For

  • Focused, enthusiastic, confident, and to the point
  • Passionate and ambitious
  • A team player
  • Energetic and mature
  • Emotionally stable and adaptable to company culture

Important Points:

  • First impressions matter.
  • The most qualified person may not always get hired â€” others also have a chance.
  • Read the job description carefully.
  • Maintain eye contact and speak confidently.
  • Be honest and highlight strengths with examples (leadership, motivation, flexibility, decision-making, problem-solving, loyalty, integrity, creativity).

IMPORTANT

  • Describe your personality honestly and explain why the job excites you.
  • Do not criticize your previous company.
  • Do not disguise a weakness as a strength (e.g., â€œI am a perfectionistâ€).
  • Mention a real weakness and explain how you are improving it.

HOW TO DRESS

  • Males: Formal suit (coat and tie).
  • Females: Formal suit or saree with sober makeup.
  • Use light deodorant or perfume.
  • Carry: Folder, pen, highlighter, certificates, CV, visiting card.
  • Keep your mobile phone switched off.
  • Reach 15 minutes early.

HOW TO HOLD THE INTERVIEWERâ€™S ATTENTION

Attention Levels:

  • 0â€“10 seconds: 100% attention
  • 10â€“60 seconds: Drops to 50%
  • 60â€“90 seconds: Drops to 10% (if no interaction)

Keep answers clear and structured.Ask relevant questions to maintain two-way communication.Avoid long, boring responses.

Other Important Questions to Prepare

  • Tell me about yourself.
  • Why do you want to do an MBA?
  • Where do you see yourself in 10 years?
  • What are your ambitions?
  • Who is your role model and why?
  • What field do you want to choose and why?
  • What are the qualities of a good leader?
  • What if you are not selected?
  • Give an example of a successful project.
  • How do you handle underperforming employees?
  • Where do you see the industry going?
  • What motivates you more â€” pay, growth, recognition, or environment?
  • Do you want to ask us something?

Examples of Questions You May Ask

  • What position are you considering me for?
  • What are the main challenges in this role?
  • What qualities make someone successful in your company?

SAMPLE ANSWERS (Improved & Structured)

1. Tell Me About Yourself

â€œI am currently a Senior Executive in Accounts with experience in taxation and auditing. I have conducted internal audits for ISO 9000 and resolved tax issues for the past two years. I have worked in the construction and media industries. My background includes roles as Junior Accountant, Senior Accountant, and Senior Auditor. I hold a CA qualification and an M.Com degree. My colleagues describe me as results-focused and detail-oriented. I have received the â€˜Employee of the Yearâ€™ and â€˜Best Suggestion Award.â€™â€

2. Why Do You Want to Leave Your Previous Organization?

â€œMy company merged with another firm, and the new management decided to bring in their own team. Before the merger, I consistently received positive performance reviews. I am happy to provide references to verify my performance.â€

3. Why Do You Want to Do an MBA?

â€œI come from an army background, and I want to gain knowledge in marketing and finance to start my own business in the future. I admire entrepreneurs like Kiran Majumdar Shaw, who built a successful biotech company. I want to learn how to manage and grow a business effectively.â€

4. Where Do You See Yourself in 10 Years?

â€œI see myself gaining experience in marketing and finance, learning how businesses operate, and eventually becoming a successful entrepreneur leading a strong team.â€

5. What Are the Qualities of a Good Leader?

â€œA good leader is a good listener, patient, and not self-centered. They motivate others, understand human behavior, and balance compassion with discipline. Strong professional knowledge and integrity are essential.â€

6. What If You Do Not Get Selected?

â€œIf I am not selected, I will treat it as a learning experience. I will continue improving my skills and apply again with better preparation.â€

Group Discussion

Again, Watch These Short Videos Before You Start

Group Discussion Strategy

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BguYUJ7cWrs

Group Discussion Tactics (Funny Video in Hindi)

https://youtu.be/QqGOUFVfoWI?si=Anh8k2omL4vnWiFX

Personality Traits Gauged in a Group Discussion

  • Ability to interact in a team
  • Communication skills
  • Reasoning ability
  • Leadership skills
  • Initiative & enthusiasm
  • Assertiveness
  • Flexibility
  • Nurturing & coaching ability
  • Creativity
  • Ability to think on oneâ€™s feet

HOW TO DO WELL IN A GROUP DISCUSSION

  • Try to grab the opportunity to speak first and introduce the topic.Example Opening (5 Sentences):â€œGood morning friends. The topic given to us is ______. This has been the center of discussion in many forums and in the media. This topic is very important in our lives, and I am glad we are discussing it today. In my view, I believe ______. Now let us hear the views of others.â€
  • Listen carefully and look for the right opportunity to speak (do not interrupt too often).
  • Agree with others and add examples or experiences.
  • Disagree politely and support your view with logic.
  • Present both sides of the issue.
  • Intervene if the discussion becomes aggressive.
  • Cooperate and show leadership.
  • Do not corner or make fun of participants.
  • Encourage quiet members to speak.
  • Use examples and real experiences.
  • If you did not start, try to conclude the discussion.

Starting a GD â€“ Introducing the Topic

â€œFriends, the topic given to us is ______. This is one of the most discussed topics.â€

OR

â€œThis is a controversial topic and has been widely debated in the media and public forums. As every issue has two sides, this topic will also have different viewpoints. Our aim should be to reach a balanced view. The way I understand this topic is ______. Some may say ______, while others may say ______. Let us proceed.â€

Intervening When the Discussion Goes Out of Control

â€œFriends, I think we need to control this discussion. Otherwise, everyone will speak, and no one will listen. Let us speak one by one. Let us also hear from those who have not yet spoken. I am fine speaking at the end.â€

Concluding the Discussion

â€œFor the past 10â€“15 minutes, we have had a very interesting discussion. We have heard diverse views. The overall consensus appears to be ______. However, we must consider the points raised by others as well. Therefore, we can conclude that:

  1. ______â€

Self-Learning UsingChatGPT â€“ Your Knowledge Assistant

ChatGPT is one of the easiest tools to use. The key is knowing how to ask the right question (Prompt Engineering).

It can:

  • Write emails
  • Summarize long essays
  • Create presentations
  • Teach subjects
  • Generate sales leads
  • Write software code
  • And much more

It is a must-learn tool for students and employees.

AI is now integrated into search engines like Google, Bing, and Microsoft applications such as Excel, Word, and Outlook.

Easy Way to Start:Download Bing on your mobile phone and start asking questions by voice.

Optimizing Questions (Prompt Engineering)

  • Assign a role: â€œYou are an expert web developer.â€
  • Give a clear task: â€œDevelop an e-commerce website to export handicrafts.â€
  • Set format: â€œUse simple language and bullet points.â€
  • Limit length: â€œRespond within 500 words.â€

Types of Prompts

  • Chain-of-thought prompts
  • Role-play prompts
  • Conversational prompts
  • Prompt priming (provide context)
  • Avoid bias and use neutral language

Examples of Prompts

  1. Create a banner for an online summer sale with a beach background and the text â€œSummer Sale â€“ Up to 50% Off.â€
  2. As a CRM executive, help a customer who cannot log into a website.
  3. Write a Python function that returns the sum of all even numbers in a list.

Free AI Tools to Improve Productivity

  • Bing
  • Grok
  • ChatGPT
  • DeepSeek
  • Gemini
  • Copilot
  • DALL·E

Other Tools:

  • 12ft.io (http://12ft.io/) â€“ Summarize large articles
  • Yoodli.ai â€“ Improve communication skills
  • SignalHire.com â€“ Lead generation
  • GPTSlides.com â€“ Create presentations
  • Formula.dog â€“ Generate Excel formulas and code
  • Writesonic â€“ Content writing
  • Midjourney â€“ Generate art
  • Replit â€“ Coding
  • Synthesia â€“ Video creation
  • Soundraw â€“ Music creation
  • Fliki â€“ TikTok generation
  • Starrytars â€“ Avatars
  • SlidesAI â€“ PPT generation
  • Remini â€“ Edit pictures
  • Pictory â€“ Edit videos
  • Wordtune â€“ Summarize notes

Public Speaking & Soft Skills

Watch These Videos Before Starting the Course

  • How to Overcome Fear of Public Speakinghttps://youtu.be/80UVjkcxGmA?si=Cqo1w3-cVWnjuBa5
  • What Well-Spoken Words Can Dohttps://youtu.be/Iqq1roF4C8s?si=PLhufNvIvye6hIdC
  • Why Public Speaking Is Importanthttps://youtu.be/cmwTl7LXAR8?si=Teg7uXFTaY1YdZ_d
  • How to Speak Fluentlyhttps://youtu.be/VVhuLMELEcY?si=vzc676KQ142G9Prs
  • Behavioral Skills Traininghttps://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PUTbDB6etVs

Confidence-Building Exercises

  1. Call out to a person 200 meters away.
  2. Announce loudly: â€œFactory closed due to heavy rains.â€
  3. Read aloud while your partner writes facing away.
  4. Dictate from 15 feet away.
  5. Dictate to the whole class.
  6. Read an essay to the class.
  7. Deliver a lecture to 1 person.
  8. Deliver a lecture to 10 people.
  9. Give an extempore speech.
  10. Deliver a motivational talk.
  11. Introduce a guest speaker.
  12. Handle a difficult negotiation.
  13. Conduct a group discussion.
  14. Moderate a brainstorming session.
  15. Participate in a panel interview.
  16. Give a farewell speech.
  17. Address your department for the first time.

Master Public Speaking with Videos & Practice

Public speaking is about:

  • Connecting with your audience
  • Communicating clearly
  • Leaving a strong impression

Why It Matters:

  • Career growth
  • Confidence building
  • Influence & persuasion
  • Networking opportunities

Practical Tips:

  • Know your audience
  • Organize your content (Introductionâ€“Bodyâ€“Conclusion)
  • Practice regularly
  • Manage anxiety with deep breathing
  • Use simple visual aids

Final Thoughts

Public speaking and soft skills are lifelong skills.Practice consistently.Use videos for learning and exercises for application.Stay open to feedback and continuous improvement.

With dedication and the right guidance, you can become a confident speaker, strong leader, and effective professional.

ADVICE BEFORE STARTING PUBLIC SPEAKING TRAINING

Watch This Video on the Necessity of Public Speaking

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Cjs7dyzLVco

Public speaking is one of the most important skills to rise in your career.

MENTAL PREPARATION BEFORE YOU START

  • FIRST THING â€“ Stop finding ways to escape.
  • NO HURRY â€“ TAKE TWO WEEKS. Before starting, you must prepare yourself mentally. Take your time.
  • DECIDE ONCE AND FOR ALL. You cannot avoid learning this skill. Avoiding it can cost your entire career.
  • Go into a room alone and speak loudly to yourself. Force your mind to believe: YOU CAN DO IT.
  • You do not need perfect English to be a good speaker.
  • No one is born a great speaker. All great speakers worked hard.
  • If you can survive the first two minutes on stage, you will survive the entire speech.
  • Never get on stage unprepared. Practice, Practice, Practice.

FIRST STEPS BEFORE GOING ON STAGE

STEP 1

Start by telling a bedtime story to a child in your mother tongue. Then tell the same story in English.

STEP 2

Practice in front of friends or family:

  • First in your mother tongue.
  • Then in English.
  • Sit on chairs with your script in hand.
  • Speak casually to one person at a time.
  • Pretend to be confident.

You may panic and forget. If that happens:

  • Take a deep breath.
  • Smile.
  • Ask a question to buy time.
  • Ask someone to summarize what they understood.
  • Look at your script confidently and continue.

STEP 3

Rewrite your script in your own words.Use short sentences and familiar words. Add a story or experience.

STEP 4

Highlight key words in yellow.Write those key words on a small card and pin it to your script.

STEP 5

Memorize the first 20 lines.

STEP 6

Rehearse at least 7 times in front of a mirror.

STEP 7

Reach the venue 30 minutes early.Mentally adjust to the environment.Carry a spare copy of your opening.

BEST WAYS TO OPEN A SPEECH (First 60 Seconds Are Critical)

Your opening must be clear, catchy, and impactful.

You can start with:

  • A question
  • A news incident
  • A startling statement
  • A quote
  • A human-interest story
  • An elevator pitch

Always tell the audience:

  • What you are going to speak about
  • How long you will take

Use facts, analogies, statistics, and opinions (with reasons).

HOW TO START YOUR PRESENTATION

  • Walk confidently to the podium.
  • Place your script on it.
  • Keep your keyword card in your pocket.
  • Wait for the audience to settle.
  • Speak the first 3 sentences.
  • Take a deep breath.

Three Things Will Definitely Happen:

  1. You may forget your script.
  2. Your heart may pound.
  3. You may panic.

What To Do:

  • Take a deep breath.
  • Look at your keyword card confidently.
  • Drink water if needed.
  • Restart calmly.
  • If needed, read from your script.
  • Confidence usually returns within 30 seconds.

DOs & DONâ€™Ts

DOs

  • Maintain eye contact with one person at a time.
  • Use short, simple sentences.
  • Speak clearly and loudly (do not shout).
  • Face the audience while speaking.
  • Use your natural accent.

DONâ€™Ts

  • Do not insult or embarrass anyone.
  • Do not speak too fast.
  • Do not read continuously.
  • Do not apologize unnecessarily.
  • Do not show relief after finishing.
  • Do not use very big words to impress.
  • Avoid filler words, mumbling, and looking down.
  • Do not exceed your time limit.

SURVIVAL KIT WHEN YOUR MIND GOES BLANK

  • Take a deep breath and smile.
  • Look at your notes confidently.
  • Ask the audience a question.
  • Share a small story or experience.

EXERCISES TO IMPROVE PUBLIC SPEAKING

  • Call out loudly to someone 200 meters away.
  • Announce loudly in a factory setting.
  • Give dictation from 15 feet away.
  • Deliver a prepared lecture.
  • Give an extempore speech.
  • Conduct a group discussion.
  • Moderate a brainstorming session.
  • Participate in a panel interview.
  • Practice negotiation skills.
  • Give a farewell speech.
  • Address your department for the first time.

(Practice regularly to build confidence.)

KEY POINTS FOR PRESENTATIONS

  1. Choose not more than 5 key points.
  2. Repeat and emphasize your key ideas.
  3. Transition smoothly between points.
  4. Use facts, analogies, and statistics.
  5. Talk instead of reading.
  6. Move naturally and maintain eye contact.

VISUAL AIDS RULES

  • Use bullet points only (no full sentences).
  • Follow the 6Ã—6 rule (max 6 lines per slide, 6 words per line).
  • Use limited colors (max 3).
  • Ensure good contrast and large fonts.
  • Do not depend completely on PowerPoint.
  • Never write â€œThank Youâ€ on the slide â€” say it instead.

DRESSING UP

  • Dress according to the occasion.
  • When unsure, dress conservatively.
  • Keep hair neat.
  • Avoid excessive jewelry.
  • Maintain proper grooming.

GESTURES

  • Keep gestures natural.
  • Avoid hands in pockets.
  • Avoid exaggerated movements.
  • Do not clasp hands tightly.

HANDLING QUESTIONS

  1. Let the person ask the question fully.
  2. Rephrase the question.
  3. Repeat it for the audience.
  4. Invite opinions from 2â€“3 people.
  5. Consolidate answers and give your view.
  6. Ask if the questioner is satisfied.

Thumb Rule:Answer one person, but speak for the whole audience.

CLOSING THE SPEECH

  • Indicate you are concluding.
  • Summarize key points.
  • Invite questions.
  • End on a positive and friendly note.
  • Leave the stage calmly.

Must-Do Courses / Self-Study

  • Toastmasters Club (Public Speaking)
  • Train the Trainer
  • Microsoft Project
  • Law & Compliance
  • World History
  • Geography
  • Physics
  • Mathematics

Competitive Exams

  • CAT
  • GRE
  • IAS, IFS, IRS, IPS
  • UPSC
  • CDS (Army, Navy, Air Force)
  • Bank Officer Exams

OBJECTIVE TYPE QUESTIONS â€“ Negative Marking Strategy

Type 1:

Correct +1 | Wrong â€“1â†’ Answer only if fully sure.

Type 2:

Correct +1 | Wrong â€“0.5â†’ Answer if you can eliminate 2 options.

Type 3:

Correct +1 | Wrong â€“0.25â†’ Answer if you can eliminate 1 option.

Type 4:

No negative markingâ†’ Attempt all questions.

Assumption Strategy Example

  • Sure about 50 questions.
  • Can eliminate 1 wrong option in 34 questions.
  • Can eliminate 2 wrong options in 16 questions.

Use elimination wisely to maximize marks.

ESSAY TYPE PAPER

Time Management Strategy

Divide the total time by the total marks to calculate the number of minutes you can spend per mark. Then multiply that by the marks allotted to each question. Spend only that calculated time on that particular question.

NOTE

The basic aim is NOT to leave any question unanswered. Even if you are unsure, write something (minimum 10 lines).

Recommended Answer Format

CENTER HEADING

Write the main topic in the center of the page.

GROUP HEADINGS

These are headings like:

  • Introduction
  • Factors to be Considered
  • Effects of Factors
  • Conclusion

Rules:

  • Do not put a full stop after Group Headings.
  • Start writing on the next line.

If there are multiple factors, list them under Para Headings as follows:

3. Cause 1This is described in sentences starting on the same line.

4. Cause 2Writing starts on the next line.

If there are multiple factors under each cause, list them under Sub Para Headings:

c. Sub Para HeadingExplanation begins on the next line.

d. Effect of Cause 2

iii. Sub-Sub Para Headingiv. Result 2 is Severe Poverty

NOTE

All levels of headings (Group Headings, Para Headings, Sub Para Headings, Sub-Sub Para Headings) must be bold and underlined in the exam.

Leadership

The best way to understand the basics of leadership is to study multiple definitions.

Useful Videos

Leadership according to Dr. A.P.J. Abdul Kalamhttps://youtube.com/shorts/mvDVooJ-v44?si=jyN_A6jgPwC16cl-

https://www.instagram.com/reel/DOpIOxYDo1j/?igsh=ZGtvczBzdGVqbjU=

How to Handle Pressurehttps://www.instagram.com/reel/DN9Q8lKjrHo/?igsh=MW5uNm4yNTJjdG1idA==https://www.instagram.com/reel/DMacOt0x09H/?igsh=NzhvY2dsMHByYjhi

Best of Sourav Ganguly on Leadershiphttps://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fTdkJTXAAjw

Definitions of Leadership

Leadership is the process of influencing the thinking, behavior, and efforts of team members toward the achievement of organizational goals.

Leadership is a winning combination of personal traits and the ability to think and act as a leader, directing the activities of others for the good of all.

â€œLeadership is the art of getting someone else to do something you want done because he wants to do it.â€ â€“ Dwight D. Eisenhower

â€œA leader has the vision and conviction that a dream can be achieved. He inspires the power and energy to get it done.â€ â€“ Ralph Lauren

â€œThe best leaders surround themselves with assistants smarter than they are.â€ â€“ Amos Parrish

â€œReason and judgment are the qualities of a leader.â€ â€“ Tacitus

100 Answers to the Question: What Is Leadership?

Leadership is difficult to define because it means different things to different people. Here are 100 definitions:

  1. A leader is best when people barely know he exists. â€“ Lao Tzu
  2. A good leader takes more blame and less credit. â€“ Arnold Glasow
  3. Leadership shows in times of challenge. â€“ Martin Luther King Jr.
  4. You donâ€™t need a title to be a leader. â€“ Mark Sanborn
  5. Lead from behind and take responsibility in danger. â€“ Nelson Mandela
  6. Leadership and learning go together. â€“ John F. Kennedy
  7. A leader gets people to do great things. â€“ Ronald Reagan
  8. Leadership is leading with heart and empathy. â€“ Bill George
  9. Leadership elicits greatness already present. â€“ John Buchan
  10. A great leader attracts great people. â€“ Goethe
  11. Right leadership makes people follow till the end. â€“ Harold Seymour
  12. Leaders must have stamina and self-reliance. â€“ Thomas Cronin
  13. Leadership rallies people to a common purpose. â€“ Bernard Montgomery
  14. Leadership confronts anxiety of people. â€“ Galbraith
  15. Leadership translates vision into reality. â€“ Warren Bennis
  16. Leadership defines the future and inspires action. â€“ John Kotter
  17. Leadership produces more leaders. â€“ Ralph Nader
  18. Leadership is integrity in action. â€“ Scott Berkun
  19. Leadership influences through passion and purpose. â€“ Myles Munroe
  20. Leadership unlocks potential. â€“ Bill Bradley
  21. Leadership is saying no wisely. â€“ Tony Blair
  22. Leadership is defined by results. â€“ Peter Drucker
  23. Leadership is measured by quality of followers. â€“ Dennis Peer
  24. Innovation distinguishes leader from follower. â€“ Steve Jobs
  25. Leadership develops others to full potential. â€“ Bob Mason
  26. Leadership is influencing willingly. â€“ Eisenhower
  27. Leadership requires clear vision. â€“ Theodore Hesburgh
  28. Leadership mobilizes others for shared goals. â€“ Kouzes & Posner
  29. A great leader takes people where they ought to go. â€“ Rosalynn Carter
  30. If you inspire others to grow, you are a leader. â€“ John Quincy Adams
  31. Leadership is a moral relationship based on trust. â€“ Joanne Ciulla
  32. Leadership balances strength and kindness. â€“ Jim Rohn
  33. Leadership raises performance standards. â€“ Peter Drucker
  34. Leadership is service. â€“ J. Donald Walters
  35. Leadership builds confidence through control. â€“ Tom Landry
  36. A leader knows, goes, and shows the way. â€“ John Maxwell
  37. Leadership persuades a group to pursue goals. â€“ John Gardner
  38. Leadership persuades people to do the difficult. â€“ Truman
  39. Leadership translates vision into reality. â€“ Bennis
  40. A leader is a dealer in hope. â€“ Napoleon
  41. Leadership energizes others. â€“ David Caullo
  42. Leadership influences even when tough. â€“ Scott Adams
  43. Leadership guides without force. â€“ Lisa Hanson
  44. Leadership takes people where they havenâ€™t been. â€“ Henry Kissinger
  45. Leadership is service and co-creation. â€“ Greenleaf
  46. Leadership works with others to achieve objectives. â€“ Hersey
  47. Leadership nurtures and enhances. â€“ Tom Peters
  48. Leadership combines strategy and character. â€“ Schwarzkopf
  49. Leadership raises aspirations. â€“ David Gergen
  50. Leadership unlocks potential. â€“ Bradley
  51. Leadership prioritizes effectively. â€“ Stephen Covey
  52. Leadership solves problems. â€“ Colin Powell
  53. Leadership is key to success. â€“ Erskine Bowles
  54. Leadership is about being. â€“ Frances Hesselbein
  55. Leadership creates a climate of excellence. â€“ Mike Vance
  56. Leadership gets voluntary effort. â€“ Fred Smith
  57. Leadership prevents emergencies. â€“ Glasow
  58. Leadership maximizes performance. â€“ W.A. Cohen
  59. Leadership cares for people. â€“ Harald Anderson
  60. Leadership has many definitions. â€“ Ralph Stogdill
  61. Leadership develops people. â€“ Firestone
  62. Share inspiration. â€“ Stevenson
  63. Passion increases influence. â€“ Michele Payn
  64. Leadership is intangible. â€“ Scott Adams
  65. Leadership is doing right unseen. â€“ George Van Valkenburg
  66. Leadership demonstrates possibility. â€“ Mark Yarnell
  67. Leadership is attitude in action. â€“ Harold Geneen
  68. Leadership empowers ingenuity. â€“ George Patton
  69. Leadership by example is real leadership. â€“ Emerson
  70. Leaders, followers, and goals form leadership. â€“ Gary Wills
  71. A leader must know clearly. â€“ Clarence Randall
  72. Leadership takes responsibility. â€“ Mitt Romney
  73. Leadership is difficult but simple. â€“ McKinney
  74. Leadership is human experience. â€“ Lance Secretan
  75. Leadership influences toward common goal. â€“ Northouse
  76. Followers shape leadership brilliance. â€“ Ira Chaleff
  77. A leader knows direction. â€“ Anonymous
  78. Leaders are made, not born. â€“ Vince Lombardi
  79. A leader leaves others able to carry on. â€“ Walter Lippmann
  80. Leadership provides stability. â€“ Friedman
  81. Leadership measured by followersâ€™ success. â€“ Omar Bradley
  82. Leadership is developed. â€“ Elaine Agather
  83. Leadership connects deeply. â€“ Kouzes & Posner
  84. Leadership selects good people. â€“ Nimitz
  85. To lead, first understand others. â€“ Hazlitt
  86. Leadership uses power responsibly. â€“ Zalenik
  87. Leadership prioritizes vital over important. â€“ Sanderson
  88. Leadership develops people through work. â€“ Trinka & Wallace
  89. Leadership sees opportunity in difficulty. â€“ Reed Markham
  90. Leadership influences morale. â€“ Viscount Slim
  91. Great leaders increase momentum. â€“ John Maxwell
  92. Great leaders inspire. â€“ William Arthur Ward
  93. Good followers make good leaders. â€“ Aristotle
  94. Leadership creates change. â€“ Melanne Verveer
  95. Leadership shapes future opinion. â€“ Doris Goodwin
  96. Leadership makes people feel ownership. â€“ David Kelley
  97. Leadership leads by example. â€“ Ken Kesey
  98. Leadership leads through action, not noise. â€“ Chinese Proverb
  99. To command is to serve. â€“ Andre Malraux
  100. Leadership is leading with your whole heart. â€“ Lolly Daskal

NATIONAL BESTSELLER

THE LEADERSHIP GAPWhat Gets Between You and Your GreatnessBy Lolly Daskal

After decades of coaching powerful executives around the world, Lolly Daskal has observed that leaders rise to their positions relying on a specific set of values and traits. But over time, every executive reaches a point when performance declines and failure persists. Very few leaders understand why this happens or how to prevent it.

About the Author

Lolly Daskal is one of the most sought-after executive leadership coaches in the world. Her cross-cultural expertise spans 14 countries, six languages, and hundreds of companies. She is the Founder and CEO of Lead From Within. Her leadership program is designed to help leaders enhance performance and make a meaningful difference in their companies, their lives, and the world.

She has been recognized as:

  • Top-50 Leadership and Management Expert by Inc. magazine
  • The Most Inspiring Woman in the World by Huffington Post

Her writing has appeared in:

  • Harvard Business Review (HBR)
  • Inc.com
  • Fast Company
  • Huffington Post
  • Psychology Today

Her book The Leadership Gap became a national bestseller.

Additional Reading You Might Enjoy

  • 12 Successful Leadership Principles That Never Grow Old
  • A Leadership Manifesto: A Guide to Greatness
  • How to Succeed as a New Leader
  • 12 of the Most Common Lies Leaders Tell Themselves
  • 4 Proven Reasons Why Intuitive Leaders Make Great Leaders
  • The One Quality Every Leader Needs to Succeed
  • The Deception Trap of Leadership

Six Traits of Effective Leadersâ€“ Christian Nevell Bovee

  1. Make others feel important
  2. Promote a vision
  3. Follow the golden rule
  4. Admit mistakes
  5. Criticize others only in private
  6. Stay close to the action

Steps to Become a Leader

  1. Get your employees to want to do their job. Avoid controlling their every move.
  2. Share your vision, enthusiasm, and energy.
  3. Motivate employees with tangible rewards and genuine concern for their wellbeing and progress.
  4. Be accessible and transparent.
  5. Be strong and effective.
  6. Be a role model.
  7. Avoid exploiting your position.
  8. Identify and fully utilize the skills and talents of your staff.
  9. Give credit to others and take responsibility for mistakes.
  10. Take care of yourself.

Shift Your Style: From Traditional Leadership to Collaborative Leadership

  1. Power comes from designation â†’ Power grows through teamwork.
  2. Secretive and protective of information â†’ Transparent sharing of information and knowledge.
  3. Non-participative management â†’ Inviting suggestions and ideas.
  4. Top-down strategy â†’ Bottom-up, democratic brainstorming style.
  5. Focus only on execution and efficiency â†’ Encourage flexibility, innovation, and risk-taking.
  6. Firefighting problems and treating symptoms â†’ Root cause analysis and prevention.
  7. Annual performance review â†’ Continuous feedback and personal coaching.

Written Test â€“ Leadership Position (Objective & Essay Type)

OBJECTIVE TYPE QUESTIONS

Usually consist of 4 options to choose from.

Strategy for Answering Negative Marking Questions

  1. Correct +1 | Wrong â€“1â†’ Answer only if you are completely sure.
  2. Correct +1 | Wrong â€“0.5â†’ Answer if you can eliminate at least 2 options.
  3. Correct +1 | Wrong â€“0.25â†’ Answer if you can eliminate at least 1 option.
  4. No negative markingâ†’ Attempt all questions.

Assumption Strategy Example

Out of 100 questions:

  • You are sure about 50 questions.
  • You can eliminate 1 wrong option in 34 questions.
  • You can eliminate 2 wrong options in 16 questions.

Use elimination wisely to maximize your total score.

Got it. I will format everything properly with main headings bold, clean structure, and full content included. Nothing will be cut.

ESSAY TYPE PAPER

Time Management Strategy

Divide the total time by the total marks to find the number of minutes you can spend per mark. Multiply that by the marks allotted to each question. Spend only that calculated time on that particular question.

NOTE

The basic aim is NOT to leave any question unanswered. Write something for every question (minimum 10 lines).

Recommended Format for Essay Answers

CENTER HEADING

Write the main topic in the center.

GROUP HEADINGS

Examples:

  • Introduction
  • Factors to be Considered
  • Effects of Factors
  • Conclusion

Rules:

  • Do not put a full stop after Group Headings.
  • Writing starts on the next line.

If there are multiple factors:

1. Cause 1This is described in sentences starting on the same line.

2. Cause 2Writing starts on the next line.

If there are multiple factors under each cause:

a. Sub Para Heading

b. Effect of Cause 2

i. Sub Sub Para Headingii. Result 2 is Severe Poverty

NOTE

All levels of headings (Group Heading, Para Heading, Sub Para Heading, Sub-Sub Para Heading) must be bold and underlined in the exam.

EXAM SHORTCUTS

  • Read the syllabus thoroughly.
  • Highlight while reading.
  • Use a speech-to-text app (like Speech Notes) to dictate notes.
  • Solve 2â€“5 practice papers with proper time management.
  • Never leave any question blank (except in objective tests where negative marking is more than half).

STUDYING FOR RETENTION

  • Highlight or underline while reading.
  • Write important points and new words in the margin.
  • Read the preface, executive summary, and about the author before starting a book.
  • First go through the index.
  • After finishing each chapter, dictate main points into a voice dictation app.
  • Apply the knowledge practically within one week through an exercise or project.

SALARY DISCUSSION

It is best to avoid discussing your current salary during the first interview.If asked again, provide the correct information.If asked about salary expectations, you can safely ask for approximately a 30% increase.

CAREER SETTLING DOWN

  • Most important subject (personal and professional).
  • Not taught formally but learned through experience (e.g., Gandhi).
  • Practical, not theoretical.
  • Understand reporting structure and communication norms. These are often unwritten.
  • Read the HR manual and employee handbook thoroughly.
  • Ask HR for detailed briefing.
  • Find a buddy with at least 2 years of experience.
  • Understand internal politics and avoid power struggles.
  • Learn written, email, phone, and body language etiquette.

BOSS HANDLING

  • Donâ€™t get too close too soon.
  • Take notes and complete assignments before deadlines.
  • Discuss problems early.
  • Ask for training when assigned new tasks.
  • Be prepared for meetings.
  • Never speak behind anyoneâ€™s back.
  • Show enthusiasm and willingness to take responsibility.

BUILDING A REPUTATION

  • Project professionalism and dependability.
  • Avoid pleasing everyone or getting too close to seniors.
  • Adhere strictly to timelines.
  • Show honesty and willingness to learn.
  • Be a team player.
  • Avoid gossip and politics.
  • Show energy and readiness for bigger roles.

HANDLING OFFICE POLITICS

  • Donâ€™t form groups.
  • Never corner anyone.
  • Discuss personal problems only with your boss privately.
  • Be honest and avoid manipulation.
  • Understand power structure and groupism.
  • Never speak behind anyoneâ€™s back.

Soft Skill Training

Master the art of successful interactions.

Key skills:

  • Communication (Written & Verbal)
  • Teamwork
  • Adaptability
  • Problem-solving
  • Conflict resolution
  • Strong work ethic
  • Positive attitude
  • Time management
  • Self-confidence
  • Accepting criticism
  • Leadership ability

TYPES OF COMMUNICATION

Only 7% of communication happens through words.93% happens through non-verbal cues:

  • 55% facial expressions
  • 38% vocal tone

VERBAL COMMUNICATION

  1. Conversations
  2. Discussions
  3. Telephonic discussions
  4. Video conferences

NON-VERBAL COMMUNICATION

  • Body language
  • Kinesics, Proxemics, Paralanguage
  • Intention
  • Dress and appearance
  • Signs and symbols

INTERESTING FACTS ABOUT BODY LANGUAGE

  • It sends messages without words.
  • Mostly communicates feelings.
  • People can read it accurately.
  • You cannot not have body language.
  • If words and body conflict, people believe the body.
  • Changing body posture can change feelings.

COMMUNICATION SECRETS

  • Success = Talking so people listen & Listening so people talk
  • Make others feel secure and valued
  • Inspire through clarity and sincerity

ACTIVE LISTENING

  • Listen for key ideas and facts
  • Distinguish evidence from argument
  • Analyze key points
  • Look for unspoken messages
  • Keep open mind
  • Ask clarifying questions
  • Avoid interruptions
  • Take brief meaningful notes
  • Paraphrase what youhear

BODY LANGUAGE OF AN ACTIVE LISTENER

  • Maintains eye contact
  • Open posture
  • Pleasant expression
  • Relaxed but alert
  • Encouraging nods or sounds

PHONE ETIQUETTE WHILE SPEAKING

  • Plan what to say
  • Smile
  • Speak slowly
  • Be polite
  • Monitor time
  • Be clear and concise
  • Seek feedback

SOCIAL SKILLS â€“ PARTY ETIQUETTE

  • Arrive 15 minutes early if picking up guests
  • Be polite to driver
  • Address respectfully
  • Avoid touching unnecessarily
  • Introduce guests properly
  • Avoid gossiping or staring
  • Prepare short speech in advance
  • Follow dining etiquette properly
  • Thank hosts and staff

NEGOTIATIONS

  • Set clear boundaries beforehand
  • Listen first
  • Be ready to walk away
  • Final rate is rarely final
  • Take assurances in writing
  • Never show joy or regret
  • Never give false commitments
  • Negotiate from strength

NETWORKING â€“ GETTING AN INTERVIEW CALL

  • Start with personal contacts
  • Ask friends, mentors for help
  • Make personal connections
  • Track contacts
  • Follow up regularly
  • Be specific about what you want

TELEPHONE ETIQUETTE

  • Answer within 2â€“3 rings
  • Greet appropriately
  • Take accurate notes
  • Avoid negative phrases
  • Thank caller
  • Inform next action clearly

TEACHING SKILLS

  • Simplify concepts
  • Prepare well
  • Motivate students
  • Use real-life examples
  • Be patient
  • Avoid ridicule
  • Use simple language
  • Be impartial

TEAM BUILDING

  • Be fair
  • Select carefully
  • Maintain clear expectations
  • Delegate and coach
  • Give credit publicly
  • Counsel privately
  • Encourage accountability

SALES TRAINING

TRAINING CARRIED OUT FOR MBA BATCHES

  1. Introduction and importance of communication
  2. Emergency announcements on shop floor (rain/blast situations)
  3. Handling latecomers (genuine and habitual)
  4. Proper stance and arm positioning while speaking
  5. Delivering a motivational lecture
  6. Talking about yourself
  7. Listening skills
  8. Eye contact in public speaking
  9. Gestures, mannerisms, and authenticity
  10. Leadership skills â€“ Leadership is the process of interpersonal influence over team members to achieve organizational goals in a given situation. Managers must work effectively with peers, supervisors, and subordinates. Understanding self and influencing others is essential.
  11. Appropriate voice volume
  12. Formal dressing
  13. Reading and writing in pairs (back-to-back)
  14. Reading aloud to class
  15. Short write-up and presentation
  16. Introduction to group discussion
  17. Preparing a CV
  18. What assessors look for
  19. Opportunities in group discussions
  20. Introducing a topic
  21. How to intervene (â€œbutt inâ€)
  22. Managing conflict
  23. Concluding and summarizing
  24. Interview preparation
  25. Emotional preparation for interviews
  26. Dressing and movement
  27. Researching a target company
  28. Following up on employment calls
  29. Controlling body language
  30. Participating in conferences
  31. Social party behavior / hosting skills
  32. Table etiquette
  33. Interviewing workers
  34. Negotiating with trade unions
  35. Written communication (types of letters)
  36. Light conversation and topics to avoid
  37. Talking with senior management
  38. Telephone etiquette
  39. Official spokesperson / P.R.O role
  40. Avoiding being misquoted
  41. Conflict resolution
  42. Making friends
  43. Maintaining a contact list
  44. Debate
  45. Talent exhibition
  46. Getting an appointment with senior officials
  47. Building and assessing organizational culture
  48. On-the-spot speaking / handling pauses
  49. Presence of mind
  50. Extempore agility

  • Information gathering skills

  • Career planning
