The Discovery Cycle has four stages, summarized in the acronym ORCA:

  1. Observation. Understand how the world is changing â€“ for example, by looking for anomalies, paradoxes, peripheral developments and direct experience.
  2. Reflection. Techniques that work best at this stage include zooming in and out, using a muse, suspending judgement, slowing down, reflecting on whatâ€™s missing, restructuring data to simplify patterns, juxtaposing pieces of different information (bisociation) and taking time to rest.
  3. Conversation. People set the pace and scope for innovation, so the best techniques to use at this stage include contrasting views, setting the agenda, framing the issues and generating hypotheses.
  4. Analysis. The final stage of the Discovery Cycle involves gathering systematic evidence, classifying and categorizing data, naming, completing data analysis and hypothesizing.

Lessons from great innovators

What lessons do innovators have for us? Several come to mind:

â€¢ Build on the ideas of others / collaborate. That should be easy for scientists who are, in the words of Isaac Newton, â€˜standing on the shoulders of giantsâ€™.â€¢ Take an unorthodox, distinctive approach.â€¢ Embrace diversity.â€¢ Create a diverse, open and creative culture.â€¢ Develop empathy for the consumer or customer (understand people).â€¢ Execute and practically take action.â€¢ Be confident and bold.â€¢ Find your motivation; enjoy your work.

This list also highlights three other vital points:

  1. Innovation relies on teamwork, agility and the ability to lead change, the other elements of this programme.
  2. Innovation is about mindset: you need to think like an innovator and you need to encourage that in others.
  3. Innovation isnâ€™t only about products: it is about improving efficiency, reducing costs, improving the quality and quantity of peopleâ€™s work, removing constraints â€“ and thatâ€™s just internally; it also means serving and understanding customers, building a brand â€“ and more.

WEEK 45

THE FORTUNE AT THE BOTTOM OF THE PYRAMID (BOP)

Developing the innovatorâ€™s mindset

If a company goes to the bottom of the wealth pyramid and builds affordable products, creates awareness and provides access, then the market is phenomenal.

The late Professor C.K. Prahalad argued that there is a â€˜poverty penaltyâ€™ where the poorest people pay more for everything because they donâ€™t have a choice: they are stuck with local monopolies and bad products and services.

Research recently highlighted by the World Resources Institute shows that the worldâ€™s four billion poorest people represent a US$5 trillion market opportunity. There are several other issues at the bottom of the pyramid:

â€¢ Pricing is vital. At the BOP, you need to start with an affordable price, understanding that price minus profit equals the acceptable level of cost. This different way of thinking leads to a new range of exciting options.â€¢ Innovation is essential. This can be accelerated and improved by focusing on BOP markets because minor, incremental changes wonâ€™t be enough: the market requires a fundamental rethink.â€¢ Businesses need to substitute investment for collaboration. Management time is needed to increase collaboration â€“ and it is cheaper than simply investing cash.

Companies that ignore growth markets will be left behind â€“ and will have five years, at best, before businesses from growth markets start competing with them.

Developing the innovatorâ€™s mindset

Where can you improve your approach to innovation? Ask yourself the following questions and mark yourself out of 10 for each attribute: this will help highlight areas for improvement.

When innovating, how effectively do you:

â€¢ engage as many people as possible â€¦?â€¢ â€¦ and build an open, diverse and positive team?â€¢ define the specific challenge or issue?â€¢ challenge assumptions: yours and other peopleâ€™s?â€¢ confront challenges and problems?â€¢ understand that good ideas can come from anywhere?â€¢ follow through â€“ by being practical and realistic, and planning implementation?â€¢ focus on the benefits as well as the potential pitfalls?â€¢ question? Questioning is a great way both to provide support (e.g. what help do you need?) and challenge (how can we do this faster/cheaper?)â€¢ give praise and credit: build momentum (revolutions fail, flywheels succeed)?â€¢ be open, build relationships?â€¢ remove constraints, tirelessly?â€¢ remember the essentials of leading change? (See Number 35.)â€¢ balance intuition and analysis?â€¢ build collaboration and teamwork? (Think of the 5Ms: meaning, mindset, measurement, mobilizing, mechanisms for renewal.)â€¢ avoid the pitfalls of decision-making? (See the description of inhibitors below â€“ which ones are your greatest vulnerability?)â€¢ consciously develop your skills?â€¢ design matters? (This affects how people feel about something: whether itâ€™s credible, engaging, worthwhile.)

WEEK 46

THE SIX THINKING HATS

If you want to get ahead, get a hat

Created by Edward de Bono, the Six Thinking Hats technique explains the different styles of thinking we use while making decisions.

Overview

People usually have a preferred thinking style. While this style may be useful, it can limit thinking and cause us to miss better solutions. The Six Thinking Hats method allows us to deliberately switch thinking styles so that problems are viewed from multiple perspectives. This leads to better, more balanced decisions.

Using only one approach, such as logical thinking, may ignore emotional reactions, risks, or creative possibilities. Applying multiple hats gives a fuller picture and improves both decision-making and implementation.

The Six Thinking Hats

White HatFocuses on facts, data, and information.Looks at what information is available and what is missing.Analyzes trends, evidence, and objective data.

Red HatUses intuition, emotions, and gut feelings.Considers how people may emotionally react to a decision.Allows expression of feelings without justification.

Black HatLooks at risks, problems, and weaknesses.Asks why an idea might fail.Helps identify dangers and prepare contingency plans.

Yellow HatFocuses on optimism and benefits.Looks at value, advantages, and positive outcomes.Helps maintain motivation during challenges.

Green HatEncourages creativity and new ideas.Explores alternatives and innovative solutions.Free-flowing thinking with minimal criticism.

Blue HatFocuses on control, structure, and process.Commonly used by facilitators or leaders.Manages discussion flow and ensures productive thinking.

WEEK 47

INNOVATION CULTURE

Peter Druckerâ€™s seven steps for developing a creative culture

Innovation is not limited to a few individuals or departments. It is a company-wide activity that requires the right mindset, culture, and systems. Profitable ideas can come from anyone, anywhere, at any time.

Where does innovation come from?

Innovation emerges when people are encouraged to question, think critically, and share ideas openly. It is not only about big breakthroughs; small, incremental improvements also create strong competitive advantages. Innovation does not depend solely on large research budgets. It is embedded in everyday thinking, operations, and decision-making.

Creating an innovative organization

Innovative organizations differ because they actively support creativity. They encourage questioning, challenge assumptions, and adapt systems to support innovation. They also collaborate with external experts to strengthen internal capabilities.

Peter Druckerâ€™s seven steps to promote innovation

  1. Analyze unexpected successes and understand why they worked.
  2. Examine situations where outcomes differed from expectations.
  3. Challenge accepted underperformance and question why it is tolerated.
  4. Identify and take advantage of changes in the market.
  5. Observe social changes to uncover new opportunities.
  6. Monitor economic changes and recognize business possibilities.
  7. Understand how new ideas, information, and technology affect customers.

Five catalysts for successful innovation

ConsciousnessPeople understand organizational goals and believe they can contribute.

MultiplicityTeams include diverse skills, backgrounds, experiences, and ideas.

ConnectivityStrong, trusting relationships are encouraged across teams and functions.

AccessibilityPeople have access to resources, time, leaders, and decision-makers.

ConsistencyInnovation is embedded in leadership style, systems, and daily practices.

To build a strong innovation culture, reflect on which of these catalysts need improvement and identify clear actions to strengthen them.

WEEK 48

DISNEYâ€™S CREATIVITY STRATEGY

When you need more than just the bare necessities

We all have a preferred thinking style â€“ some of us are dreamers, while others are realists or critics. This can prevent us from seeing an issue from other angles. Walt Disneyâ€™s method uses all three of these thinking styles to help view a situation from different perspectives and find the best way forward.

Problem solving, decision-making, and planning suffer when we have too narrow a focus, yet it can be difficult to change how we naturally approach issues. Using Disneyâ€™s three styles together will improve your decision-making.

â€¢ The Dreamer is focused on potential and possibilities.â€¢ The Realist focuses on practical aspects and implementation.â€¢ The Critic questions and challenges plans and assumptions and notices potential problems or flaws.

Using the Disney method

  1. Select an issue you want to address but put it to one side while you get into the right frame of mind.
  2. Go to three different places to think about the issue from each perspective (you will associate each environment with that approach). These can be entirely different places or simply different parts of one room.
  3. For each way of thinking (starting with Dreamer, moving to Realist and then to Critic), first remember a time when you were either creative, realistic, or critical. This will help you access that style and apply it to the current situation.
  4. In each frame of mind, address the issue at hand solely from that perspective.

â€¢ In the Dreamer space, let your ideas flow freely.â€¢ In the Realist space, think about how the ideas you have created can be implemented. How can they be achieved? What needs to happen?â€¢ In the Critic space, question and challenge your ideas and plan. Identify strengths and weaknesses; look for flaws; look for gaps or potential problems. Determine what needs to be done better.

  1. Once you have completed these stages, go back to the beginning and re-evaluate your original dream and plan through each thinking stage in turn. You can repeat this process until you feel the plan works well from each perspective.

Types of questions to ask at each stage

Dreamerâ€¢ Why am I doing this?â€¢ Can it be done better?â€¢ What would I like to happen?â€¢ Wouldnâ€™t it be great ifâ€¦?â€¢ What reward or result would I like?

Realistâ€¢ How can I make that happen?â€¢ Who else do I need to make it work?â€¢ What needs to happen â€“ and when?â€¢ What resources do I need?â€¢ How much will it cost?

Criticâ€¢ Does the idea really have potential?â€¢ Is the objective achievable?â€¢ Are there any barriers or resource issues?â€¢ Does the plan work? Consider issues such as timing, cost, or market potential.â€¢ How can the plan be improved â€“ are there gaps or are some things unnecessary?

WEEK 49

THE MATE MODEL FOR STRATEGIC SELLING

Achieving your sales objectives

Segmenting and managing your contacts within a client organization in terms of their support for your sales objectives is a highly effective way of developing client relationships and selling.

Four steps

Step 1: Define your unique sales objective.Be clear about what you are selling and when, and the value it brings. What makes it an attractive proposition? What is its value for the organization or client? This sounds simple but it can be muddled or overlooked, with disastrous consequences.

Step 2: Identify all the players using the MATE model.MATE highlights the need to focus on Money, Allies, Technical experts, and End users. Identify each contact (including those you donâ€™t know), recording their job title and name.

MoneyThey have the ultimate veto on sales.

AlliesThey provide useful information, can guide you, and influence others to support your objective.

Technical experts / assessorsThey filter out information, can be gatekeepers, and can influence â€œMoneyâ€.

End usersThey use, manage, or work with your products.

â€¢ Money. The budget holder has authority over the decision to spend. They focus on the bottom line and have the power of veto. They ask: â€œWhat impact will this have and what return will we get?â€â€¢ Allies / Advocates. They provide valuable information, lead you to the right people, and may be influential inside or outside the organization.â€¢ Technical experts. Gatekeepers who evaluate technical aspects. They can say â€œnoâ€ based on specifications.â€¢ End users. They judge how the proposal affects their work. They ask: â€œWill it work for me or my department?â€

Step 3: Consider each individualâ€™s level of support.Assess whether their support for your sales objective is high, medium, or low.

Step 4: Consider each individualâ€™s level of influence.Assess their influence within the organization â€” high, medium, or low.

Check for warning signs

â€¢ Have I at least one person for each area?â€¢ Am I free from concerns about their influence?â€¢ Have I made personal contact with them?â€¢ Do I know their response modes and what they are looking for?

Identify your tactics to further the sale and eliminate warning signs. Be honest and prepared to challenge and develop your thinking. With the information gathered, contact key people, establish rapport, and understand their needs.

WEEK 50

THE TEN Cs OF SELLING ONLINE

Building a successful business online

The Ten Cs focus on meeting customer needs and are the key drivers of success in online business. The importance of each factor may change depending on the companyâ€™s stage of growth, competition, market type, and brand strength.

1. Content

Content defines your brand and sets the tone of your website.It should be clear, engaging, informative, visually appealing, and tailored to the target audience.Customers should access information quickly and control how much they view.

2. Communication

Communication is not just about sharing information.It is about listening, building trust, and creating one-to-one relationships.Encourage interaction, collect feedback, and study customer behaviour using data like click patterns.

3. Customer Care

Trust is essential in online selling.Customers must feel confident about payments, data security, and after-sales support.Provide multiple payment options, order tracking, and fast responses to queries.Good customer care leads to repeat purchases, referrals, and higher sales.

4. Community and Culture

People use the internet to connect and socialize.Create spaces where customers can interact, share views, and access expert content.Information should be clear, useful, and engaging to build loyalty and belonging.

5. Convenience

Customers expect ease and speed.Navigation should be simple, intuitive, and user-friendly.Every feature should be evaluated from the customerâ€™s point of view.

6. Connectivity

A website should be engaging enough to make customers return.High-quality content, incentives, and useful links help retain visitors.Linking to complementary sites adds value and improves customer experience.

7. Cost and Profitability

Your online strategy should clearly define objectives and benefits.Control costs and focus on profitability to ensure long-term sustainability.

8. Customization

Customization should be planned from the beginning.Allow customers to choose options through clear communication.Refine customization continuously to stay competitive.

9. Capability

Employees must see the internet as a tool to serve customers better.Set goals, measure performance, and improve systems regularly.Actively seek customer opinions to enhance services.

10. Competitiveness

Continuously track competitors and market trends.Understand what competitors are doing and anticipate future moves.Plan for worst-case scenarios to make your online strategy strong and durable.

STEP 5.Plan your Career with year wise Targets with the help of a Mentor

This is a session with a MENTOR & A CAREER COUNSELLOR.

You will be administered a Psychometric Test to help you understand your Interests, Personality and Abilities and talents.

A career for a young person should be chosen based on:

  1. Personal interests,
  2. Skills / Ability
  3. Values,
  4. Market demand
  5. Long-term growth potential.
  6. Financial Stability
  7. Family influence
  8. Educational Budget

Surviving in the Ai Era

With the rapid adopting of Ai which will result mass replacement of jobs - Your future role is to do everything AI cannot: to provide context, judgment, ethics, inspiration, and connection.

The skills to develop are broken down into two categories:

The "AI-Immune" Core SkillsThe "AI-Augmentation" Technical Skills.The "AI-Immune" Core Human Skills

These are the skills that are incredibly difficult, if not impossible, to automate. They are the bedrock of your value.

1. Strategic Thinking & Critical Reasoning:

â€¢What it is:Moving beyond "what" to do and "how" to do it, to "why" we are doing it. Seeing the bigger picture, connecting disparate dots, and making judgments in ambiguous situations where data is incomplete or conflicting.

â€¢How to develop it:â€¢ Always ask "Why?" and "So what?" in meetings. Challenge assumptions (respectfully).â€¢ Get involved in strategic projects, even tangentially. Volunteer to help with business cases.â€¢ Read widely about industry trends, economics, and geopolitics to understand the external forces shaping your company.

2. Ethical Judgment & Nuanced Decision-Making:

â€¢What it is:AI can be trained on data, but it lacks a moral compass. You will be the one to make the call on layoffs, resource allocation, ethical dilemmas, and decisions that involve fairness, bias, and long-term consequences.

â€¢How to develop it:â€¢ Study ethics, especially in the context of AI and business.â€¢ When faced with a decision, explicitly list the ethical trade-offs.â€¢ Seek out mentors known for their strong judgment and ask them how they approach tough calls.

3. Empathy & Emotional Intelligence (EQ):

â€¢What it is:The ability to understand, connect with, and influence the emotions of your team members. This is crucial for motivation, conflict resolution, and fostering psychological safety.

â€¢How to develop it:â€¢ Practice active listening. Focus entirely on the speaker without formulating your response.â€¢ Learn to read non-verbal cues.â€¢ Regularly ask for feedback on your own management style and its impact on others.

4. Inspirational Leadership & Vision Casting:

â€¢What it is:AI can set a target; you can inspire people to want to hit it. You create a compelling vision, foster a positive culture, and motivate your team through purpose, not just process.

â€¢How to develop it:â€¢ Work on your storytelling skills. Learn to frame goals as a shared mission.â€¢ Public speaking and communication courses are invaluable.â€¢ Be transparent about challenges and celebrate wins genuinely.

5. Creativity & Complex Problem-Solving:

â€¢What it is:Not just coming up with ideas, but synthesizing novel solutions to messy, multi-faceted problems that have no precedent or clear answer.

â€¢How to develop it:â€¢ Practice brainstorming techniques and run workshops with your team.â€¢ Tackle problems from other departments to stretch your thinking.â€¢ Constantly ask, "What if we tried the opposite?" or "How would Company X in a different industry solve this?"

The "AI-Augmentation" Skills to Pick Up a New Area

These skills allow you to leverage AI, making you a more effective and valuable manager. This is your bridge to picking up new, high-value areas.

1. AI Literacy (The New Managerial Currency):

â€¢What it is:You don't need to be a data scientist, but you must understand what AI can and cannot do. Know the basics of machine learning, natural language processing, and generative AI. Understand concepts like "prompt engineering" to get the best out of tools like ChatGPT or Claude.

â€¢How to develop it:â€¢ Take online courses on AI for business (Coursera, edX, LinkedIn Learning have many).â€¢ Use AI tools daily for your own work (drafting emails, summarizing reports, brainstorming) to build an intuitive understanding.â€¢ Follow AI thought leaders and read reputable tech news sources.

2. Data Fluency, Not Just Data Literacy:

â€¢What it is:Beyond reading a dashboard, it's about knowing what questions to ask of the data, interpreting the results critically (is this correlation or causation?), and using data to tell a compelling story to drive action.

â€¢How to develop it:â€¢ Take a basic statistics course.â€¢ Always question the source and methodology behind the data you're given.â€¢ Practice creating presentations that use data to support a narrative.

3. Change Management & Ambiguity Navigation:

â€¢What it is:The business landscape will be in constant flux due to AI. Your role will be to guide your team through that change, managing fear, reskilling people, and maintaining morale during uncertainty.

â€¢How to develop it:â€¢ Volunteer to lead a small-scale change initiative.â€¢ Study change management models (e.g., ADKAR, Kotter's 8-Step).â€¢ Become comfortable making decisions with 70% of the information instead of 100%.

How to Synthesize This to Pick Up a New Area

Let's say you're a marketing manager and you want to move into Product Management, a field that heavily leverages AI.

â€¢Lead with Human Skills:Frame your value around your core strengths. "I excel at understanding customer pain points (Empathy), translating them into a compelling product vision (Strategic Thinking), and motivating a cross-functional team to build it (Leadership)."

â€¢Augment with AI Skills:Simultaneously, you learn the new domain. You use AI to analyze user feedback at scale, you study how top product managers use AI for roadmapping and A/B testing analysis (AI Literacy), and you learn the key metrics (DAU, MAU, Churn) that define the field (Data Fluency).

â€¢Become the Bridge:You position yourself as the person who can bridge the technical AI capabilities with the human needs of the customer and the business goals. This is an incredibly powerful and sought-after position.

The "Hedgehog Concept" for the AI Era (Ask, Think and Adapt the concept from Good to Great):

a. What are you deeply passionate about? (Leading people? Solving customer problems?)b. What can you be the best in the world at? (Your unique blend of human skills.)c. What drives the economic engine? (Creating value that AI cannot.)d. The intersection of these three is where your unshakable career will be.e. Conclusion: Your job won't be "taken" by AI if you evolve your role. Become the manager who uses AI as the most powerful tool in your toolbox to amplify your team's uniquely human potential. Focus on being the strategist, the coach, the ethicist, and the innovator. The managers who thrive will be those who lead with AI, not against it.

Personal Skills to develop for surviving in the VUCA World(Volatility, Uncertainty, Complexity & Ambiguity) are:

Flexible personalityListening skillsCommunication and negotiation skillsEmpathyCollaborationAdaptabilityAnticipationLearning attitudeNetworkingMental robustnessAssertiveness

STEP 6. Apply your skills in â€˜Real Life, and rise in your career with a Mentor

These are live sessions with on our Mentor who will help you apply your skills in â€˜Real Lifeâ€™ & rise in your career with MENTOR

Emotional Preparation for the Interview - Speak to yourself

â€¢ Psyche yourself â€“ What is the worst case â€“ Not hired.â€¢ There is nothing to lose but all to gain.â€¢ This is only a practice and I will learn from it.â€¢ This is one out of the 100 chances I will get & I will make full use of it to learn.â€¢ I will be honest & frank.â€¢ I will be Patient.â€¢ I will not get stressed out.â€¢ I will be positive and hopeful to the end.â€¢ I will not be negative about my present Employer or anything else.

Researching the Company

One of the most critical steps in preparing for an interview is researching the company. This knowledge will not only help you answer questions but also demonstrate your interest in the organization. Here are some tips for effective research:

Interview Process â€“ 4 Stages

  • Application Review: Employers review resumes and cover letters to shortlist candidates.
  • Initial Screening: This may involve a phone or video interview to assess basic qualifications.
  • In-Person Interview: Candidates meet with hiring managers or panels to discuss their skills and experiences.
  • Follow-Up: After the interview, candidates may receive feedback or an offer.

Qualities the interviewer is looking for :-

  • Focused, enthusiastic, confident, crisp & to the point, passionate, ambitious, team person, energetic, mature, emotional stability & adaptable to Co Culture
  • First impression matters a lot.
  • The most qualified person may not get hired â€“ so others also have a chance
  • Read the job description and research company carefully & ask for more details
  • Look into the eyes of the interviewer and act confidently.
  • Be honest and enthusiastic and highlight your strengths by giving examples of your Good Qualities â€“ e.g. Personality, Motivation, Leadership, Flexibility, Decision Making, Go-Getting Attitude, Conflict Resolution & Problem Solving Skills, Loyalty, Integrity, Creativity

IMPORTANT

  • Describe your personality honestly and why this job excites you. Do not speak ill of your previous company.
  • Do not try making a Positive when asked about a Weakness â€“ Example â€“ â€œIâ€™m a perfectionistâ€ ( Interviewers are not fooled)
  • Honestly highlight a skill that you wish to improve upon and describe what you are proactively doing to enhance your skill.

HOW TO DRESS UP

  • Males â€“ Formal (Coat & Tie)
  • Females Formals or Formal suit, Saree, Sober Make up
  • Light Deodorant / Perfume
  • Items to carry â€“ Bag/Folder, Pen, Highlighter, Certificates, CV, Visiting Card
  • Mobile must be kept off
  • Reach 15 minutes early

HOW TO HOLD THE INTERVIEWERâ€™S ATTENTION?

Attention Level

  • 0 to 10 Seconds is 100%
  • 10 to 60 Seconds it falls to 50%
  • 60 to 90 Seconds it falls to almost 10% if there are no interruptions

Near the end of your long response the interviewer starts to formulate their next question unless you keep them engaged. By asking a question you promote two-way communications and minimize the risk of talking too much. This helps you ensure they are listening while you talk.

Other Questions which you must prepare for

  • Tell me about yourself.
  • Why do you want to do an MBA
  • Where do you see yourself 10 years down the line?
  • What are your ambitions in life?
  • Who is your hero and why?
  • Which is the field you want to take and Why?
  • What are the qualities of a good Leader?
  • What if you do not get selected?
  • You are a woman. How will you balance professional life and home?
  • Give an example of a successful project, your role & why it succeeded?
  • How would your subordinates describe your management style, strengths & Weaknesses?
  • Give me an example of handling underperforming employee
  • Where do you see the industry going? What are you doing to stay on top of these changes?
  • What are the most important things to you about any job? Is it the pay, the opportunities, feelings of self-worth, fellow employees, location, benefits, etc.?
  • Tell me about a time when you accomplished something significant that wouldnâ€™t have happened if you had not been there to make it happen.
  • Describe for me a time when you may have been disappointed in your behavior.
  • Tell me about a time when you had to discipline or fire a friend.
  • Tell me about a time when youâ€™ve had to develop leaders under you.
  • Do you want to ask me something?

EXAMPLE OF QUESTIONS YOU MAY ASK

  • What position are you considering me for? What are the top challenges that Iâ€™ll face in this job?
  • What are the characteristics of people who are most successful in your company?

SOME SAMPLE ANSWERS

1. Tell me about yourself?Answerâ€œI am a presently â€˜Senior Executive Accountsâ€™. I have a lot of experience in taxissues and audit. (expertise and skills) My experience includes carryinginternal audit for ISO 9000 and resolving tax issues for the last 2 years(insert knowledge or skill) I have worked in the Construction Industryand the Media Industry. My background also includes roles as Junior Accountant(position title), Senior Accountant (position title) and Senior Auditor(position title). My education/certifications include CA (degree orcertification) and M. Com. I would like to be described by my Colleaguesas â€˜results focusedâ€™ & â€˜details oriented. Highlights of my professionalaccomplishments include winning the â€œEmployee of the Year Award in 2003 and theâ€˜Best Suggestion Award in 2004

2. Why do you want to leave your previous organization and join us?Answerâ€œMy company merged with another firm and the new management wanted to bring intheir own team. Prior to the merger I was a strong performer with positiveperformance reviews.â€ Provide References and Proof â€“ Providereferences from a former colleague and boss to verify his performance.Demonstrating a confidence and willingness to provide references to supportyour reasons for leaving is a powerful way to ensure you are believed.

3. Why do you want to do an MBA?AnswerI come from In fact an army background. Everyone in my family has been in the army or has been an employee. No one has ever started a business on their own. I want to do MBA and get the knowledge of marketing & finance so that sometime later I can start a business of my own. My role model is Mrs. Kiran M Shaw who has opened a successful biotech co. I have just appeard for my final year biotech. But I donâ€™t want to continue Btech & do Msc. bâ€™coz I feel it is more important to know how to manage the business. I actually do not know much about business now. My father is in the corporate world and would guide me. I will work hard and build a successful future for myself.

4. Where do you see yourself 10 years down the line?AnswerI will take up marketing because it is very interesting and very important tobuild a business. I want to pick up some knowledge in finance andhopefully get employment in a biotech firm later. Here Iâ€™ll learn how businessis done and gain experience. Iâ€™ll try to start business andhopefully down the line I see myself as a budding entrepreneur. I work hardto make my enterprise a success. Iâ€™ll try to build a good team of dedicatedpeople with whom Iâ€™ll share the success & the money which the enterprisemakes.

5. Who is your hero and why?AnswerMy role model is Kiran Majumdar Shaw. I like her and admire her. I too wantto do something constructive in the field of business. She is one who hasstarted an enterprise built a strong team of managers around her & who canbe credited for being Pioneers in the biotech field in India. She hasmastered biotech as a subject and the mechanics of setting up and growing a business. She has proved beyond doubts that a women have a good chance of being successful in business.

6. Which is the field you want to take and Why?AnswerI would like to take up marketing because its very interesting and also a veryimportant step in business because unless the products of the company aremarketed well, there is no scope for the expansion of the business by gettinggood contracts and deals.

7. What are the qualities of a good Leader?AnswerA good leader is firstly, a good listener. He must listen to others and considertheir suggestions. He must be patient. And he should not be self centred. Heshould think of the group as a whole. He should be a motivator & should beable to get the best out of the people. He should have a good understanding of human behavior. He should know when he should be compassionate and when he should be aggressive and demanding. I believe, a correct balance of this would make a good leader. He should have a of professional knowledge.

8. What if you do not get selected?AnswerIâ€™m quite sure I will get into one of the best business schools.I will go pick up some work in marketing or finance in a good company and learn to work in a team

Group Discussion

Again Watch these short videos before you start

Group Discussions Strategyhttps://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BguYUJ7cWrs

Group Discussion Tactics (Funny video in Hindi)https://youtu.be/QqGOUFVfoWI?si=Anh8k2omL4vnWiFX

Personality Traits Gauged in Group Discussion

  • Ability to interact in a team
  • Communications Skills
  • Reasoning ability.
  • Leadership skills.
  • Initiative & Enthusiasm.
  • Assertiveness.
  • Flexibility.
  • Nurturing & Coaching Ability.
  • Creativity.
  • Ability to think in ones feet.

HOW TO DO WELL IN A GROUP DISCUSSION

  • Grab the opportunity to be the first speaker and to Introduce the topic. Keep a pre-prepaired 5 sentence â€“ EXAMPLE â€“ Good Morning friends. â€“ Name the Topic. â€“ This has been the center of discussion in many forums and it the media. This topic has great importance s in our lives and I am glad that we are discussing this today. As per my view â€“ I believe that â€”â€”â€”. Now let us have the views of some of us.
  • Listening carefully and look for a chance of butting in (Donâ€™t do this too often).
  • Agreeing with a person and elaborating it by giving an experience or examples
  • Disagreeing & giving examples.
  • Looking on both sides of a coin. Intervening to get a balanced view.
  • Intervening during a conflict between 2 people fighting immaturely.
  • Co-operating & leading.
  • No cornering or making fun of participants
  • Intervening & giving a chance to a timid participant.
  • Giving examples & experiences
  • If you did not get a chance to start the discussion, then you must try Concluding (EXAMPLE â€“This has been an interesting discussion. We have got diverse views. It appears to be evenly balances and hence we need to make our individual choices â€”â€” not your own view, no final decision)

STARTING A GD â€“ Introducing the topicFriends, the topic given to us is â€”-This is perhaps among the most discussed topics.ORThis is almost an abstract topic or this is a very controversial topic & has been a subject of debate in the media and open forums.As there are always cain 2 sides of a a coin, this topic also will be viewed from both sides.Our aim should be to evolve a balanced view or a consolidated opinion on this. The way I understand this topic is â€”- some may say â€”- some may say â€”-. So let us proceed.

Intervening when discussion goes out of control.Friends! friends! I think we have to control this discussion. Otherwise vwe will end up with everyone speaking & no one listening. And we will not be able to come to a conclusion.Let us speak one by one & let us start with the people who have not got a chance yet.As for me, as Iâ€™ve spoken now, I donâ€™t mind being the last to speak.Ok! No.5 what is ur view on this? What woud u like to say?

CONCLUDING THE DISCUSSIONOk friends, for the last 10-15 minutes, we have generated a very interesting discussion. Some of us (smile) really pushing it very hardSo what is our consolidated view on this?I think that the overall consensus is that â€”Although we need to consider the points brought out by some of us â€”-Thus I believe that we should do the following:-

  1. â€”â€”â€”â€”â€”â€“
  2. â€”â€”â€”â€”â€”â€“

Self Learning using Chat GPTis our best knowledgeable servant!It is the easiest thing to use - We should only know HOW TO ASK (Questioning also called Prompt Engineering). It can write emails for you, summarize large essays, make Slides on any subject, teach any subject, generate sales leads, write software for you and almost everything else.It is a MUST LEARN for all employees & Students.It is now incorporated FREE all Search engines like Google, Bing, Edge and all Microsoft Office Applications like Excel, Word, Outlook etc.EASIEST WAY is to Download BING on your Mobile using Playstore. Very simple to use. Using a Mobile Phone - you can just speak to it and ask questions. (This is called Prompt Engineering)}

Optimizing Questions (Prompt Engineering)

  • Give a Role- eg You are an expert Web Developer
  • Give a Task & Be specific eg Develop an Ecommerce website to Export Handicrafts
  • Set Format eg Easy to understand by Foreigners and use Bullet Points
  • Limit length of response eg 18000 words.

Types of Prompts

  • Chain of Thought prompts â€“ breaking down complex prompts to a sequence of smaller prompts.
  • Role play prompts
  • Conversational prompts
  • Prompt Priming â€“ provide context or background
  • Avoiding Biases and Ethics in Prompt Engineering
  • Use neutral Language and avoid stereo types.

Examples of a Prompt1 Create a banner for an online store summer sale featuring a beach background with the Text â€“ Summer Sale up to 50% off2 â€“ As a CRM â€“ Help a customer who is having trouble with logging into a website.3 Write a Python function that takes a list of integers and returns the sum of all even numbers

  • Bing
  • Gork
  • Chat GPT
  • DeepSeek
  • Gemini
  • Copilot
  • DALL-E

Ai image using Nano Bananahttps://youtu.be/7J6bHCJQqek

Examples of Effective Video Resources

  1. TED Talks: These talks cover a wide range of topics, including communication and leadership. They feature experts sharing their insights and experiences.
  2. YouTube Channels: Channels like "The School of Life" offer videos on emotional intelligence and interpersonal skills.
  3. Online Courses: Platforms like Coursera and Udemy provide courses that include video lectures on various soft skills.

Interactive Tests: A Hands-On ApproachWhile videos are great for visual learning, interactive tests provide a hands-on approach. They allow you to apply what you have learned and receive immediate feedback. Hereâ€™s how interactive tests can enhance your soft skills:

Active EngagementInteractive tests require active participation. Instead of passively watching a video, you are involved in the learning process. This active engagement helps reinforce concepts and improve retention.

Immediate FeedbackOne of the biggest advantages of interactive tests is immediate feedback. After completing a test, you can see how well you did. This feedback helps you identify areas for improvement.For example, if you take a test on conflict resolution and score low, you can focus on that area in your future learning.

Self-Paced LearningLike videos, interactive tests allow for self-paced learning. You can take your time to think through questions and reflect on your answers. This approach can lead to deeper understanding and mastery of soft skills.

Examples of Interactive Test Resources

  1. Skill Assessment Platforms: Websites like Skillshare offer quizzes and tests on various soft skills.
  2. Personality Tests: Tools like the Myers-Briggs Type Indicator (MBTI) can help you understand your communication style and how it affects your interactions.
  3. Online Learning Platforms: Many online courses include quizzes and interactive exercises to test your knowledge.

Combining Videos and Interactive TestsThe best way to improve your soft skills is to combine both videos and interactive tests. Hereâ€™s how you can create a balanced learning experience:

Start with VideosBegin your learning journey with engaging videos. Choose topics that interest you or areas where you want to improve. Take notes as you watch to reinforce your learning.

Follow Up with Interactive TestsAfter watching a video, take an interactive test on the same topic. This will help you apply what you learned and assess your understanding.

Reflect on Your LearningAfter completing the test, take a moment to reflect. What did you learn? What areas do you need to work on? This reflection will help solidify your knowledge and guide your future learning.

Continuous LearningSoft skills are not something you learn once and forget. They require continuous practice and improvement. Make it a habit to regularly watch videos and take tests to keep your skills sharp.

Practical Tips for Using Videos and TestsTo make the most of videos and interactive tests, consider these practical tips:

Set Clear GoalsBefore you start, set clear learning goals. What specific soft skills do you want to improve? Having clear goals will help you stay focused and motivated.

Create a Learning ScheduleDedicate time each week to watch videos and take tests. Consistency is key to developing your soft skills.

Engage with OthersDiscuss what you learn with friends or colleagues. Engaging in conversations about soft skills can deepen your understanding and provide new perspectives.

Track Your ProgressKeep a record of the videos you watch and the tests you take. Tracking your progress can motivate you and help you see how far you have come.

Stay Open to FeedbackBe open to feedback from others. Whether itâ€™s from a test or a colleague, constructive feedback can help you identify areas for improvement.

The Future of Soft Skills DevelopmentAs technology continues to evolve, so will the methods for developing soft skills. Virtual reality (VR) and augmented reality (AR) are emerging as new tools for learning. These technologies can create immersive experiences that simulate real-life scenarios.Imagine practicing your negotiation skills in a virtual environment. You could interact with virtual characters and receive real-time feedback. This kind of immersive learning could revolutionize how we develop soft skills.

Final ThoughtsImproving your soft skills is a journey, not a destination. Engaging videos and interactive tests are powerful tools that can help you along the way. By combining these resources, you can create a rich learning experience that enhances your communication, teamwork, and problem-solving abilities.Remember, soft skills are essential for success in any field. Take the time to invest in your personal and professional development. With dedication and the right resources, you can become a more effective communicator, a better team player, and a more empathetic leader.
