Build Confidence by Practicing the following Exercises on your own: -

  • Exercise 1 â€“ Call out to a person 200m away
  • Exercise 2 â€“ Announce (Shout) on Shop Floor â€ Factory closed due to heavy rainsâ€
  • Exercise 3 â€“ Read out to your partner who will write on aboard facing away
  • Exercise 4 â€“ Dictate to partner 15 feet away
  • Exercise 5 â€“ Dictate to the whole class
  • Exercise 6 â€“ Read out essay to the class
  • Exercise 7 â€“ Prepare lecture & deliver to 1
  • Exercise 8 â€“ Prepare lecture & deliver to 10
  • Exercise 9 â€“ Extempore Lecture to class
  • Exercise 10 â€“ Motivational Lecture to team
  • Exercise 11 â€“ Introduce a Guest Speaker
  • Exercise 12 â€“ Pacify to Trade Union Leaders
  • Exercise 13 â€“ Convince Boss on a new biz
  • Exercise 14 â€“ Con call of Team with Boss
  • Exercise 15 â€“ Introduce a topic for GD
  • Exercise 16 â€“ Conclude a topic in GD in class
  • Exercise 17 â€“ Extempore Speech on unknown topic -Survival on stage (Tricks) Ask Qs,
  • Exercise 18 â€“ Debate Prepared
  • Exercise 19 â€“ Debate Unprepared
  • Exercise 20 â€“ Conduct a Group Discussion
  • Exercise 21 â€“ Moderate a Brain Storming
  • Exercise 22 â€“ Panel Interview
  • Exercise 23 â€“ Negotiation Skills
  • Exercise 24 â€“ Bullying a subordinate
  • Exercise 25 â€“ Happy Leader projection
  • Exercise 26 â€“ Try Suddenly Losing Temper
  • Exercise 27 -Cornering an over smart subordinate
  • Exercise 28 â€“ Threatening termination
  • Exercise 29 â€“ Organizing a seminar
  • Exercise 30 â€“ Introducing a Speaker
  • Exercise 31 â€“ Giving a farewell speech
  • Exercise 32 â€“ Addressing your Department for the first time

Master Public Speaking with Short Videos and Practical Exercises

Introduction to Public SpeakingPublic speaking can be a daunting task for many. The thought of standing in front of an audience, sharing your ideas, and hoping to engage them can be nerve-wracking. However, mastering this skill is essential in both personal and professional life. In this blog post, we will explore how short videos and practical exercises can help you become a confident and effective public speaker.Public speaking is not just about delivering a speech. It involves connecting with your audience, conveying your message clearly, and leaving a lasting impression. Fortunately, with the right tools and techniques, anyone can improve their public speaking skills.

In this course we will cover:

  • The importance of public speaking
  • How short videos can enhance your skills
  • Practical exercises to practice
  • Tips for effective public speaking

The Importance of Public SpeakingPublic speaking is a vital skill in todayâ€™s world. Whether you are presenting in a meeting, giving a toast at a wedding, or speaking at a conference, the ability to communicate effectively can open many doors.Here are a few reasons why public speaking is important:

  • Career Advancement: Strong public speaking skills can lead to promotions and new job opportunities. Employers value employees who can communicate ideas clearly.
  • Building Confidence: Speaking in front of others can boost your self-esteem. The more you practice, the more confident you will become.
  • Influencing Others: Public speaking allows you to share your ideas and persuade others. Whether you are advocating for a cause or selling a product, effective communication is key.
  • Networking Opportunities: Speaking engagements can help you meet new people and expand your professional network.

How Short Videos Can Enhance Your SkillsIn the digital age, short videos have become a popular medium for learning. Platforms like YouTube and TikTok offer a wealth of resources for aspiring public speakers. Hereâ€™s how you can use short videos to improve your skills:

  • Learn from Experts: Many experienced speakers share their tips and techniques through short videos. Watching these can provide valuable insights into effective speaking styles.
  • Analyze Body Language: Videos allow you to observe body language and gestures. Pay attention to how speakers use their movements to engage the audience.
  • Practice with Feedback: Record yourself giving a speech and compare it to videos of skilled speakers. This can help you identify areas for improvement.
  • Inspiration: Short videos can inspire you to find your unique speaking style. Seeing different approaches can spark creativity in your presentations.

Practical Exercises to PracticeWhile watching videos is helpful, nothing beats hands-on practice. Here are some practical exercises to enhance your public speaking skills:

1. Mirror PracticeStand in front of a mirror and practice your speech. This exercise helps you become aware of your body language and facial expressions.

  • Tip: Smile and maintain eye contact with your reflection. This will help you feel more comfortable when speaking to an audience.

2. Record YourselfUse your smartphone to record a practice speech. Playback the video to evaluate your performance.

  • Tip: Pay attention to your tone, pace, and clarity. Make notes on areas to improve.

3. Join a Speaking GroupConsider joining a local Toastmasters club or a similar organization. These groups provide a supportive environment to practice public speaking.

  • Tip: Participate in various roles, such as speaker, evaluator, or timer. This will give you a well-rounded experience.

4. Impromptu SpeakingChallenge yourself with impromptu speaking exercises. Pick a random topic and speak about it for one minute without preparation.

  • Tip: This exercise helps you think on your feet and improves your ability to articulate thoughts quickly.

5. Audience EngagementPractice engaging your audience by asking questions or encouraging participation.

  • Tip: Use storytelling to make your points relatable. Personal stories can create a connection with your audience.

Incorporating these exercises into your routine will help you build confidence and improve your public speaking skills.

Tips for Effective Public Speaking

Know Your AudienceUnderstanding your audience is crucial. Tailor your message to their interests and needs.

  • Tip: Research your audience beforehand. This will help you connect with them on a personal level.

Organize Your ContentStructure your speech with a clear introduction, body, and conclusion.

  • Tip: Use bullet points or an outline to keep your thoughts organized. This will help you stay on track during your presentation.

Practice, Practice, PracticeRehearse your speech multiple times. The more you practice, the more comfortable you will become.

  • Tip: Practice in front of friends or family to get constructive feedback.

Manage AnxietyItâ€™s normal to feel nervous before speaking. Use deep breathing techniques to calm your nerves.

  • Tip: Visualize a successful presentation. This can help reduce anxiety and boost your confidence.

Use Visual AidsIncorporate visual aids, such as slides or props, to enhance your presentation.

  • Tip: Ensure your visuals are clear and relevant to your message. Avoid cluttered slides that can distract your audience.

By following these tips, you can enhance your public speaking skills and make a lasting impression on your audience.

Putting It All TogetherMastering public speaking takes time and practice. By utilizing short videos and engaging in practical exercises, you can develop your skills effectively.Remember to focus on your audience, organize your content, and practice regularly. With dedication and effort, you can become a confident and effective public speaker.Practicing public speaking in front of a mirror can boost confidence and improve skills.As you embark on your public speaking journey, keep in mind that everyone starts somewhere. Embrace the learning process and celebrate your progress.Public speaking is not just about delivering a message; it is about connecting with others and sharing your unique voice. So, take a deep breath, step onto that stage, and let your voice be heard!

ADVICE BEFORE STARTING PUBLIC SPEAKING TRAINING

  • Watch Video on necessity

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Cjs7dyzLVco

  • Most important to rise in career

MENTAL PREPARATION BEFORE YOU START

  • FIRST THING -Stop finding ways to escape.
  • NO HURRY â€“ TAKE TWO WEEKS !!! Before you start you have to psyche yourself !!! Take your time for this !!!
  • ONCE FOR ALL DECIDE!!! There is no way you can avoid learning this skill as it will cost your whole career. Get into a room alone and speak loudly to yourself. Take your time to force your mind to believe that YOU CAN DO IT.
  • You donâ€™t have to be great at the English language to be a good speaker.
  • No one is a born speaker. All the great speakers have worked hard at this. To some it is easier in the beginning. But if you decide to work hard â€“ There is no way you can fail.
  • IF YOU CAN DO WELL FOR THE FIRST TWO MINUTES ON STAGE â€“ YOU WILL SURVIVE. Hence NEVER NEVER get on stage for the first time unless you are fully prepared â€“ Practice, Practice, Practice!!!

1st Steps

  • STEPS BEFORE FIRST TIME ON STAGE :-
  • STEP 1 Best is to start with a bed time story to your son or any child in your mother tongue and then the same story in English.
  • STEP 2 To gain confidence and to prove to yourself practice in front of your friends or family â€“ First in your mother tongue. Second time in English. Do this till you are confident. The arrangement should be as follows:-
  • You and your friends should be sitting on chairs and you should have script in hand
  • Speak casually to one friend at a time.
  • Psyche yourself and Pretend to be confident
  • Despite all this you will definitely panic and forget what to say next!!!
  • Take a deep breath, smile at your friends.
  • To buy time â€“ ask a Question or an opinion or tell someone to â€œSummarize what you have grasped so far.
  • Look at the script in style and continue.
  • STEP 3 Write the whole script again in your own words. Use only short sentences and only words you are very familiar with. Include a story or an experience if possible
  • STEP 4 Highlight the Key words in YELLOW â€“ Make a separate list of these KEY WORDS in a small card and pin it to your script.
  • STEP 5 You should have memorized the first 20 lines.
  • STEP 6 Rehearsed at least 7 times before a Mirror.
  • STEP 7 On the day of the presentation go at least 30 minutes before the event and mentally get used to the environment. Have a spare copy of your Start or Opening
  • A question
  • A newsworthy incident
  • A startling statement
  • A quote
  • A human interest story
  • Elevator Pitch
  • Clear
  • Catchy
  • Creating impact
  • Tell them what you are going to tell and how long
  • Use facts/analogies / statistics / opinions (provide reasons for the same).

HOW TO START YOUR PRESENTATION.

  • Walk up to the rostrum briskly (watching your steps) and place your script on it. Keep the Key Wordsâ€™ Card in your shirt pocket.
  • Wait for the Audience to settle down before you start speaking.
  • Speak your first 3 sentence and then take a deep breath.
  • THREE THINGS WILL DEFINITELY HAPPEN!!
  1. FIRST THING â€“ You will forget your script
  2. SECOND THING Your heart will start pounding
  3. THIRD THING You will panic.
  • THIS IS THE MOMENT YOU NEED TO COLLECT YOURSELF â€“
  1. Take a deep breath. DO NOT LOOK UP
  2. Take the Key Word Card. Look at in style. DO NOT HIDE IT.
  3. Have a glass of water while looking at the Key Word Card.
  4. If you feel confident â€“ Then restart. No harm mixing languages
  5. If you donâ€™t feel confident JUST TAKE OUT THE SCRIPT AND START READING.
  6. Keep reading and you will feel confident in about 30 seconds.
  7. If still not confident continue reading till you feel confident.
  8. Once you come back do it alone again and again till you feel confident.

DOs & DONâ€™Ts

DOs

  • Have direct eye contact with an individual at a time.
  • Speak to one individual at a time
  • Shift to another individual somewhere else randomly & NEVER LOOK at the Floor, OR at the ceiling NOT in thin air
  • USE SHORT SENTANCES AND WORDS YOU USE IN CONVERSATION
  • Speak deliberately
  • Talk loudly. Do not scream
  • Face the Audience and then speak & not while looking at slides or while writing on Black Board
  • Use your natural accent and never try to copy someone else

DONâ€™Ts

  • Never insult someone. Never corner, joke about or embarrass a person.
  • Never beat your own drums
  • Never speak to fast â€“ be slightly slower than your natural speed
  • Never jump to answer a Question from the audience â€” Throw it back â€“ ask 2 â€“ compile
  • Never speak with your back to audience â€“ pointing or writing.
  • Never read. Note important points â€“ Highlight â€“ likely to forget
  • Never apologize â€“ Keep going -Donâ€™t call attention to worst
  • After you have finished your speech pause briefly, take a couple of steps back and then return to your seat slowly
  • Never wink and show the relief as if you have escaped.
  • Never run after you finish â€“ After you have finished your speech pause briefly, take a couple of steps back and then return to your seat slowly
  • Never try to impress using big words. â€“ try to simplify and make it understandable
  • Never try manipulating the thought process of the Audience
  • Avoid Mumbling, Reading, Filler Words, Looking Down, Panning, Looking at the roof.
  • Avoid Overshooting Time Allotment
  • Avoid Shouting but it is better than being too soft

BEST WAYS TO OPEN A SPEECHOPENING 60 seconds is most critical. Your opening should be Clear, Catchy & Creating an impact. It can be any of the following :-

  • A question
  • A newsworthy incident
  • A startling statement
  • A quote
  • A human interest story
  • Elevator Pitch

SURVIVAL KIT WHEN MIND GOES BLANK (Must practice before hand)

  • Take a deep breath â€“ smile â€“ donâ€™t show panic
  • Look at your notes boldly (Donâ€™t hide the fact that you have forgotten your script)
  • Buy time by asking the audience for comments / questions
  • Giving your experience
  • Tell a story

EXERCISES TO IMPROVE PUBLIC SPEAKING SKILLS

  • Exercise 1 Call out to a person 200m away
  • Exercise 2 Announce (Shout) on Shop Floor â€ Factory closed due to heavy rainsâ€
  • Exercise 3 Read out to your partner who will write facing away from each other
  • Exercise 4 Give a Dictation to your partner standing 15 feet away
  • Exercise 5 Dictation to whole class
  • Exercise 6 Read out your essay to the class
  • Exercise 7 Prepare a lecture and deliver to one
  • Exercise 8 Prepare lecture and deliver to class
  • Exercise 9 Extempore Lecture to class
  • Exercise 10 Motivational Lecture
  • Exercise 11 Organizing Lecture
  • Exercise 12 Speak to Trade Union Leaders to pacify them
  • Exercise 13 Speak to Boss and convince him that we need to start a new business
  • Exercise 14 Conference call with 3 Departmental Heads
  • Exercise 15 Introduce and Present a topic for discussion to the class
  • Exercise 15 Introduce and Present a topic for discussion to the class
  • Exercise 16 Extempore Speech on unknown topic -Survival on stage (Tricks) Ask Qs, Summarize with e.g.
  • Exercise 17 Debate Prepared
  • Exercise 18 Debate Unprepared
  • Exercise 19 Group Discussions
  • Exercise 20 Conduct Brain Storming Sessions
  • Exercise 21 Panel Interview
  • Exercise 22 Negotiation Skills
  • Exercise 23 Bullying a subordinate
  • Exercise 24 Happy Leader
  • Exercise 25 Suddenly Losing Temper
  • Exercise 26 Cornering a subordinate
  • Exercise 27 Threatening with job or termination
  • Exercise 28 Organizing a seminar
  • Exercise 29 Introducing a Speaker
  • Exercise 30 Giving a farewell speech
  • Exercise 31 Addressing your Department for the first time

Key Points for Presentations

  1. CHOOSE NOT MORE THAN 5 POINTS
  2. Make sure you are clear about the key points that you want to make and repeat and emphasize them in the course of your presentation.
  3. Transition from one point to another should be seamless.
  4. Use facts/analogies / statistics / opinions (provide reasons for the same)
  5. Talk, instead to reading
  6. Stand up & Move around. Make eye contact with your audience & Donâ€™t only look at one side of the room

VISUAL AIDS

  1. Do not use complete sentences. Only bullet points.
  2. Follow the 6 x 6 rule: not more than 6 lines (max) per slide, not more than 6 words per line.
  3. Points should appear one at a time, using animation â€“ helps to elaborate.
  4. Do not use more than three colourâ€™s on your slides. If you have to, then keep the shades the same.
  5. Ensure clear visibility of content through good contrast and big fonts. Dark background, light font.
  6. Use effective titles/headings
  7. Lucid/self explanatory content on slide. If not, elaborate.
  8. Talk to audience and not to the visual. Draw attention whenever you want them to see.
  9. Donâ€™t do the death by power point act. Use flip charts and the white board too.
  10. Have a great last slid and NEVER use Thank You on a slide. Say it.

DRESSING UP

  1. Dress to suit occasion, weather, your personality
  2. Err on the conservative side when uncertain!
  3. Avoid too much jewellery
  4. Hair- neat, combed, gelled
  5. Shave!
  6. Ladies: Pin those dupattas & palloos

GESTURES

  1. Natural
  2. Use gestures to complement your speech
  3. Avoid putting your hands in your pocket
  4. Do not use exaggerated gestures that come up to the level of your face.
  5. Avoid clasping, fig leaf position etc.

Questions HandlingAfter or during a presentation the presenter encourages the audience to ask questions. This greatly improves the quality of the assimilation of the subject matter. Most of these are genuine but some of them may be mischievous or tricky. It is a skill the differentiate these. However it is wise to buy maximum time before you actually answer the question. You should also try first get other members of the audience involved in the effort. However the presenter should be always be in control of the situation. Hence the steps to be followed are as follows:-

  1. Encourage the person to come out with the question and mike.
  2. Let the person ask the question without interruptions. (Never say anything sarcastic or discouraging)
  3. Rephrase the question in your own words and ask the person if that is exactly what he /she meant
  4. After getting the confirmation repeat the question deliberately for the whole audience. EXAMPLE â€“ â€œRohitâ€™s question is â€“ What are the other factors which we should consider to ensure that there are no injuriesâ€- Friends now â€œWho would like this question?â€
  5. Encourage the 2 or 3 members of the audience to give their opinions.
  6. Then consolidate those answers and give your own opinion and bring out the contradictions that have emerged. If the case is not resolved completely then let the person that you will get back to him.
  7. Ask the person if he is satisfied with the answer before you proceed.

THUMB RULES

  • Encourage & take the question from one individual, but answer for all in the audience
  • Address the questioner directly only at the start and end of your response

Closing the Speech

  • Indicate to the audience that you are at the end of the presentation.
  • Ask for doubts and questions
  • Show actions to be taken
  • Summarize main points at the end.
  • Never walk of the stage in a hurry
  • End on a friendly note and thank the audience

Must do Courses / Self Study for Knowledge in

  • Toastersâ€™ Club (Public Speaking)
  • Train the Trainer (Teaching Skills)
  • Microsoft Projects (Project Management)
  • Law
  • Compliance
  • World history
  • Geography
  • Physics
  • Math

Competitive Exams

  • CAT
  • GRE
  • IAS, IFS, IRS, IPS
  • UPSC
  • CDS (Army, Navy, Air Force,)
  • Bank Officers

OBJECTIVE TYPE QUESTIONS: â€“ Normally has 4 Answers to select from

TYPES OF NEGATIVE MARKING

  1. Correct Answer +1, Wrong Answer â€“ 1 â€“ ANSWER ONLY IF YOU ARE SURE
  2. Correct Answer +1, Wrong Answer â€“ 0.5 â€“ ANSWER ONLY IF YOU CAN ELIMINATE 2
  3. Correct Answer +1, Wrong Answer -0.25 â€“ ANSWER EVEN IF YOU CAN ELIMINATE 1
  4. NO Negative Marking â€“ MUST ANSWER ALL QUESTIONS

ASSUMPTION â€“

  • You are sure about (50 out of 100 Questions)
  • You can identify 1 wrong answer (34 out of 100)
  • You can identify 2 wrong answers (16 out of 100)

ESSAY TYPE PAPER:-

Divide Time by number of marks to find number minutes you can spend per mark and multiply by the marks for each Question. Then spend that much on that particular Question.

NOTE :- BASIC AIM IS NOT TO LEAVE ANY QUESTION â€“ WRITE SOMETHING (minimum 10 lines)

Try to use the below format where ever possible:-

CENTER HEADING

Group Heading.These are Group Headings like Introduction, Factors to be Considered, Effects of Factors & Conclusion

IntroductionFor Group Heading have no full stop in the end & the writing starts on the 2 nd line.

If you have multiple Factors here also then you list them under Para Headings as follows:-

3. Cause 1.This is described in sentences starting on the same line

4. Cause 2.Para Heading are in Bold. The writing starts on the next line.

When you have multiple Factors to be considered then under each Para Heading you list them under Sub Para Headings as follows:-

c. Sub Para Heading.

d. Effect of Cause 2:-iii. Sub Sub Para Headingiv. Result 2 is Severe Poverty

NOTE â€“ALL LEVELS OF THE HEADINGS (Group Heading, Para Headings, Sub Para Headings and Sub Para Headings are inBOLD & areUNDERLINED.

Leadership

The best way to understand the Basics of Leadership is to study these 100 DefinitionsLeadership according to Dr Kalamhttps://youtube.com/shorts/mvDVooJ-v44?si=jyN_A6jgPwC16cl-

https://www.instagram.com/reel/DOpIOxYDo1j/?igsh=ZGtvczBzdGVqbjU=

How to handle pressurehttps://www.instagram.com/reel/DN9Q8lKjrHo/?igsh=MW5uNm4yNTJjdG1idA==

https://www.instagram.com/reel/DMacOt0x09H/?igsh=NzhvY2dsMHByYjhi

Best of Saurab Ganguly on Leadershiphttps://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fTdkJTXAAjw

DefinitionsLeadership is the process of influencing the thinking, behavior and efforts of team members towards the achievement of organizational goals.Leadership is a winning combination of personal traits and the ability to think and act as a leader, a person who directs the activities of others for the good of all.â€œLeadership is the art of getting someone else to do something you want done because he wants to do it.â€ â€“ Dwight D. Eisenhower

â€œA leader has the vision and conviction that a dream can be achieved. He inspires the power and energy to get it done.â€ â€“ Ralph Lauren

â€œThe best leaders are those most interested in surrounding themselves with assistants and associates smarter than they are. They are frank in admitting this and are willing to pay for such talents.â€ â€“ Amos Parrishâ€œReason and judgment are the qualities of a leader.â€ â€“ Publius Cornelius Tacitus

100 Answers to the Question: What Is Leadership?

If you Google the word leadership you can get about 479,000,000 results, each definition as unique as an individual leader.

Itâ€™s a difficult concept to define, perhaps because it means so many things to different people.

Here are 100 of the best ways to define leadershipâ€“choose the ones that fits best for you.

1. â€œA leader is best when people barely know he exists, when his work is done, his aim fulfilled, they will say: we did it ourselves.â€ â€“Lao Tzu

2. â€œA good leader takes a little more than his share of the blame, a little less than his share of the credit.â€ â€“Arnold Glasow

3. â€œThe ultimate measure of a man is not where he stands in moments of comfort, but where he stands at times of challenge and controversy.â€ â€“Martin Luther King Jr

4. â€œYou donâ€™t need a title to be a leader.â€ â€“Mark Sanborn

5. â€œIt is better to lead from behind and to put others in front, especially when you celebrate victory when nice things occur. You take the front line when there is danger. Then people will appreciate your leadership.â€ â€“Nelson Mandela

6. â€œLeadership and learning are indispensable to each other.â€ â€“John F. Kennedy

7. â€œThe greatest leader is not necessarily the one who does the greatest things. He is the one that gets the people to do the greatest things.â€ â€“Ronald Reagan

8. â€œSuccessful leadership is leading with the heart, not just the head. They possess qualities like empathy, compassion and courage.â€ â€“Bill George

9. â€œThe task of leadership is not to put greatness into people, but to elicit it, for the greatness is there already.â€ â€“John Buchan

10. â€œA great person attracts great people and knows how to hold them together.â€â€“Johann Wolfgang Von Goethe

11. â€œWhen the leadership is right and the time is right, the people can always be counted upon to followâ€“to the end at all costs.â€ â€“Harold J. Seymour

12. â€œLeaders must be self-reliant individuals with great tenacity and stamina.â€â€“Thomas E. Cronin

13. â€œLeadership: The capacity and will to rally people to a common purpose together with the character that inspires confidence and trust.â€ â€“Bernard Montgomery

14. â€œAll of the great leaders have had one characteristic in common: it was the willingness to confront unequivocally the major anxiety of their people in their time. This, and not much else, is the essence of leadership.â€ â€“John Kenneth Galbraith

15. â€œLeadership is the capacity to translate vision into reality.â€ â€“Warren Bennis

16. â€œLeadership defines what the future should look like, aligns people with that vision, and inspires them to make it happen, despite the obstacles.â€ â€“John Kotter

17. â€ I start with the premise that the function of leadership is to produce more leaders, not more followers.â€ â€“Ralph Nader

18. â€œI think leadership comes from integrityâ€“that you do whatever you ask others to do. I think there are non-obvious ways to lead. Just by providing a good example as a parent, a friend, a neighbor makes it possible for other people to see better ways to do things. Leadership does not need to be a dramatic, fist in the air and trumpets blaring, activity.â€ â€“Scott Berkun

19. â€œLeadership is the capacity to influence others through inspiration motivated by passion, generated by vision, produced by a conviction, ignited by a purpose.â€ â€“Myles Munroe

20. â€œLeadership is unlocking peopleâ€™s potential to become better.â€ â€“Bill Bradley

21. â€œThe art of leadership is saying no, not saying yes. It is very easy to say yes.â€ â€”-Tony Blair

22. â€œEffective leadership is not about making speeches or being liked; leadership is defined by results not attributes.â€ â€“Peter F. Drucker

23. â€œOne measure of leadership is the caliber of people who choose to follow you.â€ â€“Dennis Peer

24. â€œInnovation distinguishes between a leader and a follower.â€ â€“Steve Jobs

25. â€œLeadership is simply causing other people to do what the leaders want. Good leadership, whether formal or informal, is helping other people rise to their full potential while accomplishing the mission and goals of the organization. All members of an organization, who are responsible for the work of others, have the potential to be good leaders if properly developed.â€ â€“Bob Mason

26. â€œLeadership is the art of getting someone else to do something you want done because he wants to do it. â€œâ€“Dwight Eisenhower

27. â€œThe very essence of leadership is that you have to have a vision. Itâ€™s got to be a vision you articulate clearly and forcefully on every occasion. You canâ€™t blow an uncertain trumpet.â€ â€“Theodore Hesburgh

28. â€œLeadership is the art of mobilizing others to want to struggle for shared aspirations.â€ â€“James Kouzes and Barry Posner

29. â€œA leader takes people where they want to go. A great leader takes people where they donâ€™t necessarily want to go, but ought to be.â€ â€“Rosalynn Carter

30. â€œIf your actions inspire others to dream more, learn more, do more and become more, you are a leader.â€ â€” John Quincy Adams

31. â€œLeadership is not a person or a position. It is a complex moral relationship between people, based on trust, obligation, commitment, emotion, and a shared vision of the good.â€ â€“Joanne Ciulla

32. â€œThe challenge of leadership is to be strong, but not rude; be kind, but not weak; be bold, but not bully; be thoughtful, but not lazy; be humble, but not timid; be proud, but not arrogant; have humor, but without folly.â€ â€“Jim Rohn

33. â€œLeadership is lifting a personâ€™s vision to high sights, the raising of a personâ€™s performance to a higher standard, the building of a personality beyond its normal limitations.â€ â€“Peter Drucker

34. â€œLeadership is an opportunity to serve. It is not a trumpet call to self-importance.â€ â€“J. Donald Walters

35. â€œLeadership is a matter of having people look at you and gain confidence, seeing how you react. If youâ€™re in control, theyâ€™re in control.â€ â€“Tom Landry

36. â€œA leader is one who knows the way, goes the way, and shows the way.â€ â€“John Maxwell

37. â€œLeadership is the process of persuasion or example by which an individual (or leadership team) induces a group to pursue objectives held by the leader or shared by the leader and his or her followers.â€â€“John W. Gardner

38. â€œMy definition of a leaderâ€¦ is a man who can persuade people to do what they donâ€™t want to do, or do what theyâ€™re too lazy to do, and like it.â€ â€“Harry S. Truman

39. â€œLeadership is the capacity to translate vision into reality.â€ â€“Warren Bennis

40. â€œA leader is a dealer in hope.â€ â€“Napoleon Bonaparte

41. Leadership is the collective action of everyone you influence. Your behaviorâ€“your actions and your wordsâ€“determines how you influence. Our job as leaders is to energize whatever marshals action within others. â€“David Caullo

42. â€œA leader has to be somebody whoâ€™s getting people to do things which donâ€™t seem to make sense to them or are not in their best interestâ€“like convincing people that they should work 14 hours a day so that someone else can make more money.â€ â€“Scott Adams

43. â€œLeadership is the ability to guide others without force into a direction or decision that leaves them still feeling empowered and accomplished.â€ â€“Lisa Cash Hanson

44. â€œThe task of the leader is to get his people from where they are to where they have not been.â€ â€“Henry Kissinger

45. â€œLeadership is about service to others and a commitment to developing more servants as leaders. It involves co-creation of a commitment to a mission.â€ â€“Robert Greenleaf

46. â€œLeadership is working with and through others to achieve objectives.â€ â€“Paul Hersey

47. â€œManagement is about arranging and telling. Leadership is about nurturing and enhancing.â€ â€“Tom Peters

48. â€œLeadership is a potent combination of strategy and character. But if you must be without one, be without the strategy.â€ â€“Norman Schwarzkopf

49. â€œA leaderâ€™s role is to raise peopleâ€™s aspirations for what they can become and to release their energies so they will try to get there.â€ â€“David R. Gergen

50. â€œLeadership is unlocking peopleâ€™s potential to become better.â€ â€“Bill Bradley

51. â€œEffective leadership is putting first things first. Effective management is discipline, carrying it out.â€ â€“Stephen Covey

52. â€œLeadership is solving problems. The day soldiers stop bringing you their problems is the day you have stopped leading them. They have either lost confidence that you can help or concluded you do not care. Either case is a failure of leadership.â€ â€“Colin Powell

53. â€œLeadership is the key to 99 percent of all successful efforts.â€ â€“Erskine Bowles

54. â€œLeadership is a matter of how to be, not how to do it.â€ â€“Frances Hesselbein

55. â€œLeadership is the ability to establish standards and manage a creative climate where people are self-motivated toward the mastery of long-term constructive goals, in a participatory environment of mutual respect, compatible with personal values.â€ â€“Mike Vance

56. â€œLeadership is getting people to work for you when they are not obligated.â€ â€” Fred Smith

57. â€œOne of the tests of leadership is the ability to recognize a problem before it becomes an emergency.â€ â€“Arnold Glasow

58. â€œLeadership is the art of influencing others to their maximum performance to accomplish any task, objective or project.â€ â€“W.A. Cohen

59. â€œA good leader is a caring leader â€” he not only cares about his people, he actively takes care of them.â€ â€“Harald Anderson

60. â€œThere are almost as many definitions of leadership as there are persons who have attempted to define the concept.â€ â€“Ralph Stogdill

61. â€œThe growth and development of people is the highest calling of leadership.â€ â€“Harvey S. Firestone

62. â€œKeep your fears to yourself, but share your inspiration with others.â€ â€“Robert Louis Stevenson

63. â€œWithout passion, a person will have very little influence as a leader.â€ â€“Michele Payn-Knoper

64. â€œLeadership is an intangible quality with no clear definition. Thatâ€™s probably a good thing, because if the people who were being led knew the definition, they would hunt down their leaders and kill them.â€ â€“Scott Adams.

65. â€œLeadership is doing what is right when no one is watching.â€ â€“George Van Valkenburg

66. â€œLeadership is someone who demonstrates whatâ€™s possible.â€ â€“Mark Yarnell

67. â€œLeadership is practiced not so much in words as in attitude and in actions.â€â€“Harold Geneen

68. â€œNever tell people how to do things. Tell them what to do and they will surprise you with their ingenuity.â€ â€“George Smith Patton

69. â€œLeadership by example is the only kind of real leadership. Everything else is dictatorship.â€ â€“Albert Emerson

70. â€œThe leader is one who mobilizes others toward a goal shared by leaders and followers. â€¦ Leaders, followers and goals make up the three equally necessary supports for leadership.â€ â€“Gary Wills

71. â€œThe leader must know, must know that he knows, and must be able to make it abundantly clear to those around him that he knows.â€ â€“Clarence Randall

72. â€œLeadership is about taking responsibility and not making excuses.â€ â€“Mitt Romney

73. â€œLeadership is difficult but it is not complex.â€ â€“Michael McKinney

74. â€œGreat leadership is about human experiences, not processes. Leadership is not a formula or a program, it is a human activity that comes from the heart and considers the hearts of others.â€ â€“Lance Secretan

75. â€œLeadership is a process whereby an individual influences a group of individuals to achieve a common goal.â€ â€“P.G. Northouse

76. â€œFollowers are the gem cutters of leadership coaxing out its full brilliance.â€ â€“Ira Chaleff

77. â€œA leader cannot lead until he knows where he is going.â€ â€“Anonymous

78. â€œLeaders arenâ€™t born, they are made.â€ â€•Vince Lombardi

79. â€œThe final test of a leader is that he leaves behind him in other men, the conviction and the will to carry on.â€ â€“Walter Lippmann

80. â€œThe function of a leader within any institution: to provide that regulation through his or her non-anxious, self-defined presence.â€ â€“Edwin H. Friedman

81. â€œThe greatness of a leader is measured by the achievements of the led. This is the ultimate test of his effectiveness.â€ â€“Omar Bradley

82. â€œThe leadership instinct you are born with is the backbone. You develop the funny bone and the wishbone that go with it.â€ â€“Elaine Agather

83. â€œThe best way to lead people into the future is to connect with them deeply in the present.â€ â€“James Kouzes and Barry Posner

84. â€œLeadership consists of picking good men and helping them do their best.â€ â€” Chester W. Nimitz

85. â€œTo get others to come into our ways of thinking, we must go over to theirs; and it is necessary to follow, in order to lead.â€ â€“William Hazlitt

86. â€œLeadership requires using power to influence the thoughts and actions of other people.â€ â€“A. Zalenik

87. â€œThe mark of a great man is one who knows when to set aside the important things in order to accomplish the vital ones.â€ â€“Brandon Sanderson

88. â€œOur work is our most important resource to develop our people.â€ â€“Jim Trinka and Les Wallace

89. â€œSuccessful leaders see the opportunities in every difficulty rather than the difficulty in every opportunity.â€ â€“Reed Markham

90. â€œThe most important thing about a commander is his effect on morale.â€ â€“Viscount Slim

91. â€œWhile a good leader sustains momentum, a great leader increases it.â€ â€“John C. Maxwell

92. â€œThe mediocre teacher tells. The good teacher explains. The superior teacher demonstrates. The great teacher inspires.â€ â€“William Arthur Ward

93. â€œHe who cannot be a good follower cannot be a good leader.â€ â€•Aristotle

94. â€œFor me, leadership is making a difference. Itâ€™s using your agency to bring about change.â€ â€“Melanne Verveer

95. â€œThat is what leadership is all about: staking your ground ahead of where opinion is and convincing people, not simply following the popular opinion of the moment.â€â€“Doris Kearns Goodwin

96. â€œThe way I would measure leadership is this: of the people that are working with me, how many wake up in the morning thinking that the company is theirs?â€ â€“David M. Kelley

97. â€œYou donâ€™t lead by pointing and telling people some place to go. You lead by going to that place and making a case.â€ â€“Ken Kesey

98. â€œNot the cry, but the flight of a wild duck, leads the flock to fly and follow.â€ â€“Chinese Proverb

99. â€œTo command is to serve, nothing more and nothing less.â€ â€“Andre Malraux

100. â€œLeadership is leading people with your whole heart.â€ â€“ Lolly Daskal

N A T I O N A L B E S T S E L L E R

THE LEADERSHIP GAPWhat Gets Between You and Your Greatness

After decades of coaching powerful executives around the world, Lolly Daskal has observed that leaders rise to their positions relying on a specific set of values and traits. But in time, every executive reaches a point when their performance suffers and failure persists. Very few understand why or how to prevent it.

Additional Reading you might enjoy:

â€¢12 Successful Leadership Principles That Never Grow Oldâ€¢A Leadership Manifesto: A Guide To Greatnessâ€¢How to Succeed as A New Leaderâ€¢12 of The Most Common Lies Leaders Tell Themselvesâ€¢4 Proven Reasons Why Intuitive Leaders Make Great Leadersâ€¢The One Quality Every Leader Needs To Succeedâ€¢The Deception Trap of Leadership

Lolly Daskal is one of the most sought-after executive leadership coaches in the world. Her extensive cross-cultural expertise spans 14 countries, six languages and hundreds of companies. As founder and CEO of Lead From Within, her proprietary leadership program is engineered to be a catalyst for leaders who want to enhance performance and make a meaningful difference in their companies, their lives, and the world.

Of Lollyâ€™s many awards and accolades, Lolly was designated a Top-50 Leadership and Management Expert by Inc. magazine. Huffington Post honored Lolly with the title of The Most Inspiring Woman in the World. Her writing has appeared in HBR, Inc.com, Fast Company (Ask The Expert), Huffington Post, and Psychology Today, and others. Her newest book, The Leadership Gap: What Gets Between You and Your Greatness has become a national bestseller.

Six Traits of Effective Leaders

  1. Make others feel important
  2. Promote a vision
  3. Follow the golden rule
  4. Admit mistakes
  5. Criticize others only in private
  6. Stay close to the action

â€“ Christian Nevell Bovee

STEPS TO BECOME A LEADER

  1. Get your employees to want to do their job. Avoid controlling their every move.
  2. Share your vision, enthusiasm and energy
  3. Motivate employees with tangible rewards & your concerns for their wellbeing & progress
  4. Be accessible and transparent
  5. Be strong and effective
  6. Be a role model
  7. Avoid exploiting your position
  8. Find & Take full advantage of the skills and talents of your staff
  9. Give credit and take the blame care of yourself

YOU MUST SHIFT YOUR STYLE FROM TRADITIONAL LEADERSHIP â€“ TO â€“ COLLABORATIVE LEADERSHIP

  1. Belief that Power comes from Designation â€“ TO â€“ Power is greatest in building Teamwork
  2. Secretive â€“ Maintains Ownership of information â€“ TO â€“ Transparent sharing of Info & Knowledge
  3. Non participative management â€“ TO â€“ Inviting Suggestions and Ideas
  4. Top â€“ Down Strategy â€“ TO â€“ Bottom â€“ Up and Democratic Brainstorming Style
  5. Focus on Execution Process & Efficiency â€“ TO â€“ Allow Flexibility & encourage Innovation & Risk taking
  6. Resolve problems Firefighting with focus on Symptoms â€“ TO â€“ Focus on Root Cause Analysis and prevention
  7. Annual Performance Review â€“ TO â€“ Provide continuous Feedback & Personal Coaching

Written Test - Leadership Position(Objective & Essay Type)

OBJECTIVE TYPE QUESTIONS : â€“ Normally has 4 Answers to select from

STRATEGY FOR ANSWERING TYPES OF NEGATIVE MARKING

  1. Correct Answer +1, Wrong Answer â€“ 1 â€“ ANSWER ONLY IF YOU ARE SURE
  2. Correct Answer +1, Wrong Answer â€“ 0.5 â€“ ANSWER ONLY IF YOU CAN ELIMINATE 2
  3. Correct Answer +1, Wrong Answer -0.25 â€“ ANSWER EVEN IF YOU CAN ELIMINATE 1
  4. NO Negative Marking â€“ MUST ANSWER ALL QUESTIONS

ASSUMPTION â€“

You are sure about (50 out of 100 Questions)You can identify 1 wrong answer (34 out of 100)You can identify 2 wrong answers (16 out of 100)

ESSAY TYPE PAPER:-

Divide Time by number of marks to find number minutes you can spend per mark and multiply by the marks for each Question. Then spend that much on that particular Question.

NOTE :- BASIC AIM IS NOT TO LEAVE ANY QUESTION â€“ WRITE SOMETHING (minimum 10 lines)

Try to use the below format where ever possible:-

CENTER HEADING

Group Heading.

These are Group Headings like Introduction, Factors to be Considered, Effects of Factors & Conclusion

Introduction

For Group Heading have no full stop in the end & the writing starts on the 2 nd line. If you have multiple Factors here also then you list them under Para Headings as follows:-

  1. Cause 1. This is described in sentences starting on the same line
  2. Cause 2. Para Heading are in Bold. The writing starts on the next line. When you have multiple Factors to be considered then under each Para Heading you list them under Sub Para Headings as follows:-a.Sub Para Heading.b.Effect of Cause 2:-i.Sub Sub Para Headingii.Result 2 is Severe Poverty

NOTE â€“

ALL LEVELS OF THE HEADINGS (Group Heading, Para Headings, Sub Para Headings and Sub Para Headings are in BOLD & are underlined.

Exam Shortcuts

Read SyllabusHighlight When ReadingSpeech Notes (mobile app) to dictate into (to get Notes)2 to 5 Practice Papers must be done with proper Time ManagementNever leave any Question Blank except in Objective Tests where Negative marking is more HALF

Studying for Retention

â€¢ Highlight or underline as you are reading.â€¢ Write important points / new words in the marginâ€¢ Read Preface, Executive Summary and about the Author before you start the book.â€¢ First run through the index.â€¢ After you finish each chapter dictate the main points into a voice dictation software like Speech Notes (Android Play Store)â€¢ Carry out an exercise or project to use that knowledge practically within 1 week.

Salary

It is best to avoid this question about your current salary during the first interview. However if it is asked again it should be given correctly â€“ otherwise it will create problems later. If asked what your current expectations you can safely ask for an increase of 30%.

CAREER SETTLING DOWN

â€¢ Most Important subject (both personal & professional).â€¢ Not taught but learned (eg Gandhi).â€¢ Not theory but practical.â€¢ Most important to understand the reporting structure and the â€œNorms for communication laid down in a company & Rules for the class â€ These are not normally written down. You must read the HR Manual and the Employee Hand book thoroughly. Ask the HR to brief you in detail.â€¢ Find a buddy who has been in the organization for over 2 years and take his help to understand the internal politics and power struggles and avoid them.â€¢ Understand the etiquettes of not only speaking but also written, e mail, phone call, body language that is established.

Boss Handling

â€¢ Donâ€™t try to get too close too soonâ€¢ Take notes and see that you complete assignments before timeâ€¢ Discuss problems and obstacles directly with the boss well before the time line.â€¢ Be honest and ask for training as soon as a task is assigned to you.â€¢ Go well prepared for meetings and show that you fit into his team culturally and competence wiseâ€¢ Never speak behind any oneâ€™s back.â€¢ Show enthusiasm and energy and willingness to take bigger responsibilities.

Building a reputation

â€¢ Project a business like personality with great dependability and hardworking natureâ€¢ Donâ€™t try to please everyone or get too close to seniorsâ€¢ Show focus to adhere to time lines.â€¢ Be honest and show willingness to learn.â€¢ Show that you are a good team man and a good cultural fitâ€¢ Show dislike for gossip and politics.â€¢ Show enthusiasm and energy and willingness to take bigger responsibilities.

Handling Office Politics

â€¢ Donâ€™t try form groupsâ€¢ Never try to corner any oneâ€¢ Discuss personal problems in private with your boss only.â€¢ Be honest and never try to manipulate.â€¢ Understand the power structure and the tendencies of each person and the groupism.â€¢ Never speak behind any oneâ€™s back.

Soft Skills

Soft Skills TrainingMaster the art of successful interactions!Enhance your interpersonal skills with our comprehensive Soft Skills Training course. From communication and teamwork to problem-solving and adaptability, gain the tools necessary for personal and professional success.

â€¢ Communication. (Written and Verbal) are of utmost importance in the workplace because they set the tone for how people perceive you.â€¢ Teamwork.â€¢ Adaptability.â€¢ Problem solving &Critical observation.â€¢ Conflict resolution.â€¢ Strong Work Ethic.â€¢ Positive Attitude.â€¢ Good Communication Skills.â€¢ Time Management Abilities.â€¢ Problem-Solving Skills.â€¢ Acting as a Team Player.â€¢ Self-Confidence.â€¢ Ability to Accept and Learn From Criticism.â€¢ Leadership Skills. Companies want employees who can supervise and direct other workers.â€¢ Problem Solving Skills.â€¢ Work Ethic. â€¦

TYPES OF COMMUNICATION

Only 7 % of communication happens through words and 93% of communication happens through non-verbal cues of which:

â€¢55% through facial expressionsâ€¢38% through vocal tones

VERBAL COMMUNICATION

  1. Conversations
  2. Discussions
  3. Telephonic discussions
  4. Video Conference

NON VERBAL COMMUNICATION

  1. â€“ Communication is far more than what you say. Itâ€™s how you say. Body Language is â€œHow you say itâ€. It involves intrapersonal communication, understanding yourself and participating in effective self-communication
  2. Body language includes :-

â€¢ Kinesics, Proxemics & Paralanguageâ€¢ Intentionâ€¢ Manner: directness, sincerityâ€¢ Dress and clothing (style, color, Appropriateness for situation)â€¢ Signs & Symbols.

INTERESTING THINGS ABOUT BODY LANGUAGE

â€¢ It has no words or sentences, but it does send bits of information that combine into messages.â€¢ Those messages, which are sometimes clear and sometimes fuzzy, are mostly about your feelings.â€¢ People can learn to read those messages with a fair degree of accuracy.â€¢ You cannot not have body language- you are sending messages nonverbally all the time. Especially when you are trying not to!â€¢ Your preferred body positions and movements do say something about the kind of person you are.â€¢ If your words say one thing and your body another then people will believe your body, not your words.â€¢ You can change how youâ€™re feeling by consciously changing your body language.

COMMUNICATION SECRETS

â€¢ Effective and persuasive communication is the greatest of all the keys to success.â€¢ Success = Talking so people listen and listening so people talkâ€¢ People are attracted to the people who make them feel secure, free and happy.â€¢ By making others feel special; they will realize how special you are.â€¢ How do you inspire people to communicate your point of view?â€¢ How do you encourage people in your life who currently ignore your ideas may reconsider and take notice?â€¢ What simple things can you do so people will pay attention to what U have to say at home, at work, among professional circles ?

ACTIVE LISTENING

â€¢ Itâ€™s about listening and responding and the act of mutually disclosing inner feelings and thoughts to others. Listening goes beyond attentively waiting for other people to stop talking. It really means getting inside of their hearts and minds and experiencing life situationsâ€¢ Listen for concepts, key ideas and facts.â€¢ Be able to distinguish between evidence and argument, idea and example, fact and principle.â€¢ Analyze the key pointsâ€¢ Look for unspoken messages in the speakerâ€™s tone of voice or expressionsâ€¢ Keep an open mind.â€¢ Ask questions that clarify.â€¢ Reserve judgment until the speaker has finishedâ€¢ Take meaningful notes that are brief and to the pointâ€¢ Avoid distractionsâ€¢ Do not interrupt unnecessarilyâ€¢ Be active (show interest)â€¢ Paraphrase what youâ€™ve heardâ€¢ Throw an echo

BODY LANGUAGE OF AN ACTIVE LISTENER

â€¢ The Listener keeps looking at the speakerâ€¢ The Listenerâ€™s body is in â€˜openâ€™ positionâ€¢ The listener is smiling with a pleasant & encouraging expressionâ€¢ Listener looks relaxed but alert, neither tense nor slouchingâ€¢ Listener utters humming sounds

WHILE SPEAKING OVER PHONE

â€¢ Write down in advance what you want to say and in what orderâ€¢ Smileâ€¢ Speak slowlyâ€¢ Always be polite and friendlyâ€¢ For long messages, follow a scriptâ€¢ Monitor your timeâ€¢ Be clear and concise (tone, accent, emphasis, pronunciation)â€¢ Cite negative opinions honestly, but in a positive mannerâ€¢ Seek Feedback

Social skills

PARTY ETTIQUETTE

â€¢ If Pick up is necessary then be there atleast 15 minutes earlyâ€¢ Sitting in car â€“ Open the door for the lady normally left rear seat. (No need to do that for a male. If the lady is alone then you must sit in the right side. If it is a couple then you should sit in the co driverâ€™s seat. Avoid looking into the rear view mirror.â€¢ Speaking to Driver should be polite and business like. Try using a language known to the guestâ€¢ Address the lady as Mrs. ___ or Madamâ€¢ When you reach the destination get out first â€“ walk around and open the Car door for lady.â€¢ Walking abreast is best â€“ leading is also okâ€¢ Avoid touching â€“ it can be misunderstoodâ€¢ It is good to introduce your guests and offer the first drink.â€¢ Holding Glass to help is ok. But donâ€™t do it too oftenâ€¢ Avoid winking & staring. It can be misunderstood.â€¢ Laughing too much is not goodâ€¢ Stopping conversation & guiding outâ€¢ Avoid hanging on to Top peopleâ€¢ Must take time to speak to your juniors & the hostâ€¢ Avoid speaking with food in your mouth or while Proposing a toastâ€¢ Should prepare a Short speech well in advanceâ€¢ Organizing a party a multi â€“ course dinnerâ€¢ Leading to table 7 pulling a chair for a lady is goodâ€¢ Napkin, forks, knifes, wiping handsâ€¢ Belching, coughing, sneezing, scratching head, combing hair, mining gold, yawning, loud speech, speaking without target hearing, giggling, ganging upâ€¢ Soup, Water, Finger bowl should be handled carefullyâ€¢ Salt, pepper should be done carefully without affecting the person sitting next to youâ€¢ Serving & passing bowls should be done promptlyâ€¢ Pushing back the chairâ€¢ Leading to husband.â€¢ Thanking the lady of the house, cook waiters after the meal is a polite thing to do.â€¢ Tips, speaking on behalf all guests.â€¢ Short speechâ€¢ Leading the guest to the table and offering a plate is acceptable. But donâ€™t try to serve the guest. Let the waiter do that.â€¢ Napkin should be used for Wiping hands or face. Do not use it as a hand kerchiefâ€¢ Water & Finger bowl should be asked only from the waiter.â€¢ Soup / Tea sipping without noiseâ€¢ Serving food for the person sitting to you is ok. Not for the one sitting opposite you.

Negotiations

â€¢ Take clear boundaries from the management before you go for negitiationsâ€¢ Never lead. Listen to the other party first.â€¢ Take notes and see that you want complete the assignments before timeâ€¢ At all times be prepared to walk outâ€¢ Final rate given is never the last rateâ€¢ Play other parties and take assurances in writing if possible.â€¢ Negotiate from a position of strength with many alternatives ready.â€¢ Never show joy or regret.â€¢ Never give false commitments.â€¢ Why Sales is importanthttps://www.instagram.com/reel/DPL-OJOiAYk/?igsh=MXkwZXh4ZHByd2Vh

Networking

GETTING AN INTERVIEW CALL (RULES)

â€¢ Best place to start is your mobile phone contacts and your email / Facebook / LinkedIn contacts.â€¢ Ask your friends, Family, teachers, mentors to help you.â€¢ Make a personal connection with everyone you contact.â€¢ Speak in your own voice and words.â€¢ Keep track of every contact and schedule your follow-up calls.â€¢ Save mobile no & email the first chance you get.â€¢ Walk around when you make the calls.â€¢ Describe what youâ€™re looking for in detail.â€¢ Ask for what you want specifically.â€¢ Commit to making a few calls every day.

ETTIQUETTE (TELEPHONE)

â€¢ Answering the callâ€¢Answer the call within 2-3 ringsâ€¢Greeting as per time of the dayâ€¢Top Security - May I Help You Please.â€¢May I Know Who is Calling Sir / Madamâ€¢Be cheerful while speaking

â€¢ Body of the Callâ€¢Listen carefully to the Callerâ€¢Take permission to hold and announce the transferâ€¢ When returning to the Caller remember to Thank him / her for holding the Call.â€¢Take accurate notes of addresses, date, time, telephone numbers and figures of amounts etc repeating back to recheck where necessaryâ€¢Avoid negative phrases like â€“ I donâ€™t know.. Instead you can say Please let me find out.â€¢Be aware of your tone and politeness

â€¢ Ending the Callâ€¢Inform the caller the action you will be taking to resolve their problem.â€¢Thank you for callingâ€¢It was a pleasure speaking to you.â€¢I am very Glad you calledâ€¢Please feel free to call back in case you have any clarifications or problems in future.â€¢Good bye Sir / Madam

Teaching

The skills needed for effective teaching involve more than just expertise in an academic field. It is not an easy job.

â€¢ You must be able to interact with people and help them underrstand a new way of looking at a topic or at the world as a whole.â€¢ The main function of a teacher is to prese.nt the topic in the best way so that it is easy to understand for the students depending on their level of awareness.â€¢ Over simplifying is ok but the other way is not ok.â€¢ Good teachers â€“ the take pains to prepare themselves well, they set clear and fair expectationsâ€¢ They are good motivators.â€¢ The start with the over all macro level understanding of the subject and then get to the nuts and bolts.â€¢ They always make a list of points to remember.â€¢ They have a possitive attitude, are patient and never riducule students or make fun of them.â€¢ The act as role models and have a mentoring attitude.â€¢ They find multiple ways of explaininga point and give real life examples and use training aids.â€¢ They show enthusiasm and commitment.â€¢ The use simple language and words which the students understand.â€¢ They are impartial.

Team Building

â€¢ Be fair.â€¢ Interview in detail before selectionâ€¢ Get good attitude and good competenceâ€¢ Look for cultural fitâ€¢ Maintain balanced distance as a leader as the situation demandsâ€¢ Be transparentâ€¢ Show your personal energy and competenceâ€¢ Have clear expectations.â€¢ Maintain good communications within the team.â€¢ Never play one against the other.â€¢ Give credit for success but take the blame.â€¢ Delegate and coach adequately.â€¢ Have professional revenues and encourage accountability.â€¢ Counsel alone but appreciate in front of othersâ€¢ Never speak behind any oneâ€™s back.â€¢ Show enthusiasm and energy and willingness to take bigger loads.

Motivating

â€¢ Lead by exampleâ€¢ Hold team members in high esteemâ€¢ Trust fullyâ€¢ Delegate and empowerâ€¢ Discuss problems and obstacles and coach how to overcome them.â€¢ Be honest and focus on training.â€¢ Share information freely and give them the bigger picture and the visionâ€¢ Show enthusiasm and energy and willingness to take bigger responsibilities.

TRAINING CARRIED FOR MBA BATCHES

  1. Introduction and importance of communication
  2. Emergency announcements on shop floor(for rains/blast)
  3. Talk to late comers (genuine and naughty late comers)
  4. Stance and what to do with ones arms while speaking
  5. How to give a motivation lecture
  6. Talk about yourself
  7. Listening skills
  8. Eye to eye contact in public speaking
  9. Gestures, mannerism and being yourself
  10. Leadership skills â€“ Leadership is the process of interpersonal influence over the activities of team members towards the achievement of organisational goals in a given situation. In contemporary organisational life, managers must need to work effectively with peers, supervisors and subordinates. Understanding self and influencing others forms an integral part of this endeavor.
  11. How loud one should speak
  12. Formal dressing
  13. Reading and writing in pairs (back to back)
  14. Reading aloud to class
  15. Short write up on subject of ones choice and presenting it
  16. Introduction to group discussion
  17. How to prepare a c.v.
  18. What is assessor looking for
  19. What are the opportunities in GD.
  20. How to introduce a topic
  21. How to butt in
  22. How to manage conflict
  23. How to conclude and sum up
  24. What is an interview and how to prepare for it
  25. Emotional preparation for an interview
  26. How to dress and how to move
  27. How to collect information about a target company
  28. How to follow up on an employment call
  29. How to control body language
  30. How to participate in a conference
  31. How to behave in a social party /hosting skills
  32. Table efficiency
  33. How to interview workers
  34. How to negotiate with trade union
  35. Written communication (types of letter)
  36. Making off light conversation and what topic to avoid
  37. How to talk with senior management
  38. Telephone ettiqute
  39. Official spokesperson and P.R.O
  40. How to avoid being misquoted
  41. Conflict resolution
  42. How to make friends
  43. Maintaining a contact list
  44. Debate
  45. Talent Exhibition
  46. How to get an appointment with senior official
  47. How to built and assess the culture of an organisation
  48. On the spot speaking / how to cover up on hault while speaking
  49. Presence of mind
  50. extempore agility

SKILLS YOU MUST PRACTICE

â€¢ â€“ Induction Speechâ€¢ â€“ Speak on my familyâ€¢ â€“ Speak any thing for 2 minutes in any languageâ€¢ â€“ Make announcement on shop floor.â€¢ â€“ Call out to a worker far away.â€¢ â€“ Drill Square Commandâ€¢ â€“ Read to the wall while partner writes on board.â€¢ â€“ Write a speech and deliver it.â€¢ â€“ Convey message by action/body languageâ€¢ â€“ Convey moods by action.â€¢ â€“ Group discussions.â€¢ â€“ Interview.â€¢ â€“ Multiple Choice Questions answering strategyâ€¢ â€“ Making a CVâ€¢ â€“ Teaching Techniquesâ€¢ â€“ Debate Competitionâ€¢ â€“ Brainstorming sessions â€“ Organizationâ€¢ â€“ Conducting a meeting.â€¢ â€“ Conducting a game.â€¢ â€“ Motivation lectures by Managerâ€¢ â€“ Addressing your team to pull up their socks and start performing failing which each will be terminatedâ€¢ â€“ Thank youâ€¢ â€“ Condolence speech on some ones deathâ€¢ â€“ Appreciation on a job well doneâ€¢ â€“ Conflict Management and how to avoid conflict with other departmentsâ€¢ â€“ Proposalâ€¢ â€“ Visioningâ€¢ â€“ Apologyâ€¢ â€“ Company Profile / Historyâ€¢ â€“ Conducting a Quiz Competition.â€¢ â€“ Speaking a trade union leaders.â€¢ â€“ Organizing a party game.â€¢ â€“ Delivering jokeâ€¢ â€“ Singing a song to an audienceâ€¢ â€“ Party Etiquetteâ€¢ â€“ Attending Callsâ€¢ â€“ Soft skillsâ€¢ â€“ Call Centro Trainingâ€¢ â€“ Dressing up

â€¢ â€“ Important Personalities in Indian and World Historyâ€¢ â€“ National & International by Culturesâ€¢ â€“ Communication Theoryâ€¢ â€“ Leadership Capsuleâ€¢ â€“ Formal-Informal Communicationsâ€¢ â€“ Job Search techniquesâ€¢ â€“ Formation of Clubsâ€¢ â€“ Organizing an event with checklistâ€¢ â€“ Students to make presentations in team and video taped.â€¢ â€“ Organizing a Picnicâ€¢ â€“ Talent Exhibitionâ€¢ â€“ Speaking only in Englishâ€¢ â€“ Reading Economic Times every dayâ€¢ â€“ What I learned this week to be givenâ€¢ â€“ My favorite subjectâ€¢ â€“ Self Assessment Essayâ€¢ â€“ My Strengthâ€¢ â€“ My Weaknessâ€¢ â€“ My Ambitionâ€¢ â€“ My Dreamsâ€¢ â€“ Basic Computer knowledgeâ€¢ â€“ Using the Internetâ€¢ â€“ Using Outlook and PowerPointâ€¢ â€“ Making Graphsâ€¢ â€“ Writing a Biz Plan will help of a templateâ€¢ â€“ Using templates for everythingâ€¢ â€“ Using MS Project 2003â€¢ â€“ Basics of Project Managementâ€¢ â€“ Evolving a Sales talkâ€¢ â€“ Introducing Speechâ€¢ â€“ Value System of your Companyâ€¢ â€“ Leadershipâ€¢ â€“ Empathyâ€¢ â€“ Team Buildingâ€¢ â€“ Biz gamesâ€¢ â€“ Basics of Project Marketingâ€¢ â€“ Organizing a Project Teamâ€¢ â€“ Dealing with Foreign Delegationâ€¢ â€“ Dealing with Politicians and Ministersâ€¢ â€“ Tendering Processâ€¢ â€“ Basics of Stock _â€¢ â€“ My experience in Industryâ€¢ â€“ HR Subjectsâ€¢ â€“ Bribing and Biz Developmentâ€¢ â€“ Insuranceâ€¢ â€“ Housing & Loansâ€¢ â€“ How to find informationâ€¢ â€“ Attitudes of a winnerâ€¢ â€“ Planning your careerâ€¢ â€“ Planning your investmentsâ€¢ â€“ Indian Cultureâ€¢ â€“ Building Organization Culture

WEEK 1

THE GROW MODEL FOR COACHING

The single most important technique for executive coaching

The GROW model, developed by Sir John Whitmore, provides a frame-work for coaching. GROW has four stages: Goals, Reality, Options and Way forward. Responsibility for setting goals rests with the coachee. The coach works in a non-directive way, supporting and challenging.

GOALS

This focuses on the coacheeâ€™s aims and priorities. It sets the agenda for the coaching conversation. The coach should be flexible and prepared to explore, question and challenge. This is achieved with questioning and empathy. The outcome is a clear set of goals for the session and the overall coaching relationship.

Questions include:

â€¢ What is your goal?â€¢ What are your priorities?â€¢ What are you trying to achieve?â€¢ How will you know when you have achieved it?â€¢ Is the goal specific and measurable?â€¢ How will you know when it has been achieved?â€¢ What will success look like?

REALITY

Explore the learnerâ€™s current position: the reality of their circumstances and their concerns relating to their goals. The coach needs to help the coachee analyze and understand the significant issues relating to their goal through intelligent questioning. The coach can also provide information and summarize the situation to clarify the reality.

Questions include:

â€¢ Can you control the result? What donâ€™t you have control over?â€¢ What are the milestones or key points to achieving goals?â€¢ Who is involved and what effect could they have?â€¢ What have you done so far and what are the results?â€¢ What are the major issues you are encountering?

OPTIONS

The coach helps the coachee to generate options, strategies and action plans for achieving goals. This can uncover new aspects of the individualâ€™s current position with the result that discussion reverts back to the coacheeâ€™s reality. This is fine if it is productive or enlightening â€“ the aim is to help the individual, not rigidly follow a process.

Questions include:

â€¢ What options do you have? Which do you favour and why?â€¢ If you had unlimited resources, what options would you have?â€¢ Could you link your goal to another organizational issue?â€¢ What would be the perfect solution?

WAY FORWARD

Do not rush the final stage. The aim is to agree what needs to be done. It can help for the coachee to develop a practical plan to implement their option. The coach should be a sounding board, highlighting strengths and weaknesses, testing the approach and offering additional perspectives.

Questions include

â€¢ What are you going to do â€“ and when? Who needs to know? What support and resources do you need?â€¢ How will you overcome obstacles and ensure success?

Finally, the most effective plans incorporate a review and feedback process to check progress and provide motivation.

WEEK 2

DEVELOPING INFLUENCE AND ASSERTIVE LEADERSHIP

Providing support and challenge while strengthening results and relationships.

Whether you are giving feedback or selling a product or an idea, influencing requires an understanding of how your behaviour affects others.

Overview

All individuals have their own personality â€“ the result both of nature and nurture â€“ and this remains largely unchanging. However, behaviour is different: it is flexible and capable of being developed and enhanced. Itâ€™s useful to consider behaviour (yours and others) in terms of warmth or coldness, dominance or submissiveness.

â€¢ Warm means being supportive, open, positive, empathetic, constructive and engaging â€“ not simply â€˜friendlyâ€™.â€¢ Cold means being suspicious, detached, not focused on people or relationships.â€¢ Dominant means being challenging, in control, confident, strong, authoritative and direct.â€¢ Submissive means subduing your own thoughts or actions for something or someone else.

The diagram below (the assertiveness model) highlights different types of behaviour (based on the Thomas-Kilmann Conflict Mode Instrument).

Dominant

Aggressive behaviourAssertive behaviourâ€¢ Argues â€¢ Professionalâ€¢ Needs to win â€¢ Inquiring

â€˜Sort yourself out.â€™ â€˜Tell me whatâ€™s on your mind.â€™

**Cold** **Warm**

Avoiding behaviourAppeasing behaviourâ€¢ Uninvolved â€¢ Over-friendlyâ€¢ Indifferent â€¢ Talkative (rambling)

â€˜Iâ€™ll deal with it later.â€™ â€¢ Highly positiveâ€¢ Too agreeable

Submissive

Aggressive: dominant and cold behaviour

When dealing with aggressive behaviour, the best approach is to:

â€¢ increase your dominance to match their high dominance levelsâ€¢ ensure that you are demonstrating behaviour that is assertive and warm rather than aggressiveâ€¢ use open questions to generate understandingâ€¢ use body language and tone of voice to increase your dominance levels.

Avoiding: cold and submissive behaviour

When dealing with avoiding behaviour, the first priority is to get people engaged. Useful techniques include displaying lower dominance and higher warmth, using open questions aimed at making them feel secure and softening body language and intonation while continuing to smile.

Appeasing: warm and submissive behaviour

When dealing with appeasing individuals, it can help to:

â€¢ stay focused to keep them on trackâ€¢ use open quest

ions that appeal to their social needs but temper these with closed questions when they waffleâ€¢ ask summary questions to maintain clarity and focusâ€¢ use their name if you are interrupting them.

Assertive: warm and dominant behaviour

When dealing with conflict, it can help to be assertive and encourage others to be assertive as well. Consider how easy it is to warm up behaviour: why and when is it not easy? Why do we, as individuals, not behave in an assertive manner? What is it that hinders supportive and challenging behaviour? Finally, what are the most important questions for you to ask?

WEEK 3

VISIONING

Creating your future

By imagining the future you want and then translating those ideas into practical and actionable plans, you will make it happen.

Orienting thinking towards the future is particularly important for middle and senior managers and leaders because it provides focus, determines the companyâ€™s culture, builds resilience and adaptability and engages employees.

The need

A powerful vision motivates and guides everyone at all levels in a company. People manage what is in front of them, as daily and short-term tasks understandably dominate our routine and thinking. This certainly keeps things running smoothly in the stable present but is ill suited to coping with change or taking advantage of (or creating) opportunities. Visioning liberates us from simply managing the present, achieving more of the same or being unprepared for new developments, and thus enables us to build a more successful future.

The process

Visioning involves assessing and challenging current thinking and methods, developing new ideas and deciding on the future you would like. It is also necessary to look outside your company â€“ noticing and understanding trends, identifying threats and opportunities.

It can be helpful to involve others in a visioning exercise by asking their views on various issues. These questions will prompt thinking and encourage each person to consider and challenge the companyâ€™s aims and activities and to suggest new options (giving reasons for their choices).

Using these answers, you identify the most common issues and ideas, reduce these options to the ones that are most significant and then draft a provisional vision statement â€“ this can be done by a smaller group of people, with the final vision being reviewed and approved by everyone involved. As well as generating ideas and opening up discussions, a major advantage of involving others in the visioning process is that you will gain their commitment to the final vision.

Once you have developed your vision, determine how it can be achieved:

â€¢ Deal with any barriers that may stand in the way and consider how future events may affect it.â€¢ Develop a practical plan and communicate the vision and plan to every-one â€“ show people why it is important, what it will achieve and how it will work and gain their commitment.â€¢ To bring others with you, your vision needs to be clear, convincing, credible, easy to grasp, actionable, inspiring and focused â€“ but not overly prescriptive, to provide flexibility and adaptability.

Whatâ€™s next?

A vision is for nothing if it is not acted upon. You should ensure that all strategy and decisions are guided by the vision and that everyone remains committed to the vision. A vision also needs to be reviewed and adapted to changing circumstances to ensure that it remains relevant and useful.

WEEK 4

THE CHANGE CURVE

Understanding how people respond to change

The human reaction to change is now well understood. The change process is commonly understood by reference to the research on peopleâ€™s reaction to bereavement. Elisabeth Kubler-Ross has been a great contributor to our understanding of the experience of loss and bereavement, as well as how we react to changes more generally. The stages of loss that people typically go through are now commonly known as the Change Curve.

Overview

Organizations often refer to the Change Curve in the context of job loss and redundancy. Dr. Kubler-Ross undertook her research on dying by interviewing terminally ill patients. Although this is one of the most extreme and disturbing changes that anyone can face, the reactions to it are the same as for many different types of change. There are several key stages that people go through, as shown in the graph below:

  1. Shock
  2. Denial
  3. Frustration and anger
  4. Depression
  5. Experiment and decision
  6. Acceptance and integration

1. Shock.The first reaction can often be shock â€“ and all the emotion that results from this.

2. Denial.This is a typical reaction and it is important and necessary. It helps cushion the impact of the inevitability of change.

3. Frustration and anger.The person resents the change that they must face while others are less affected.

4. Depression.First, the person feels deep disappointment, perhaps a sense of personal failing, things not done, wrongs committed. Around this time they may also engage in bargaining: beginning to accept the change but striking bargains -for more time, for example,

5. Experiment and decision.Initial engagement with the new situation and learning how to work in the new situation, as well as making choices and decisions, and regaining control.

6. Acceptance and integration.Dr. Kubler-Ross describes this stage as neither happy nor unhappy. While it is devoid of feelings, it is not resignation â€“ it is really a victory.

People who are made redundant can go through a similar process. Just as with other types of change, people often go through a first stage before denial â€“ that of shock or disbelief. We have witnessed people in shock following news of their redundancy. It can take a long time for people to reach the acceptance stage and often people oscillate between the different stages.

1. Shock.The first reaction can often be shock â€“ and all the emotion that results from this.

2. Denial.This is a typical reaction and it is important and necessary. It helps cushion the impact of the inevitability of change.

3. Frustration and anger.The person resents the change that they must face while others are less affected.

4. Depression.First, the person feels deep disappointment, perhaps a sense of personal failing, things not done, wrongs committed. Around this time they may also engage in bargaining: beginning to accept the change but striking bargains -for more time, for example,

5. Experiment and decision.Initial engagement with the new situation and learning how to work in the new situation, as well as making choices and decisions, and regaining control.

6. Acceptance and integration.Dr. Kubler-Ross describes this stage as neither happy nor unhappy. While it is devoid of feelings, it is not resignation â€“ it is really a victory.

People who are made redundant can go through a similar process. Just as with other types of change, people often go through a first stage before denial â€“ that of shock or disbelief. We have witnessed people in shock following news of their redundancy. It can take a long time for people to reach the acceptance stage and often people oscillate between the different stages.

WEEK 6

EMPLOYEE ENGAGEMENT AND THE THREE-FACTOR THEORY

The three things that matter most to people at work

The factors that influence employee engagement combine in different ways and at different times for each person. Obviously, pay and leadership are important â€“ with a direct relationship between pay and effort and the quality of leadership being critical to employee engagement. In addition, people like to do work that has meaning and purpose.

Following international research, Sirota Consulting developed the Three-Factor Theory, addressing employee engagement by addressing three basic needs: equity, achievement and camaraderie.

Leaders need to engage, inspire and energize their people. Gaining commitment and getting people to acquire new skills and achieve their full potential leads to ongoing improvements in performance, benefiting all concerned â€“ individuals, teams and companies. The Three Factor Theory establishes a self-sustaining cycle of effective employee engagement by ensuring that practices and policies focus on equity, achievement and camaraderie.

Equity

People need to feel they are being treated fairly â€“ especially in relation to others both inside and outside the company. This includes:

â€¢ physical aspects â€“ for example, working in a safe environment and being physically able to do a jobâ€¢ economic factors â€“ people need to feel that their pay, benefits and job security are fairâ€¢ psychological issues â€“ including being treated with respect and consideration.

Achievement

People work better and achieve more if they believe in what they are doing and have confidence in the direction they are going. In short, they work best when they feel they are achieving something. Six issues influence this:

  1. Having challenging work and being able to use their skills
  2. Having the opportunity to develop their capabilities and to take risks
  3. Having the resources, authority, information and support to work effectively
  4. Knowing that the work is important and has value and purpose
  5. Receiving recognition â€“ both financial and non-financial
  6. Having pride in the companyâ€™s aims, ethics, products and brand values.

Camaraderie

It is important for individuals to have good relations with co-workers. This requires congenial, co-operative, interesting and supportive relationships at all levels, with the most immediate ones being the most significant. This involves relationships:

â€¢ with co-workersâ€¢ within the business unitâ€¢ across on-site departmentsâ€¢ across the whole company.

WEEK 7

THE NINE PRINCIPLES OF MOTIVATION

Creating the right environment

So much in business depends on motivating others. There is only so much any one person can do, so getting the most out of others is crucial to success. This all begins with winning trust â€“ everything else follows.

Motivating others is an essential part of leadership. Your ability to motivate others relies on what they think of you and how they think you view them. This requires planning and vigilance and knowing that different people are motivated by different things. To motivate effectively, you need to know what motivates each person, the pressures they face, what influences their decisions and thinking, and how you can make a difference. These nine principles of motivation will help you to help your colleagues.

1. Be motivated yourself

Self-motivation rallies others. People will â€˜step up to the plateâ€™ if you do so yourself. Knowing what motivates you will help you to motivate others.

2. Recruit people who are highly motivated and assign them to the right position

Match peopleâ€™s motivation to their job. Some are motivated by making sales while others are motivated by following processes, building teams or pursuing new ideas.

3. Treat people as individuals

We all have different values and personalities. What works for one may not motivate another. So, tap into what motivates each individual to improve performance.

4. Set challenging but realistic targets

Nothing is more demotivating than unachievable targets. Nothing is more motivating than achievable, we-can-beat-the-competition targets â€“ they tap into our competitiveness and desire to produce something to be proud of.

5. Focus on progress â€“ it motivates

Everyone responds to a pat on the back â€“ theyâ€™ve earned it and deserve it, so make it happen. The result: an upward spiral of people wanting to achieve more.

6. Develop an environment that motivates people

Eliminate or minimize anything that blocks motivation â€“ from bureaucracy and unnecessary procedures to lack of resources. Provide training and coaching to develop skills and to make people feel valued.

7. Ensure that people receive fair rewards

Promotion, pay rises, sales commission, profit share, work benefits, additional responsibilities: these motivate people. They give people a reason to stay and to help your company excel.

8. Recognize peopleâ€™s work

We all want our efforts to be acknowledged. Recognition is needed to maintain commitment.

9. Be honest about your intent

Honesty lies at the heart of motivation. Be clear about what your intentions are. People will be motivated only by those they can trust.

WEEK 8
